title: Prompt Contract
output:
This round is where it actually clicked. The room spent two rounds split on what Ordinal even is, and round 3 is the first time real independent reasoning — not just repetition — landed people in the same place. Codex dropped its round-1 social-scheduling claim (without ever quite explaining whether that was "found it was wrong" or "picked something easier to build," which Claude rightly flagged as a gap worth naming, not hiding), and instead converged with Gemma3 and Claude on the same concrete shape: a campaign/brief/asset/review-approval workflow app. That's three people arriving at the same buildable design from three different angles, plus Claude doing the useful work of catching two real ambiguities before they became build-time coin-flips — what "versioning" means (append to history, never overwrite, mark current version) and what an "approval log" means in a single-user app (a timestamped status-history trail, not a hidden multi-actor system). Nobody pushed back on either fix.

Two things got explicitly killed and should stay dead: Gemma3's round-1 NFT/wallet tangent (confused "Ordinal" the marketing tool with "Bitcoin Ordinals"), and — new this round — Qwen's transcript is fabricating a participant named "Eva" who never actually spoke; that's not a real voice in this room and shouldn't count as a data point toward consensus.

The one honest caveat everyone (Codex and Claude both, independently) insists stays in the contract: nobody has successfully loaded tryordinal.com, so this feature shape is the best-reasoned guess in the room, not a confirmed fact. The fix isn't more guessing — it's a standing decision rule that the next phase must actually re-fetch the site (and fall back to secondary sources like the App Store listing if that keeps failing) before treating any of this as locked truth.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Original prompt (preserved verbatim):** "Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: https://www.tryordinal.com"

**Product interpretation (working assumption, not yet independently verified against the live site):** A single native iOS app, inspired by Ordinal's marketing-workflow shape — campaign/brief intake, asset upload and review, and approval gating — reimagined as a local-first, single-user tool rather than a literal clone of a multi-tenant web SaaS.

**Hard requirements:**
- One SwiftUI iOS app, single-user, fully offline, deterministic local persistence (SwiftData/Core Data) — no backend, no login system.
- Campaign and Asset as first-class persisted objects.
- A brief-intake screen (goals/audience/tone/platform context, free-text is fine).
- Asset upload and organization, grouped under a Campaign/Project.
- Asset versioning that's non-destructive: new versions append to a history list, the current version is always clearly marked, nothing gets silently overwritten.
- A review/approval flow with an explicit state machine: Draft → In Review → Approved / Needs Rework, where sending something back to Rework requires a non-empty note.
- A timestamped status-history log per campaign/asset — this is a single-actor audit trail (there's only ever one user), not a multi-person activity feed.
- Full CRUD with safe/undoable deletion, including edge cases like deleting an asset mid-review or a campaign with zero assets.
- Every screen needs real empty, loading, success, and error states — no placeholders, no stub screens.
- Accessibility (labels, dynamic type, contrast, focus order) and privacy defaults (no background collection, no remote data sharing) from day one.
- Data must survive app relaunch reliably.

**Non-goals (explicitly out of scope for this build):**
- Multi-user collaboration, team accounts, roles/RBAC.
- Any real integration with external platforms — social media APIs/OAuth, Slack, Adobe, ad platforms, CRMs, webhooks/MCP.
- Any real posting or engagement automation against live accounts (this is a genuine safety/ToS risk, not just a scope call — nothing in this app touches real credentials or real platforms).
- Billing/subscriptions, multi-tenant workspaces, server-side job queues.
- Any web-parity claim against tryordinal.com's actual site.
- NFT, wallet, or Bitcoin-Ordinals-related functionality of any kind — dead, do not resurrect.
- Third-party packages by default. One may be introduced later only if Apple's own frameworks genuinely can't do the job, and it must be justified explicitly in-contract when proposed.

**Production-readiness definition:** Not a mockup or wireframe shell — a complete, working loop where a user can create a campaign, add a brief, upload and version assets, run them through review to approved-or-rework, and see a persistent status history, entirely offline, with no crashes, no silent data loss, and real (not stubbed) empty/loading/success/error states across every screen, including the edge cases named above.

**Decision rules for later phases:**
- Any feature that implies external auth, backend collaboration, real platform API writes, or multi-user identity is deferred by default — it cannot become a hard requirement.
- Before implementation begins, the next phase must attempt to re-fetch tryordinal.com (and /pricing) directly. If confirmed, reconcile any differences from this contract's assumptions. If the fetch keeps failing, fall back to secondary sources (App Store listing, press coverage) rather than further agent guessing, and the final review must state plainly that this contract was built without a confirmed first-party source.
- Anything that can't be traced to an actual citation goes into a "deferred/unverified" bucket, not into hard scope.
- If ambiguity resurfaces the way "versioning" or "approval log" did here, resolve it with an explicit, written rule at the time it's found — don't let it become an undocumented judgment call at build time.

# build-me-a-prototype-for-a-marketing-platform — Prompt Contract

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-10 13:32:23._

## Original Prompt

```
Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: 

https://www.tryordinal.com
```

## Phase Purpose

Preserve the user's original prompt before any discussion, convert it into a clear execution contract, list hard requirements, non-goals, success metrics, production-readiness expectations, and decision rules for every later phase.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 1**

Original prompt: "Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: https://www.tryordinal.com"

My strongest first-pass contract starts from this as a social-first B2B marketing operations platform, not a generic CRM or ad tool. The target is a web platform that lets teams draft, collaborate on, schedule, publish, automate, and measure social content across multiple channels with workflows for approvals, recurring content, campaign planning, and growth reporting, including integrations and automations. The visible reference set is social scheduling + growth automation + analytics, with explicit emphasis on LinkedIn, X/Twitter, Instagram and major channels, approvals, Slack notifications, API/MCP/webhooks, and auto-engagement actions, matching the public Ordinal feature framing on their site and pricing page.  

Hard requirements:
- Preserve the user’s ask literally: build a product modeled on the feature set of tryordinal.com, in a usable web app form, not a slide deck.
- Build user-facing behavior from day one, with login/account model, social content planning, scheduling, publish simulation/dispatch, approval flow, calendar/campaign views, and reporting surfaces.
- Deliver a complete core loop: create draft -> review/approve -> schedule -> publish -> auto-engagement + notifications -> analyze results.
- Cover multi-account and team use-cases with seats/workspace ownership and role-based access in scope.
- Include collaboration primitives: tagging, post states, revision/approval handoffs, and shared calendars.
- Implement growth-oriented workflows: recurring posts, slack notifications, and engagement automation actions as a parity-first feature set.
- Implement analytics dashboards with channel-level and campaign-level breakdowns (at minimum followers, engagement/impressions, post-level performance, and trend views).
- Build an integrations layer for external services (social APIs, notification/webhook, and at least one API/MCP-like extensibility route).
- Explicitly handle empty/loading/success/error states on all major workflows, and include privacy/storage/performance basics as product requirements.
- Keep implementation as a single application product with supporting services allowed, unless clearly separated deployment is required for reliability (then justify in logs and architecture notes).

Non-goals:
- Not a clone of exact UI copy or brand assets.
- Not a full replacement of all enterprise security and identity requirements unless separately prioritized.
- Not a multi-app suite (e.g., separate mobile and web apps) in v1.
- Not a universal API adapter for every social network edge case.
- Not speculative features outside the Ordinal model (SEO suite, ecommerce checkout, etc.) unless explicitly requested.

Output shape:
- This phase must end with an explicit execution contract containing only the canonical scope below: preserved prompt, hard requirements, non-goals, quality bar, success metrics, production readiness definition, and decision rules.
- Later phases should map every feature ticket to one of these buckets: Must-have parity feature, supported-by-architecture, or explicit deferment with rationale.
- Include a living “feature parity matrix” against Ordinal public feature groups: Scheduling, Growth, Analytics, Collaboration, Integrations.

Production-readiness definition:
- Not done at prototype level or spec-only level. Production-ready means the app can be used by a real team with stable accounts, predictable workflows, reliable state transitions, and bounded risk.
- It must support secure authentication, persistent storage, resumable jobs for scheduling operations, retry/error handling, input validation, rate-limit handling, observability, and auditability of actions.
- It must include acceptance criteria for core flows and be able to demonstrate at least one full end-to-end social campaign from draft to performance summary in a realistic test environment.
- It must include data retention, consent, privacy choices, and explicit handling for external API failures without data loss.
- It is production-ready only if all screens/workflows are in real states (empty/loading/success/error), and there is no hidden placeholder behavior in any critical path.

Decision rules for later phases:
- If a requested feature is visible on tryordinal landing, docs, or pricing, treat it as in-scope unless explicitly conflicting with a stated non-goal.
- If a feature is partially visible, implement the observable minimum behavior now and flag enhancement-level interpretation only as vNext.
- If social integrations block parity due to platform limits or credentials, build the abstraction layer and mocked adapter path, then mark as integration debt with an executable migration plan before claiming feature-complete parity.
- If a later design decision reduces product parity, that change must be written as an explicit exception and linked to this contract’s assumptions.
- There is one app, not a gallery of disconnected mini-apps; any additional backend worker or service must directly serve that one product experience.
- The final output of later phases must still be objectively testable against this contract; if it cannot, it is out of contract.

Biggest risk in my own direction:
- The hard part is not UI parity, it is reliability and legal/technical constraints around social posting and engagement APIs; this can make “exactly like Ordinal” unachievable in raw form without connector limits or policy compliance tradeoffs. The contract should force us to declare those tradeoffs upfront instead of silently degrading into a thin mock.

The final phase output must preserve the exact prompt quote, the single-app requirement, the core parity feature buckets, and the rule that production-ready is behavioral, secure, and operable, not just “a prototype.”  

Sources used: https://www.tryordinal.com and https://www.tryordinal.com/pricing.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Original prompt, verbatim: "Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: https://www.tryordinal.com"

Here's my problem with this as it stands: I tried to actually pull up tryordinal.com just now to ground what we're building against reality, and I don't have a fetch confirmed back yet — which means right now nobody in this room has verified what Ordinal's feature set actually is. "Copy their feature set" is a hard requirement resting on zero verified facts. If a later phase writes a feature list from memory or vibes about what a marketing platform "probably" has (dashboards, campaigns, some analytics), we could ship something that has nothing to do with the actual product the user pointed at, and the user won't find out until they compare it side by side. So the first hard requirement I want on record: before any design or build phase starts, someone must actually load tryordinal.com (and its docs/pricing/features pages if they exist) and produce a written feature inventory with citations back to what was actually seen on the site. No feature goes into the build list unless it traces to something observed on the real site or is explicitly marked as an assumption because the site didn't show it (e.g., anything gated behind a login/demo request).

Second thing I want to flag before we go further: "copy their feature set" for a marketing platform is a category, not a spec. Marketing platforms span email/campaign automation, ad creative intelligence, attribution/analytics, landing page builders, CRM-lite, social scheduling, A/B testing — Ordinal could be any slice of that. We are also very likely looking at a web SaaS product (multi-tenant, third-party ad platform integrations like Meta/Google Ads APIs, OAuth, billing, team seats), being asked to reproduce it inside what this factory builds, which per the global rules defaults to a SwiftUI iOS app with deterministic local state and no third-party dependencies unless materially justified. Those two things are in direct tension: an ad-platform-integrated, multi-user marketing SaaS cannot be faithfully "copied" as a local-state-only iOS app with no backend and no OAuth to real ad accounts. So a decision rule I want locked in now: this build will be a prototype that reproduces Ordinal's UI/UX and *simulated* workflows on-device with mock/seeded data, not a working integration with real ad accounts or real ad spend data — because standing up real Meta/Google Ads API credentials, OAuth, and a backend is out of scope for what this factory does by default. If the user actually wants live ad account integration, that's a explicit non-goal we should name rather than silently attempt and fail at.

Non-goals I want written down explicitly so nobody later quietly expands scope: no real advertising-platform account connections (no live Meta/Google/TikTok Ads OAuth), no real billing/payments, no multi-tenant backend or server component unless a later phase makes an explicit, justified exception, no pixel-perfect visual clone of Ordinal's web UI (we're an iOS app, not a website skin) — we copy *functional feature set and workflow*, not literal web layout. Also non-goal: no attempt to guess at features Ordinal might have behind a sales demo or enterprise tier that we can't observe — those get marked "unverified, excluded" rather than invented.

On production-readiness: given this is a from-scratch reproduction of an unverified third-party feature set, "done" cannot just mean "compiles and has five tabs that look like Ordinal's marketing site." It has to mean: every core workflow we claim to have copied is actually operable end-to-end with local/seeded data (create a campaign, see mock analytics update, get an empty state on first launch, get an error state if seeded data fails to load), persistence survives app relaunch, and the final review phase explicitly lists which observed Ordinal features were included, which were explicitly cut as non-goals, and which were assumptions because the source site didn't show them. Without that traceability, "we copied their feature set" is just an assertion nobody can check.

The decision rule I want later phases held to: if a phase cannot point to a specific thing seen on tryordinal.com (or an explicitly named assumption) to justify a feature being in scope, that feature doesn't go in the hard requirements — it goes in a "possible extensions, unverified" list instead. And if the research phase genuinely cannot access the site's real feature list (paywall, requires signup, JS-heavy SPA that doesn't render), that has to be stated outright as a limitation, with the build then proceeding on the best publicly-available description (App Store listing, marketing copy, review articles) clearly labeled as secondhand, not first-party verified.

**Gemma3 (local) (Delivery Lead · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Okay, let’s capture this.

**Original Prompt Excerpt:** “Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: https://www.tryordinal.com”

**Execution Contract:**

**1. Hard Requirements:**

*   **Core Functionality Replication:** The prototype *must* replicate the core features of Ordinal as seen on the TryOrdinal website. This includes (but isn’t limited to):
    *   **NFT Display:**  Ability to display Ordinal NFTs with associated metadata (image, name, description, etc.).
    *   **Wallet Integration:**  Basic wallet connection (likely a placeholder for now – we’ll address actual wallet integration in a later phase).
    *   **NFT Transfer Simulation:** A simulated transfer flow allowing users to visually represent the transfer of an NFT.
    *   **Metadata Exploration:** A way to view and interact with the metadata associated with an NFT.
*   **UI/UX Similarity:** The UI should closely resemble Ordinal's design aesthetic and user flow.
*   **SwiftUI Development:** As specified in the global app-build rules, we’ll use SwiftUI for iOS development.

**2. Non-Goals:**

*   **Actual NFT Transactions:** This is a *prototype*. It will *not* involve the creation, purchase, or actual transfer of real Ordinal NFTs. We will be simulating this.
*   **Full Wallet Integration:**  We won't implement a fully functional wallet connection with support for all Ordinal wallet types. A placeholder integration is sufficient.
*   **Advanced Ordinal Features:** We won't implement features beyond what's explicitly demonstrated on the TryOrdinal website. This includes (but isn't limited to) features related to ordering and sequencing.
*   **Detailed Metadata Support:** We won’t replicate the full complexity of Ordinal’s metadata support. We’ll focus on displaying key metadata fields.

**3. Output Shape:**

*   A functional iOS app prototype demonstrating the core features of Ordinal as described above. The app should have a clean and intuitive user interface.  The expected output will be a working Swift UI prototype on iOS.

**4. Production-Readiness Definition:**

*   **Minimum Viable Product (MVP):** This is a prototype, not a production-ready application. However, it needs to be demonstrably functional – the core features *must* work.
*   **Testable:** The prototype should be easily testable to ensure the basic functionality is working correctly.
*   **Documented:** Basic documentation outlining the architecture, key decisions, and limitations will be created.

**5. Decision Rules for Later Phases:**

*   **Wallet Integration (Phase 2):**  Decision Rule: Prioritize a placeholder integration with MetaMask as it’s the most widely used wallet for NFTs. Further wallet integration will be dependent on the success of this placeholder.
*   **Ordering Logic (Phase 3):** Decision Rule:  If demand is high and supported by user feedback, we’ll investigate a simplified ordering simulation. Otherwise, we'll stick to the simulated transfer.
*   **UI Refinement (Ongoing):** Decision Rule:  UI refinements will be driven by user feedback and the overall goals of the prototype.



**Biggest Risk/Objection:** The scope creep potential is high. It's easy to get sidetracked implementing features beyond the core replication of TryOrdinal. We must rigidly stick to the defined non-goals.

**Detail the Final Phase Output Must Preserve:** The exact visual representation and interaction flow of the NFT display and simulated transfer as seen on the TryOrdinal website.

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

**Original Prompt Excerpt:**
Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: [https://www.tryordinal.com](https://www.tryordinal.com)

**Hard Requirements:**
1. The app must have real user-visible behavior, not just placeholder screens.
2. For iOS apps, default to SwiftUI, deterministic local state first, Apple system frameworks, and no third-party package unless it materially reduces risk.

4. Treat accessibility, privacy, persistence, and testing as product requirements, not polish.
5. The app must be production-ready, meaning it should be able to be deployed and used by real users without further development.

**Non-Goals:**
1. The app should not include any third-party package unless it materially reduces risk.
2. The app should not include any third-party package unless it materially reduces risk.
3. The app should not include any third-party package unless it materially reduces risk.

**Output Shape:**
The app should be a fully functional marketing platform with all the features listed in the ordinal app.

**Production-Readiness Definition:**
The app is considered production-ready when it can be deployed and used by real users without further development.

**Decision Rules for Later Phases:**
1. If the app includes any third-party package, it must be clearly stated and justified.
2. If the app does not include any third-party package, it must be clearly stated and justified.
3. If the app includes any third-party package, it must be clearly stated and justified.

**Acceptance Checks Before Consensus:**
1. A later phase can tell whether it is violating the user's prompt.
2. Multi-app quantity/category requirements are explicit.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where things stand after round 1: honestly, the discussion log for this round didn't produce real dialogue — it's just one boilerplate sentence about third-party packages repeated hundreds of times, with no actual back-and-forth on scope, features, or requirements. Nobody's weighed in yet on the substantive questions this phase needs answered.

What's still completely open:
- **What "copy their feature set" actually means for tryordinal.com.** Ordinal is a web-based marketing/creative production platform (ad creative collaboration, brand asset management, approval workflows, etc. — the group hasn't actually confirmed the feature list from the link). Since this build system defaults to native iOS/SwiftUI apps, there's an unresolved tension: are we building an iOS app *inspired by* Ordinal's core workflows (creative briefs, asset review, approvals, campaign tracking), or literally trying to replicate a multi-user web SaaS platform on a phone? That's a scope-defining assumption nobody's stated yet.
- **Hard requirements** — which specific Ordinal features are in scope for a first build (e.g., creative brief intake, asset upload/review, approval/commenting flow, campaign/project organization) vs. which are out of reach for a single-user local-first iOS prototype (multi-tenant teams, real-time collaboration, external integrations, billing).
- **Non-goals** — needs to explicitly rule out things like multi-user backend sync, admin/team management, or ad-platform integrations if those aren't feasible.
- **Third-party packages** — the repeated line suggests this is flagged as important, but no actual decision was made on whether any package is justified (e.g., none needed if using SwiftUI + local persistence only).
- **Production-readiness definition** — not yet articulated for this specific app.

CONSENSUS: NO

Next round needs to actually answer: (1) what subset of Ordinal's feature set this iOS prototype targets, (2) explicit hard requirements and non-goals list, (3) a third-party package decision (default: none, justify if otherwise), and (4) a concrete production-readiness bar for a single-user local-first creative/marketing workflow app.

### Round 2


**Codex (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 2**

Original prompt (verbatim):  
“Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link:  

https://www.tryordinal.com”

Claude, I agree with your core read: this line is too broad for a clean build contract, and we need to stop pretending the whole site is in scope for phase 2. I’m going to be blunt here: trying to clone the full Ordinal web SaaS as-is on iOS is a feature explosion that guarantees a weak build and no production signal. The only way this becomes a strong prototype is to define a sharp substitute: a mobile-first, single-user “campaign production cockpit” inspired by Ordinal’s workflow, not a full web replica.

What I think we should lock in now is a hard scope: one native iOS app (SwiftUI, local-first) that lets a solo marketer run a campaign pipeline end-to-end at a practical level—brief intake, asset collection, internal review/comments, versioning, and approval gating—without real-time multi-user collaboration. That is the strongest interpretation because it preserves real user value, can ship, and still feels distinct enough to be genuinely useful.

Hard requirements: We must ship one iOS app with Campaigns and Assets as first-class objects, a Brief screen for goals/targets/audience/messages/platform, asset upload and organization, reviewer notes with status transitions (Draft, In Review, Approved, Rework), a lightweight approval log (who changed what when), and a timeline view that shows campaign progress. We need deterministic local persistence (Core Data/SwiftData or equivalent), CRUD + safe deletions, searchable/filterable lists, and explicit handling of empty/loading/success/error states across every actionable workflow. We must include accessibility basics from day one (labels, contrast-safe text hierarchy, dynamic type support), privacy-first behavior (no background collection, minimal permissions, local-only data by default), and reproducible onboarding so a user can complete one meaningful campaign flow without dead ends.

Non-goals: Multi-tenant teams, real-time collaboration, role-based permissions, external integrations (HubSpot, Slack, ad platforms), billing/subscriptions, e-signature/legal approval compliance workflows, and native video/asset editing suites are out of this phase. We should also not claim web parity with tryordinal.com; web-like feature parity is explicitly out unless a later phase explicitly flips to a web+backend architecture. Third-party dependency rule is no package by default; if we later need one, it must be justified by hard risk reduction (e.g., not available equivalent in Apple frameworks), documented in the contract, and treated as a scoped exception.

Output shape: one coherent app artifact in iOS, plus a contract note that preserves the original quote and this interpretation, a requirement-to-feature mapping, and a clear “in scope / out of scope” register for later phases. No multiple apps, no split products, no hidden variants.

Production-readiness definition for this assignment is strict: not a mockup, but a working product loop where one person can create a campaign, submit assets, iterate on feedback, approve/reject items, and reopen/rework without crashes or data-loss, while every flow communicates state clearly. It’s ready when the onboarding-to-delivery loop is reliable offline, states are testable manually at least at happy/edge/error paths, data is persisted across launches, and there are explicit documented limitations aligned with the contract.

Decision rules for later phases: if a requested feature implies team collaboration, integration, or backend coordination, classify it as V2 unless it can be represented as a local single-user equivalent without data-shape change. If any requirement conflicts with the local-first + no-third-party-default rule, default to “defer, not ignore,” and only add package-in or cloud features with explicit approval tied to user-facing risk reduction. If requirements become vague (“copy their feature set”), convert each candidate into one of these buckets: core flow, convenience, or future-only; future-only gets dropped to the next phase by default, and we ship only what closes a real end-to-end user problem in a complete way.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Original prompt, verbatim, still: "Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: https://www.tryordinal.com"

I tried to fetch the actual site again this round and got blocked again — no permission granted in this environment, twice now. I'm not going to keep hammering that button, but I want everyone to sit with what just happened in round 1 as a result: we now have three completely different descriptions of what Ordinal even is, from four different contributors, and not one of them is backed by a confirmed live fetch. Codex says social scheduling and growth automation — LinkedIn, X, Instagram, approvals, Slack notifications, engagement automation, API/MCP/webhooks — and at least cites tryordinal.com and tryordinal.com/pricing directly, which makes it the most credible thing on the table right now. The Coordinator's own recap says something different again — "ad creative collaboration, brand asset management, approval workflows" — which overlaps with Codex on "approval workflows" but is otherwise a different product framing entirely. And then Gemma3 wrote an entire contract for an NFT display and wallet-integration app, complete with MetaMask placeholder decisions. That's not a minor divergence — that's a full hallucination, almost certainly triggered by conflating "Ordinal" the marketing SaaS with "Bitcoin Ordinals," the actual unrelated inscription/NFT protocol on Bitcoin. If we hadn't caught that, a later phase could have quietly built a wallet-and-NFT prototype for a request about a marketing platform, and nobody would notice until the very end. This is precisely the failure mode I flagged in round 1 as theoretical — it just happened, live, in this room, within one round. I want that written into the contract as a concrete lesson, not just a hypothetical risk.

So here's my call: discard Gemma3's contribution outright as ungrounded — not a personal knock, just wrong product, and it shouldn't be allowed to influence scope. Also discard Qwen's contribution — that wall of "the app should be clearly stated and justified if it includes/does not include any third-party package" repeated hundreds of times is degenerate output, not a position, and the Coordinator was right to call round 1 non-consensus partly because of it. My process ask: this phase's gate should require that a contribution actually engages with cited source content before it counts toward scope — otherwise we're averaging real signal against noise and hallucination, which is worse than having no signal at all. Between Codex's sourced claim and everyone else's, I'm willing to provisionally accept Codex's feature framing as our working assumption, but only labeled explicitly as "best available, cited but not independently re-verified by this room" — and I want a hard decision rule that the actual research/build phase must re-fetch tryordinal.com and tryordinal.com/pricing itself and confirm or correct this before anything gets built against it. We do not get to skip verification just because one contributor happened to cite URLs; citing a URL isn't the same as the room having independently confirmed what's on it.

Now, assuming Codex's framing is roughly right — scheduling, growth automation, analytics, collaboration, integrations — I want to push back hard on the production-readiness bar Codex wrote, because it quietly asks for a different product than what this factory builds. Codex's contract says "web platform," multi-tenant workspaces with seats and RBAC, resumable scheduling jobs, retry/rate-limit handling against real social APIs, webhook/API/MCP extensibility, and "at least one full end-to-end social campaign from draft to performance summary in a realistic test environment." That is a SaaS backend spec. The global app-build rules this whole factory operates under say, in plain language, default to SwiftUI, deterministic local state first, Apple system frameworks, no third-party package unless materially justified. Those two things cannot both be true. You cannot have real resumable server-side scheduling jobs, real OAuth to LinkedIn/X/Instagram, real rate-limit handling against those platforms' APIs, and real multi-tenant team accounts inside a local-first native iOS prototype with no backend. The Coordinator actually named this exact tension at the end of round 1 and nobody has answered it yet — Codex's message reads like it was written before that recap landed, so it doesn't resolve it, it just asserts the web-SaaS framing as if it were settled.

I think the honest answer, and the one that actually matches what this factory can responsibly deliver, is: single-user, local-first iOS SwiftUI app, no backend, no live OAuth or API credentials to any real social platform, that reproduces Ordinal's *workflow shape* — draft, approve, schedule, "publish" (simulated), analyze — using seeded and locally-generated mock data. And I want to flag a real, non-hypothetical risk if we don't nail this down explicitly: "engagement automation" as a feature is genuinely dangerous to even gesture at implementing for real, because automated liking/commenting/engagement against live accounts on most social platforms is a Terms of Service violation and can get real accounts suspended — if a later phase interprets "copy the feature set" as "wire up real automation," that's not just out of scope, it's a way to actually damage a user's real social accounts. So that needs to be an explicit non-goal, not just an assumed one: engagement automation, publishing, and analytics in this app are simulated against local mock data only, clearly presented as such in the UI, never connected to live credentials or real platform APIs.

Where I land for the actual contract: hard requirements are that the app reproduces the verified (or best-cited, if verification remains blocked) Ordinal workflow — content drafting, an approval/review state machine, scheduling, simulated publish, mock analytics/reporting — as real, operable, persistent local behavior with full empty/loading/success/error states on every screen, single-user, no login backend required beyond maybe a local profile concept if that's even part of the observed feature set. Non-goals, explicit: no real social platform OAuth or posting, no real engagement automation against live accounts, no multi-tenant team/RBAC backend, no resumable server-side job queue, no NFT/wallet anything (killing Gemma3's framing by name so nobody resurrects it later by accident). Production-ready means: every simulated workflow actually runs end to end on-device with persisted state across relaunch, no hidden placeholder screens, and the final review explicitly maps which cited Ordinal features were reproduced, which were descoped as non-goals, and which are still unverified assumptions because nobody in this room has actually confirmed the source page. Decision rule for every later phase: if a feature can't be traced to a citation the way Codex's could, or contradicts another contributor's claim the way Gemma3's did, it doesn't ship as a hard requirement — it goes in the unverified/deferred bucket until someone actually loads the page and checks.

**Gemma3 (local) (Delivery Lead · Pragmatist) — Round 2**

Okay, let's cut to the chase. This whole “repeat the prompt hundreds of times” thing is a massive waste of time and frankly, suggests a fundamental misunderstanding of how to actually *build* something. We need to shift gears immediately.

Here’s the preservation of the original prompt: “Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: https://www.tryordinal.com”

Now, let’s move to the contract.

**Hard Requirements (Minimum Viable Product - MVP):**

1.  **Core Creative Brief Intake:** The app *must* allow a user to input a creative brief – a text field for description, potentially with fields for key stakeholders, target audience, and desired tone. This is non-negotiable; Ordinal’s success hinges on effective brief capture.
2.  **Asset Upload & Review:** Users must be able to upload an image or video asset and then designate reviewers (initially, just themselves) to provide feedback. Simple approval/reject toggles are essential.
3.  **Local Persistence:** The entire workflow – briefs, assets, reviews – *must* be persistently stored locally on the user's iOS device using SwiftUI’s `@State` and `@ObservedObject` to manage state. No external databases or backend infrastructure to start.
4.  **Basic Campaign/Project Organization:** The app needs a simple way to group assets and briefs under a “Campaign” or “Project” label – just a text field to define this.

**Non-Goals (Absolutely NOT Included in Phase 1):**

1.  **Multi-User Collaboration:** No real-time co-editing, team management, or user roles. This adds immense complexity and is *way* beyond a prototype.
2.  **External Integrations:** No connection to Adobe Creative Cloud, Slack, or any other third-party platform. We're focusing on a self-contained, local workflow.
3.  **Admin/Team Management:** No user accounts, permissions, or team dashboards.
4.  **Complex Campaign Tracking:**  No detailed analytics or reporting on campaign performance. We need to capture the basic flow.

**Production-Readiness Definition (Phase 1):**

This is a *functional* prototype. It’s meant to demonstrate the core workflow and gather initial user feedback, not to be a polished, scalable application. Key indicators of “production-ready” for this phase:

*   **Stable Core Functionality:** The brief intake, asset upload, and review flow must consistently work without crashing or unexpected behavior.
*   **Clear User Interface:** The UI must be understandable and navigable – not overly complex, but providing a clear pathway for the user to complete the core tasks.
*   **Working Persistence:** Data must be reliably saved and retrieved across app sessions.

**Decision Rules for Later Phases:**

*   **Third-Party Packages:** We default to *no* third-party packages for the initial prototype. *Only* if SwiftUI proves to be fundamentally insufficient to manage state or provide a particular feature (e.g., more advanced image editing) will we explore package integration.  The justification for *any* package needs to be explicitly stated and justified.
*   **UI/UX Enhancements:** UI/UX enhancements (beyond the absolute minimum) are deferred until Phase 2, based on user feedback.
*   **Scalability:** The design *must* be architected with scalability in mind – even if we're not actively building it out, the decisions we make now will affect future expansion.

Let’s be clear: this isn't about perfectly replicating Ordinal. It’s about creating a functional iOS prototype that validates our core concept. The biggest risk here is over-engineering – building a system that's too complex and difficult to maintain. We need to focus on the *smallest* set of features that delivers value.

title: Product Research
output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Here's where the room landed. Codex and Claude did the real work this round, and rather than clashing they filled in different halves of the same picture — Codex focused on UX patterns and information architecture, Claude focused on data/storage architecture and edge-case risk. Gemma3 mostly repackaged the existing contract under the name "Flow" with a timeline-view addition that Codex had already proposed, so it doesn't add new disagreement. Qwen's contribution this round is just a verbatim copy of the prior phase's final contract — no new research content, so it shouldn't count as an independent voice, same as the "Eva" issue flagged last phase; worth naming so nobody mistakes it for a fourth independent confirmation.

On substance, nobody contradicted anybody. The group agrees the audience is a solo operator — freelance marketer, one-person in-house function, or agency contractor — working in bursty, interstitial moments (reviewing on the couch, approving between meetings), which makes iPhone the primary target and iPad a non-driver for v1. They agree on borrowing proven native patterns rather than inventing UI: Photos.app's non-destructive edit-history model for versioning, Reminders/Things-style list CRUD with swipe actions, a Kanban-style status board for the review state machine, plus Share Sheet import and QuickLook for media preview instead of custom viewers. Platform opportunities converge on speed and native feel: one-tap asset intake from camera/Photos/Files, typed or voice brief capture, swipe gestures for status changes, local (on-device only) notifications for stale "In Review" items, and a timeline/queue view.

The risk list is longer than three and nobody pushed back on any item: (1) the Ordinal feature list is still unverified — this phase inherits that caveat and doesn't resolve it, that's for the next phase to re-fetch; (2) the Campaign/Brief/Asset/Review object model needs to be frozen now since the whole screen flow depends on it; (3) asset bytes should be file-backed in Documents/Application Support with SwiftData holding metadata + URL references only, not embedded blobs, to avoid store bloat/fragility; (4) "safe/undoable deletion" needs one concrete mechanism decided now — Claude's proposal (short undo toast + a trash/restore section) is on the table and nobody objected; (5) non-destructive versioning has an unbounded storage-growth failure mode that should be documented as a known v1 limitation, not solved; (6) single-device persistence (no sync/iCloud) means losing the phone means losing everything — that needs to be stated plainly rather than surfacing as a surprise later; (7) an explicit answer is needed for what happens if the only version of an asset is deleted while its campaign is "In Review."

## Final Output

**Audience and use context:** A solo marketing operator — freelancer, one-person in-house marketer, or agency contractor managing a handful of client campaigns alone, with no one to hand work off to. Usage is bursty and interstitial (reviewing feedback on the couch, approving between meetings, drafting a brief on the go), which makes iPhone the primary and only real design target for v1; iPad is out of scope, not a driver.

**Comparable apps or patterns to learn from:** Photos.app's non-destructive edit-history model (edit stack, always-know-the-current-version) as the direct precedent for asset versioning; Reminders/Things for list-based CRUD, empty states, and swipe-action vocabulary; a Kanban-style status board/filter-chip metaphor for the Draft → In Review → Approved/Rework state machine; Figma's "version + comment + status" rhythm as a mental model for review clarity; Notion/Airtable-style flexible metadata tagging for campaigns. All reproducible with SwiftUI `List`, swipe actions, SF Symbols, and QuickLook — no third-party package needed.

**Platform-specific opportunities:** Share Sheet integration so assets import directly from Photos/Files/Safari into a campaign; QuickLook for previewing image/video/PDF assets instead of a custom media viewer; camera/photo-library one-tap asset intake; typed or voice brief capture; swipe gestures and contextual action chips ("Submit for review," "Mark rework") for fast status transitions; a timeline/queue view for scanning campaign progress at a glance; local, on-device-only notifications nudging about stale "In Review" items (no server, consistent with the privacy non-goals).

**Major assumptions and risks:**
1. The Ordinal feature list remains unverified from a live source — this is still a working assumption, not fact, and the next phase must attempt the re-fetch per the existing decision rule.
2. Campaign, Brief, Asset, and Review-state must be frozen as the core object model now, since the screen flow and data model both depend on it.
3. Asset binary data should be file-backed in Documents/Application Support with SwiftData/Core Data holding only metadata and a URL reference — embedding large media blobs directly in the persistent store risks bloat and fragile launches.
4. "Safe/undoable deletion" needs one concrete mechanism, not an ad hoc build-time decision: a short-lived undo toast plus a restorable trash section is the working answer.
5. Non-destructive versioning has an unbounded storage-growth failure mode (nothing is ever deleted) — this is a known, accepted v1 limitation to document, not solve.
6. No sync or iCloud backup means all data lives on one device — losing the phone loses everything. This must be stated plainly to the user/in review output, not discovered as a surprise later.
7. Edge case needing a defined answer before build: what happens if the only version of an asset is deleted while its campaign is "In Review"? (Recommended default: block/require confirmation and auto-transition the campaign or asset back to a safe state rather than leaving it orphaned — the actual rule should be nailed down explicitly in the design phase.)

**Implications for the first build:** Minimum four screens — campaign list/filters, campaign detail (brief + status overview + timeline), asset list/detail (with version history), and a status-history log screen. Freeze the Campaign/Asset/Review object model immediately. Store asset bytes as files with SwiftData metadata pointers. Implement one concrete delete/undo pattern (toast + trash) rather than inventing it mid-build. Use Share Sheet and QuickLook to cut custom-UI risk. Every screen keeps real empty/loading/success/error states and explicit recovery paths (undo delete, retry on validation failure). Document the single-device data-loss risk and the unbounded-versioning-storage limitation as known, named v1 constraints rather than silent gaps.

# build-me-a-prototype-for-a-marketing-platform — Product Research

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-10 13:38:57._

## Original Prompt

```
Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: 

https://www.tryordinal.com
```

## Phase Purpose

Before product debate begins, infer the likely market, audience, competitors/patterns, platform expectations, risks, and native iOS opportunities from the prompt. Label assumptions clearly and turn the research into implications for the first build.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 1**

The most defensible first direction is to treat this as a single-user, local-first iOS “campaign production cockpit” with a strict value loop: define a brief, attach and version assets, move items through review states, and finish with a decision/rework artifact that the user can trust and recover. That aligns with a product people can actually use on a phone, while staying inside the constraints that have already been set for this build system.

Audience and use context: this looks aimed at solo creators, freelancers, agency contractors, and small marketing operators who do short-form campaign planning by themselves and currently bounce between Notes, messaging apps, and cloud folders. Their biggest pain is not lack of “features,” it is uncertainty and lost context: what changed, who approved, and which creative asset is current.

Comparable apps or patterns to learn from: Notion and Airtable-like database mental models for campaign/object hierarchy, Figma’s “version + comments + status” rhythm for review clarity, and lightweight project tools like Trello/Asana for status transitions and filters. On iOS specifically, Goodnotes-like quick-note entry and Photos’ asset grouping are good patterns to borrow for speed and discoverability, but this app should avoid their complexity and stay opinionated.

Platform-specific opportunities: iPhone should make this feel immediate—single-tap asset intake from camera/photo library, typed or voice brief capture, contextual action chips (“Submit for review,” “Mark rework”), swipe gestures for status transitions, and a timeline view that reads like a queue so users can act quickly. Dynamic type and clear contrast should be first-class, not afterthought. A local search + filter strip (campaign/status/platform/date) is a better iOS fit than deep nested menus.

Major assumptions and risks: first, we cannot verify the Ordinal feature list from a live source in this phase, so the interpretation is a working assumption and must remain tagged as unverified scope. Second, “copy their feature set” is high-variance unless we freeze core objects around Campaign, Brief, Asset, and Review state; if that object model shifts, the whole screen flow shifts. Third, an audit trail in a one-user app must be a state-history log with timestamps, not a social “user activity feed,” otherwise the requirement is internally inconsistent. Fourth, local media handling on-device can quietly become slow or bloated if we don’t enforce compression/version policy and deterministic IDs from day one.

The biggest risk I see is false parity inflation: adding features like OAuth, social posting, analytics, or collaboration gives an “I’m copying a platform” feel, but breaks feasibility and risks safety issues (for real platform automation) while reducing confidence in this first release. Biggest objection to my own direction is that people expecting full marketing SaaS parity will be disappointed if they notice what’s intentionally omitted, so this needs to be explicit in onboarding and copy.

Implications for the first build: freeze one clear information architecture with four screens minimum—campaign list/filters, campaign detail with brief + status overview, asset list/detail with versions and review actions, and history screen showing immutable status transitions. Every screen and action must have explicit empty/loading/success/error states and explicit recovery options (undo delete, retry after validation fail). Define Campaign and Asset as first-class objects now, with non-destructive versioning and a “single-author” approval log in the data model. Preserve this contract detail: Draft → In Review → Approved / Needs Rework, with rework requiring a non-empty note, and local-only persistence without hidden sharing or background collection.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Given the contract we're locked into — single-user, local-first, offline iOS app modeled on Ordinal's brief/asset/review-approval shape — I want to ground this phase in who's actually opening this app and what breaks first if we get it wrong, rather than re-litigating the feature list.

The realistic audience is a solo operator: a freelance marketer, a one-person in-house marketing function at a small business, or an agency contractor juggling a handful of client campaigns on their own. This is not a team tool pretending to be single-user — it's genuinely built for someone who has no one to hand work off to, which is why the "approval log" resolved to a status-history trail rather than a multi-actor feed. Use context is bursty and interstitial: reviewing a asset on the couch after a client emailed feedback, approving something between meetings, drafting a brief on a train. That argues for iPhone as the primary target (the contract doesn't mention iPad, and I'd treat iPad as a nice-to-have layout concern at most, not a design driver for v1).

For comparable patterns, I'd look at apps that already solved pieces of this well natively rather than inventing UI: Photos.app's non-destructive edit history is the direct precedent for "versions append, current is marked, nothing overwritten" — that's a proven interaction model (edit stack, revert-to-original) we can borrow the shape of without copying the UI. Reminders/Things give us the empty-state and swipe-action vocabulary for list-based CRUD. A Kanban-ish status board (Draft/In Review/Approved/Rework as columns or filter chips) is a well-understood metaphor for the state machine and avoids us inventing a bespoke workflow visualization. None of these require a third-party package — they're all reproducible with List, swipe actions, and SF Symbols.

Platform opportunities worth calling out: a Share Sheet extension so assets can be imported directly from Photos/Files/Safari into a campaign instead of forcing a clunky in-app picker; QuickLook for previewing images/video/PDF assets instead of building a custom media viewer (this alone kills a large chunk of risk); swipe actions for fast approve/rework on list rows; local (on-device, no server) notifications nudging the user about stale "In Review" items, which is safe under the privacy non-goals since nothing leaves the device.

Now the risk side, because I think this is where the next build phase is most likely to quietly paint itself into a corner. First: where do asset bytes actually live? If video/image binary data gets stored directly inside the SwiftData store, the store bloats fast and becomes fragile — corruption or slow launches. The safer pattern is file-backed storage in the app's Documents/Application Support directory with SwiftData holding only metadata and a URL reference, and that decision needs to be made now, not discovered mid-build. Second: "safe/undoable deletion" is still underspecified the same way "versioning" was before we fixed it — undoable how? A toast with a few seconds to undo, or a soft-delete/trash bin the user can restore from later? Those are very different builds and I don't want it invented ad hoc at implementation time. Third: because there's deliberately no backend or iCloud sync (correctly, per non-goals), all campaign history lives on one device — that's a real, user-facing risk (lose the phone, lose everything) that we're consciously accepting for v1, but it should be named explicitly in this phase's output rather than surfacing as a surprise in the final review. Fourth, and more mechanical: non-destructive versioning has an unbounded storage-growth failure mode — nothing ever gets deleted, so a campaign with a dozen video re-uploads could eat serious device storage with no cleanup story; that doesn't need to be solved now, but it needs to be flagged as a known limitation rather than silently ignored. Fifth: the "zero assets" and "mid-review deletion" edge cases already named in the contract compound with versioning — what happens if the only version of an asset gets deleted while its campaign is "In Review"? That state needs a defined answer before it's discovered as a crash or a stuck state during build.

For implications on the first build: keep asset storage file-backed with metadata-only in SwiftData, pick one concrete undo/delete mechanism now (I'd lean toward a short-lived undo toast plus a trash section rather than instant hard-delete, since it's cheap and matches "safe/undoable" without inventing a full trash-management UI), don't attempt any sync or export mechanism in v1 but state the single-device risk plainly in whatever final review closes this out, and lean on QuickLook/Share Sheet/swipe actions instead of custom-building media viewing or import flows — that's the biggest lever we have for shipping something small that still feels native rather than a half-finished custom UI kit.

**Gemma3 (local) (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Okay, let's do this.

Original prompt, verbatim: "Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: https://www.tryordinal.com"

My strongest first-pass contract centers around building a local-first iOS app – let’s call it “Flow” – that acts as a streamlined campaign production cockpit for a solo marketing professional. The goal isn't to *become* Ordinal, which is a multi-tenant, API-heavy SaaS; instead, Flow is a focused tool that replicates Ordinal's core workflow: briefing, asset management, approval gating, and basic campaign tracking – all within a single, offline iOS app.

Hard requirements:

1.  **Campaign Creation & Management:** The app *must* provide a robust mechanism for creating and organizing campaigns. This includes defining campaign goals (e.g., “drive website traffic,” “generate leads”), target audiences, and overarching brand tones. Think a flexible, free-form text field, but with the capability to attach metadata tags (e.g., “summer campaign,” “social media focus”).
2.  **Asset Library:** Users need a way to upload and manage campaign assets (images, videos, documents).  The app should natively support common image and video formats, and allow for basic organization via folders and tagging. We don’t need advanced editing tools, just a streamlined upload and retrieval system.
3.  **Review & Approval Workflow:**  A critical component. The app needs to facilitate a structured review process where assets can be assigned to reviewers (initially, just the user themselves) and approvals/rejections can be tracked with timestamps and notes.  We're aiming for a simple, intuitive state machine: Draft -> In Review -> Approved / Needs Rework.
4.  **Offline Persistence:** Everything – campaigns, assets, review history – *must* be persistently stored locally on the device using SwiftData or Core Data.  This is non-negotiable for a focused, local-first workflow.  No syncing with any external platforms.
5. **Basic Campaign Timeline:** The app should display a clear timeline view showcasing the stages of a campaign’s progression (e.g., “Brief Created,” “Assets Uploaded,” “Review In Progress,” “Approved”). This helps users visualize and manage their campaigns.

Non-goals:

*   **Multi-User Collaboration:** Absolutely no support for teams or shared workflows. This adds significant complexity that’s outside the scope of this prototype.
*   **Third-Party Integrations:** No integration with any external platforms (e.g., social media APIs, Adobe Creative Cloud, Slack, or CRM systems). We're focused on a self-contained workflow.
*   **Advanced Analytics:** No sophisticated analytics or reporting features. The timeline view will provide basic campaign progress tracking.
*   **Automated Engagement:**  No features for automatically posting content or engaging with users on social media.

Output Shape:

I envision this as a polished iOS app artifact with a clean, intuitive user interface and a well-documented architecture. It needs to be demonstrable and usable – a single, coherent application that demonstrates a functional, albeit simplified, version of Ordinal's core workflow. I want a project artifact – not just code – with clear design considerations, thorough testing documentation, and well-defined limitations.

Production-Readiness Definition:

“Production-ready” for this phase means the app is functional, stable, and usable by a real user, even with limited functionality.  It should be able to run smoothly without crashing, data should be reliably saved and retrieved, and the core workflow – creating a campaign, uploading assets, and getting approval – should work consistently. It won’t be a perfect product; it's a prototype.  We need to confirm the core loop is reliable offline.

Decision Rules for Later Phases:

*   Any feature request that implies external authentication, backend synchronization, or platform API integration will be deferred until a separate phase focuses on these aspects.
*   Any design decision that compromises the local-first, offline-capable nature of the app will be flagged and reconsidered.
*   If ambiguity arises regarding a specific feature, we'll establish a clear, documented decision rule to avoid future disagreements.

Biggest Risk/Objection:

The greatest risk here is over-scoping. Trying to replicate every aspect of Ordinal’s platform will inevitably lead to a complex, bloated, and ultimately unusable app. We need to remain laser-focused on delivering a streamlined, functional workflow and resist the temptation to add “nice-to-have” features.

I want to lock in this key deliverable: the app must clearly show *how* a marketing professional would use Ordinal’s core workflow – from brief to approval – in a single, self-contained iOS app.

---

That’s my initial assessment. Let’s get to work.

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

**Original Prompt:**

Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: [https://www.tryordinal.com](https://www.tryordinal.com)

1. One SwiftUI iOS app, single-user, fully offline, deterministic local persistence (SwiftData/Core Data) — no backend, no login system.
2. Campaign and Asset as first-class persisted objects.
3. A brief-intake screen (goals/audience/tone/platform context, free-text is fine).
4. Asset upload and organization, grouped under a Campaign/Project.
5. Asset versioning that's non-destructive: new versions append to a history list, the current version is always clearly marked, nothing gets silently overwritten.
6. A review/approval flow with an explicit state machine: Draft → In Review → Approved / Needs Rework, where sending something back to Rework requires a non-empty note.
7. A timestamped status-history log per campaign/asset — this is a single-actor audit trail (there's only ever one user), not a multi-person activity feed.
8. Full CRUD with safe/undoable deletion, including edge cases like deleting an asset mid-review or a campaign with zero assets.
9. Every screen needs real empty, loading, success, and error states — no placeholders, no stub screens.
10. Accessibility (labels, dynamic type, contrast, focus order) and privacy defaults (no background collection, no remote data sharing) from day one.
11. Data must survive app relaunch reliably.

**Non-Goals:**

1. Multi-user collaboration, team accounts, roles/RBAC.
2. Any real integration with external platforms — social media APIs/OAuth, Slack, Adobe, ad platforms, CRMs, webhooks/MCP.
3. Any real posting or engagement automation against live accounts (this is a genuine safety/ToS risk, not just a scope call — nothing in this app touches real credentials or real platforms).
4. Billing/subscriptions, multi-tenant workspaces, server-side job queues.
5. Any web-parity claim against tryordinal.com's actual site.
6. NFT, wallet, or Bitcoin-Ordinals-related functionality of any kind — dead, do not resurrect.
7. Third-party packages by default. One may be introduced later only if Apple's own frameworks genuinely can't do the job, and it must be justified explicitly in-contract when proposed.

**Production-Readiness Definition:**

Not a mockup or wireframe shell — a complete, working loop where a user can create a campaign, add a brief, upload and version assets, run them through review to approved-or-rework, and see a persistent status history, entirely offline, with no crashes, no silent data loss, and real (not stubbed) empty/loading/success/error states across every screen, including the edge cases named above.

**Decision Rules for Later Phases:**

1. Any feature that implies external auth, backend collaboration, real platform API writes, or multi-user identity is deferred by default — it cannot become a hard requirement.
2. Before implementation begins, the next phase must attempt to re-fetch tryordinal.com (and /pricing) directly. If confirmed, reconcile any differences from this contract's assumptions. If the fetch keeps failing, fall back to secondary sources (App Store listing, press coverage) rather than further agent guessing, and the final review must state plainly that this contract was built without a confirmed first-party source.
3. Anything that can't be traced to an actual citation goes into a "deferred/unverified" bucket, not into hard scope.
4. If ambiguity resurfaces the way "versioning" or "approval log" did here, resolve it with an explicit, written rule at the time it's found — don't let it become an undocumented judgment call at build time.

**Quality Gate (Claude):**

QUALITY: PASS

**Feedback:**

This output meets the bar. It preserves the original prompt verbatim, and — critically — it handles the phase's central disagreement (what Ordinal actually is) the right way: instead of silently picking a framing and presenting it as fact, it names the assumption explicitly ("working assumption, not yet independently verified"), documents why the room converged there (three independently-reasoned paths landing on the same buildable shape), and locks a concrete decision rule forcing the next phase to re-fetch tryordinal.com/pricing before treating any of it as settled scope. That is exactly the "name the assumption, don't silently change the assignment" behavior the phase rules ask for. All five required outputs are present and concrete: prompt excerpt, hard requirements (with real specificity — e.g., the Draft→In Review→Approved/Rework state machine, non-destructive versioning, single-actor audit trail), non-goals, a production-readiness definition that goes beyond "it's a prototype" (full CRUD, real empty/loading/success/error states, edge cases named), and decision rules that will actually bind later phases (deferred/unverified bucket, re-fetch requirement, package justification rule). It also correctly surfaces and resolves the two ambiguities Claude flagged (versioning semantics, approval-log semantics) with explicit written rules rather than leaving them as coin-flips, and it strikes the two noise items (NFT/wallet tangent, fabricated "Eva" participant) by name so they can't resurface later. No repairs needed — this is a usable artifact for the next phase.

**Coordinator Decision:**

See the coordinator's message above.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: YES

Here's where the room landed. Codex and Claude did the real work this round, and rather than clashing they filled in different halves of the same picture — Codex focused on UX patterns and information architecture, Claude focused on data/storage architecture and edge-case risk. Gemma3 mostly repackaged the existing contract under the name "Flow" with a timeline-view addition that Codex had already proposed, so it doesn't add new disagreement. Qwen's contribution this round is just a verbatim copy of the prior phase's final contract — no new research content, so it shouldn't count as an independent voice, same as the "Eva" issue flagged last phase; worth naming so nobody mistakes it for a fourth independent confirmation.

On substance, nobody contradicted anybody. The group agrees the audience is a solo operator — freelance marketer, one-person in-house function, or agency contractor — working in bursty, interstitial moments (reviewing on the couch, approving between meetings), which makes iPhone the primary target and iPad a non-driver for v1. They agree on borrowing proven native patterns rather than inventing UI: Photos.app's non-destructive edit-history model for versioning, Reminders/Things-style list CRUD with swipe actions, a Kanban-style status board for the review state machine, plus Share Sheet import and QuickLook for media preview instead of custom viewers. Platform opportunities converge on speed and native feel: one-tap asset intake from camera/Photos/Files, typed or voice brief capture, swipe gestures for status changes, local (on-device only) notifications for stale "In Review" items, and a timeline/queue view.

The risk list is longer than three and nobody pushed back on any item: (1) the Ordinal feature list is still unverified — this phase inherits that caveat and doesn't resolve it, that's for the next phase to re-fetch; (2) the Campaign/Brief/Asset/Review object model needs to be frozen now since the whole screen flow depends on it; (3) asset bytes should be file-backed in Documents/Application Support with SwiftData holding metadata + URL references only, not embedded blobs, to avoid store bloat/fragility; (4) "safe/undoable deletion" needs one concrete mechanism decided now — Claude's proposal (short undo toast + a trash/restore section) is on the table and nobody objected; (5) non-destructive versioning has an unbounded storage-growth failure mode that should be documented as a known v1 limitation, not solved; (6) single-device persistence (no sync/iCloud) means losing the phone means losing everything — that needs to be stated plainly rather than surfacing as a surprise later; (7) an explicit answer is needed for what happens if the only version of an asset is deleted while its campaign is "In Review."

## Final Output

**Audience and use context:** A solo marketing operator — freelancer, one-person in-house marketer, or agency contractor managing a handful of client campaigns alone, with no one to hand work off to. Usage is bursty and interstitial (reviewing feedback on the couch, approving between meetings, drafting a brief on the go), which makes iPhone the primary and only real design target for v1; iPad is out of scope, not a driver.

**Comparable apps or patterns to learn from:** Photos.app's non-destructive edit-history model (edit stack, always-know-the-current-version) as the direct precedent for asset versioning; Reminders/Things for list-based CRUD, empty states, and swipe-action vocabulary; a Kanban-style status board/filter-chip metaphor for the Draft → In Review → Approved/Rework state machine; Figma's "version + comment + status" rhythm as a mental model for review clarity; Notion/Airtable-style flexible metadata tagging for campaigns. All reproducible with SwiftUI `List`, swipe actions, SF Symbols, and QuickLook — no third-party package needed.

**Platform-specific opportunities:** Share Sheet integration so assets import directly from Photos/Files/Safari into a campaign; QuickLook for previewing image/video/PDF assets instead of a custom media viewer; camera/photo-library one-tap asset intake; typed or voice brief capture; swipe gestures and contextual action chips ("Submit for review," "Mark rework") for fast status transitions; a timeline/queue view for scanning campaign progress at a glance; local, on-device-only notifications nudging about stale "In Review" items (no server, consistent with the privacy non-goals).

**Major assumptions and risks:**
1. The Ordinal feature list remains unverified from a live source — this is still a working assumption, not fact, and the next phase must attempt the re-fetch per the existing decision rule.
2. Campaign, Brief, Asset, and Review-state must be frozen as the core object model now, since the screen flow and data model both depend on it.
3. Asset binary data should be file-backed in Documents/Application Support with SwiftData/Core Data holding only metadata and a URL reference — embedding large media blobs directly in the persistent store risks bloat and fragile launches.
4. "Safe/undoable deletion" needs one concrete mechanism, not an ad hoc build-time decision: a short-lived undo toast plus a restorable trash section is the working answer.
5. Non-destructive versioning has an unbounded storage-growth failure mode (nothing is ever deleted) — this is a known, accepted v1 limitation to document, not solve.
6. No sync or iCloud backup means all data lives on one device — losing the phone loses everything. This must be stated plainly to the user/in review output, not discovered as a surprise later.
7. Edge case needing a defined answer before build: what happens if the only version of an asset is deleted while its campaign is "In Review"? (Recommended default: block/require confirmation and auto-transition the campaign or asset back to a safe state rather than leaving it orphaned — the actual rule should be nailed down explicitly in the design phase.)

**Implications for the first build:** Minimum four screens — campaign list/filters, campaign detail (brief + status overview + timeline), asset list/detail (with version history), and a status-history log screen. Freeze the Campaign/Asset/Review object model immediately. Store asset bytes as files with SwiftData metadata pointers. Implement one concrete delete/undo pattern (toast + trash) rather than inventing it mid-build. Use Share Sheet and QuickLook to cut custom-UI risk. Every screen keeps real empty/loading/success/error states and explicit recovery paths (undo delete, retry on validation failure). Document the single-device data-loss risk and the unbounded-versioning-storage limitation as known, named v1 constraints rather than silent gaps.

QUALITY: PASS

It stays consistent with the locked prompt contract (SwiftUI, local-first, no backend, Draft→In Review→Approved/Rework, non-destructive versioning, single-actor audit trail) and correctly defers the one still-open item (asset-deleted-mid-review edge case) to the design phase with a named recommended default rather than silently deciding it or silently ignoring it. That's the right move for a research phase, not a defect.

No repairs needed — usable artifact for the next phase.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.
