title: Portfolio Selection
output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Here's where the room landed on round 1: everyone agrees, without any real pushback, that this is a single-app request — one native iOS "marketing campaign cockpit" app, not a portfolio. The original prompt asks for one thing ("something like ordinal"), and the whole contract locked over the last three phases already assumes one integrated object model (Campaign, Brief, Asset, Review-state) — splitting that into separate sibling apps (a "brief app," an "asset app," a "review app") would actually break the design everyone already agreed to, since those pieces are meant to work as one connected flow, not independent products. So: one folder, build=true, no portfolio-json needed.

Where people added value without contradicting each other: Claude (Product Strategist seat) pushed the group past just restating the contract and into what actually matters for day one — the review-decision screen (looking at an asset and hitting Approve or Needs Rework) is the single highest-stakes moment in the whole app and deserves outsized design/QA attention, brief intake should be one fast free-text field with everything else optional/collapsed so campaign creation takes under 15 seconds, and the first-launch empty state needs to get a new user to their first campaign in one tap rather than a passive "nothing here" message. Nobody in the room pushed back on any of that — it reads as a refinement everyone's fine with, not a fork. Gemma3 flagged wanting a deeper conversation about "versioning" semantics, but that's already been resolved explicitly in earlier phases (append-only history, current version always marked, never overwritten), so that's not actually open — just a contributor catching up to where the contract already is, not new disagreement.

## Final Output

**Single-app vs. portfolio-parent decision:** Single app. No portfolio split. The prompt requests one product, and the locked contract already treats Campaign/Brief/Asset/Review as one integrated domain — dividing it into sibling apps would fragment a workflow that's designed to be one connected loop.

**Selected app:**
- Category: marketing workflow / campaign production
- Slug: `marketing-campaign-cockpit`
- Promise: Let a solo marketer create campaigns, capture a brief, upload and version assets, move each asset through Draft → In Review → Approved/Needs Rework, and keep a persistent local audit trail — entirely offline, single-user, on iPhone.
- Target user: Freelance marketer, agency contractor, or one-person in-house marketing operator managing a handful of client campaigns alone.
- Build: true

**Selection rationale:** The prompt is singular ("build me something like ordinal"), and three prior phases of debate already converged on one buildable object model. A single SwiftUI app is the only shape that can ship the full brief→asset→review→approval loop reliably without inventing cross-app sync or artificial seams between what is really one workflow.

**Rejected alternatives:** A separate scheduling/publishing companion app, a standalone analytics app, and an asset-management-only app were all considered and rejected — each would either duplicate the same core state machine, require backend/integration assumptions explicitly ruled out as non-goals, or represent an unverified guess about Ordinal's actual (still-unconfirmed) feature set rather than the grounded contract this room already built.

**Carried-forward product notes for the build phase (non-binding refinements, not new hard requirements):** treat the review-decision screen (current asset version + Approve/Needs Rework, QuickLook-based, hard to misclick) as the product's center of gravity; keep brief intake to one fast free-text field with audience/tone/platform genuinely optional and collapsed; make the first-launch empty state guide a new user to a created campaign in one tap.

**Portfolio-json:** Not applicable — this prompt does not require a portfolio split; recorded explicitly per phase rules.
discussion_markdown:
# build-me-a-prototype-for-a-marketing-platform — Portfolio Selection

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-10 13:43:09._

## Original Prompt

```
Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: 

https://www.tryordinal.com
```

## Phase Purpose

If the prompt requests multiple apps, force a real independent selection process: one app per requested category or requested count, with no collapsed wrapper project. Produce the required portfolio-json manifest for sibling child projects. If the prompt is a single app, explicitly record that no portfolio split is needed.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Single-app, not a portfolio parent. No portfolio split is needed because the request is for one marketing workflow product, and adding sibling apps would fragment the same campaign/brief/assets pipeline without independent user need.

Selected app: category "marketing workflow"; slug "marketing-campaign-cockpit"; promise "let a solo marketer create campaigns, capture briefs, upload and version assets, move each item through Draft -> In Review -> Approved/Needs Rework, and keep a local audit trail that survives relaunch"; target user "freelance marketer, contractor, or one-person marketing operator on iPhone"; build: true.

Rationale: the prompt is singular and asks for one “like Ordinal” experience, so this is one concrete native iOS app contract instead of several parallel apps. A single object model (campaign, asset, version, status log) is the only shape that can ship reliably in this phase.

Rejected alternatives: a separate scheduling/publishing companion app, a separate analytics app, and a separate asset-management-only app were rejected because each would either duplicate flow/state, depend on backend integrations, or drift into unverified interpretation of Ordinal’s web features.

The biggest risk in this choice is building the wrong feature shape if Ordinal’s real site differs; this must be carried forward explicitly by requiring real re-fetch/verification in the next phase before any assumptions become hard scope, and by keeping this phase locked to local-first, offline, no-backend behavior to guarantee a shippable minimum.

**Claude (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

This is a single-app run — no portfolio split needed here. The prompt names one product ("something like ordinal"), and the contract already locked this into one campaign-production-cockpit iOS app: Campaign, Brief, Asset, Review-state as the core objects, offline/local-first, single user. There's no requested count or set of categories to divide across sibling projects — splitting this into multiple apps (e.g., a separate "brief app" and "asset app" and "review app") would violate the phase's own object model, which treats Campaign and Asset as one integrated domain, not three products. One folder, one build=true entry.

From the Product Strategist / User Advocate seat, I want to push on something the last three phases haven't nailed down yet: what does day one actually look like for the specific person using this, and what's the one thing that has to work perfectly or the whole app is dead on arrival?

The user is a solo operator with, realistically, 2-6 active campaigns at a time, each with a handful of assets cycling through review. The single highest-value moment in this entire app is the review decision itself — the instant where they look at an asset (probably a photo or short video someone sent them, or that they made) and decide Approved or Needs Rework. Everything else — the brief screen, campaign metadata, tags — is scaffolding around that moment. If that screen is slow to load a video, if the rework note is annoying to type, if it's unclear which asset version they're even looking at, the app fails at its one job. So I want the production-readiness bar for that screen specifically to be higher than "has four states" — it needs to load the current version instantly (QuickLook, not a custom viewer, per prior research), show version number/date at a glance, and make the two decision buttons (Approve / Needs Rework) impossible to misclick or trigger accidentally via swipe.

The biggest risk to my own direction: I think there's a real temptation building through these phases to over-model the "campaign" concept — goals, audience, tone, platform context, tags — into something that feels like filling out a corporate brief template. A solo freelancer doesn't want to fill out five fields before they can upload a photo. I'd rather the brief-intake screen have one meaningful free-text field front and center (what is this campaign for) with everything else — audience, tone, platform — genuinely optional and collapsed by default, so campaign creation takes under 15 seconds. If a later phase makes brief intake feel like a form, that's a real product failure even if every field maps cleanly to Ordinal's supposed feature set.

Second risk, and this is a repeat of what surfaced in research but I want to sharpen it into a product requirement rather than a technical footnote: the empty state for a brand-new user matters enormously and I haven't seen anyone actually spec it. First launch, zero campaigns — this can't just be "no campaigns yet, tap + to add one." It should make the one required action obvious and get them to a created campaign in one tap, because if the first five seconds are confusing, a solo operator (who has no onboarding call, no teammate showing them around) just deletes the app.

What the final phase output needs to preserve from my seat: this stays single-app, no portfolio; the review-decision screen (asset + version indicator + Approve/Rework with mandatory rework note) is the product's center of gravity and should get disproportionate design and QA attention relative to campaign/brief screens; brief intake defaults to one fast free-text field with everything else optional and collapsed; and the first-launch empty state must get someone to their first created campaign in one tap, not a passive "nothing here yet" message.

**Gemma3 (local) (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Okay, here’s my first-pass contribution as Gemma3 (local), Design Lead:

Original prompt (verbatim): “Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: https://www.tryordinal.com”

My strongest first-pass contract centers around building a local-first iOS app – let’s call it “Flow” – that acts as a streamlined campaign production cockpit for a solo marketing professional. The goal isn’t to *become* Ordinal, which is a multi-tenant, API-heavy SaaS; instead, Flow is a focused tool that replicates Ordinal’s core workflow: briefing, asset management, approval gating, and basic campaign tracking – all within a single, offline iOS app.

Hard requirements:

1.  **Campaign Creation & Management:** The app *must* provide a robust mechanism for creating and organizing campaigns. This includes defining campaign goals (e.g., “drive website traffic,” “generate leads”), target audiences, and overarching brand tones. Think a flexible, free-form text field, but with the capability to attach metadata tags (e.g., “summer campaign,” “social media focus”).
2.  **Asset Library:** Users need a way to upload and manage campaign assets (images, videos, documents).  The app should natively support common image and video formats, and allow for basic organization via folders and tagging. We don’t need advanced editing tools, just a streamlined upload and retrieval system.
3.  **Review & Approval Workflow:**  A critical component. The app needs to facilitate a structured review process where assets can be assigned to reviewers (initially, just the user themselves) and approvals/rejections can be tracked with timestamps and notes.  We’re aiming for a simple, intuitive state machine: Draft → In Review → Approved / Needs Rework.
4.  **Offline Persistence:** Everything – campaigns, assets, review history – *must* be persistently stored locally on the device using SwiftData or Core Data.  This is non-negotiable for a focused, local-first workflow.  No syncing with any external platforms.
5. **Basic Campaign Timeline:** The app should display a clear timeline view showcasing the stages of a campaign’s progression (e.g., “Brief Created,” “Assets Uploaded,” “Review In Progress,” “Approved”). This helps users visualize and manage their campaigns.

Non-goals:

*   **Multi-User Collaboration:** Absolutely no support for teams or shared workflows. This adds significant complexity that’s outside the scope of this prototype.
*   **Third-Party Integrations:** No integration with any social media APIs, Adobe Creative Cloud, Slack, or CRM systems. We're focused on a self-contained workflow.
*   **Advanced Analytics:** No sophisticated analytics or reporting features. The timeline view will provide basic campaign progress tracking.
*   **Automated Engagement:**  No features for automatically posting content or engaging with users on social media.

Output Shape:

I envision this as a polished iOS app artifact with a clean, intuitive user interface and a well-documented architecture. It needs to be demonstrable and usable – a single, coherent application that demonstrates a functional, albeit simplified, version of Ordinal’s core workflow.

Production-Readiness Definition:

“Production-ready” for this phase means the app is functional, stable, and usable by a real user, even with limited functionality. It should be able to run smoothly without crashing, data should be reliably saved and retrieved, and the core workflow – creating a campaign, uploading assets, and getting approval – should work consistently. It’s not a perfect product; it’s a prototype. We need to confirm the core loop is reliable offline.

Decision Rules for Later Phases:

*   Any feature request that implies external authentication, backend synchronization, or platform API writes will be deferred until a separate phase focuses on these aspects.
*   Any design decision that compromises the local-first, offline-capable nature of the app will be flagged and reconsidered.
*   If ambiguity arises regarding a specific feature, we’ll establish a clear, documented decision rule to avoid future disagreements.

Biggest Risk/Objection:

The greatest risk here is over-scoping. Trying to replicate every aspect of Ordinal’s platform will inevitably lead to a complex, bloated, and ultimately unusable app. We need to remain laser-focused on delivering a streamlined, functional workflow and resist the temptation to add “nice-to-have” features.

I want to lock in this key deliverable: the app must clearly show *how* a marketing professional would use Ordinal’s core workflow – from brief to approval – in a single, self-contained iOS app.

---

*Note: I'm flagging the need for a critical, detailed discussion about the concept of “versioning” and its implementation here. The implications are significant and could easily derail this entire project if not addressed up front.*

Let’s move to the next round.

title: Initial Discussion
output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Here's the read on round 1. Codex and Claude both did real, substantive work and didn't contradict each other anywhere — Codex nailed down the product promise, primary user, core loop, and hard scope boundaries at the level this phase asks for, and Claude built directly on top of that with the actual screen-by-screen shape (Campaign List → Campaign Detail → Asset Detail → Status History) plus three concrete decisions that close gaps rather than open new ones: Approve requires a deliberate tap-and-confirm while Rework can be a single tap (because a wrongly-approved asset is the worse failure mode), deleting an asset that's the only one on an "In Review" campaign auto-reverts that campaign to Draft with a logged system line rather than leaving it orphaned, and previewing an old version is read-only — "restoring" an old version has to append as a new version, never silently rewrite history. Nobody pushed back on any of that, and it doesn't conflict with anything locked in earlier phases.

Gemma3 and Qwen's contributions this round are the same pattern flagged in every earlier phase of this run — near-verbatim restatements of the existing locked contract (Qwen's message even cuts off mid-sentence), not new independent reasoning, so they don't count as additional confirmation, but they also don't contradict anything Codex or Claude said.

Net: this phase's required outputs are all present, specific, and self-consistent — a one-sentence promise, a named user and scenario, a concrete four-screen core loop, explicit hard scope boundaries, and measurable, checkable success criteria. Scope is small enough to build in one pass and the out-of-scope list is explicit.

## Final Output

**One-sentence product promise:** A local-first SwiftUI iPhone app that lets a solo marketer take a campaign from a one-line brief through versioned asset uploads to an Approve/Needs-Rework decision, entirely on-device, with every transition permanently logged.

**Primary user and scenario:** A solo marketing operator (freelancer, one-person in-house marketer, or agency contractor) managing 2–6 campaigns alone, working in short interstitial bursts — drafting a brief on a train, reviewing an asset on the couch, approving between meetings. Never at a desk with focused time, so every core action must be reachable in two taps and the review decision must survive being done one-handed.

**Core loop (four screens, nothing more):**
1. **Campaign List** — persistent, unmissable "new campaign" affordance.
2. **Campaign Detail** — brief text, asset grid, status summary.
3. **Asset Detail** — current version pinned at top (e.g. "v3 of 3, uploaded Tuesday") with QuickLook preview, collapsed version-history strip below (tapping an old version previews it read-only; "restoring" it appends a new version, never overwrites), and Approve / Needs Rework controls. Approve requires a deliberate tap-and-confirm; Needs Rework is a single tap but requires a non-empty note. No swipe-to-approve, ever.
4. **Status History** — append-only log, per-asset and rolled up per-campaign, including system-generated lines (e.g. "asset removed, reverted to Draft").

**Hard scope boundaries:** One native iOS app, single-user, fully offline, no backend, no login, no team collaboration, no social/platform integrations or automation, no analytics beyond local history, no multi-device sync, no third-party packages unless Apple frameworks genuinely can't do the job. No fifth screen (no dashboard, no settings tab, no insights) — the only allowed addition is a static "About/limitations" screen disclosing the single-device data-loss risk. If a campaign's only asset is deleted while "In Review," the campaign auto-reverts to Draft rather than being left orphaned or silently vanishing.

**Measurable success criteria:**
- A first-time user goes from cold launch to a created campaign with a brief in under 15 seconds, with only the free-text brief required.
- All four screens demonstrably show correct empty/loading/success/error states — verifiable by wiping data and relaunching, and by force-killing mid-upload.
- A full cycle — create campaign → brief → upload v1 → upload v2 → submit for review → rework with note → upload v3 → approve → view history — survives an app relaunch with every version and timestamped transition intact, byte-identical.
- Deleting an asset mid-review and deleting a zero-asset campaign both complete without crashing and without leaving orphaned state (no asset pointing at a dead campaign, no campaign stuck in an impossible status).

If any of these can't be demonstrated, the app isn't done — regardless of how much of the broader feature list got built.

# build-me-a-prototype-for-a-marketing-platform — Initial Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-10 13:46:50._

## Original Prompt

```
Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: 

https://www.tryordinal.com
```

## Phase Purpose

Establish a shared, precise understanding of what this app is, who it is for, the core problem it solves, hard scope boundaries, and measurable success criteria.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

One-sentence product promise: Build a local-first SwiftUI iOS app that lets a solo marketer move a campaign from brief capture to versioned asset intake and approval decisions entirely on-device, with a clear audit trail and safe recovery when things change.

Primary user and scenario: One person marketer (freelancer, one-person in-house operator, or agency contractor) who works in short bursts and needs to stage assets, review them quickly between meetings, and make reliable approve/rework decisions without team handoffs, internet access, or account setup.

Core loop: Create or open a campaign with a brief, add or import media assets, attach a new version when changes come in, send an asset to review, approve it or send it back with a required rework note, and carry that status history forward so the next action is always obvious.

Hard scope boundaries: One native iOS app only, single-user, no backend, no login, no team collaboration, no social posting or platform automation, no external webhooks/OAuth/web integrations, no analytics pipeline beyond local history, no multi-device sync, and no third-party libraries unless Apple frameworks truly cannot cover a requirement. Campaign and asset are first-class local objects; versioning is append-only and never destructive; approval history is a single-user, timestamped status log; deletion is undo-safe. Every user flow must include empty/loading/success/error behavior and explicit recovery.

Measurable success criteria: A new user can complete a full end-to-end campaign cycle—create campaign, capture brief, import at least one asset, add a new version, submit for review, complete an approval or needs-rework decision with note, and view status history—on-device without crashes or silent failures; all state persists across relaunches; and each of the above workflows exposes a recoverable error path (undo/restore/ retry) for the edge cases users hit first in real use. biggest risk I’m calling out is trying to chase unverified Ordinal-parity features instead of shipping this local workflow loop, so the contract must preserve “best-effort matching of the workflow, not web-parity claims” and “asset bytes remain local file-backed with metadata in persistence” as non-negotiable constraints.

**Claude (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Building on the locked contract, here's my read as design lead on how this actually needs to work as a system of screens, not just a features list.

The product promise I'd write down: a local-first iPhone app where a solo marketer turns a one-line brief into a campaign, uploads and versions creative assets against it, and pushes each asset through an explicit Draft → In Review → Approved/Rework loop, with every decision permanently logged — no login, no network, no collaborator, ever.

Primary user and scenario: someone managing 2-6 client campaigns solo, opening the app in short bursts — on the train drafting a brief, on the couch reviewing a photo a subcontractor texted them, between meetings tapping Approve. They are never at a desk with focused time. That constrains the IA hard: everything has to be reachable in two taps from launch, and the review decision itself has to survive being done one-handed while distracted.

Core loop, concretely as screens: Campaign List (with a persistent, unmissable "new campaign" affordance) → Campaign Detail (brief text + asset grid + status summary) → Asset Detail (QuickLook preview of current version + version history strip + Approve/Rework controls) → Status History (append-only log, both per-asset and rolled up per-campaign). That's the whole app. I want to resist the urge to add a fifth screen — a dashboard, a settings screen, a "insights" tab — none of that serves the loop and all of it just gives QA more surface to half-finish.

Where I think the real design risk lives is in the transitions, not the screens themselves. A few things I don't want left as undocumented judgment calls at build time: what a swipe-to-approve gesture looks like versus a deliberate button tap — I'd say no swipe-to-approve at all, since an accidental full-approve is much worse than an accidental rework (rework just costs a re-tap; a wrongly-approved asset going out the door is the actual failure mode this app exists to prevent), so Approve should require a tap-and-confirm while Rework can be a single tap since it's the safer direction to err toward. Second, the interaction between deleting an asset and campaign status — if a campaign's only asset gets deleted while "In Review," the campaign can't silently stay In Review with nothing to review; it should auto-drop to Draft with a visible system-generated line in its status history ("asset removed, reverted to Draft"), not just vanish from the board. Third, versioning UI: the asset detail screen needs the current version pinned at the top with a clear "v3 of 3, uploaded Tuesday" label and older versions in a collapsed history strip below — Photos.app's edit-stack pattern, but I want it explicit that tapping an old version previews it read-only, it does not "restore" it as current (restoring would need to itself become a new version, appended, never a silent rewrite — otherwise we've broken the non-destructive guarantee we just spent three phases locking in).

Hard scope boundaries, restated from the design side rather than the feature-list side: no tab bar with more than the one navigation stack this loop needs; no settings screen beyond maybe an "About/limitations" static screen that honestly discloses the single-device data-loss risk (that's a UX obligation, not just an engineering footnote — the user should see it once, not discover it after losing their phone); no in-app onboarding wizard beyond the empty state itself doing the job.

Measurable success criteria I'd hold this to: a first-time user can go from cold launch to a created campaign with a brief in under 15 seconds and zero required fields beyond the free-text brief; every one of the four core screens demonstrably renders correct empty/loading/success/error states (this should be checkable by literally deleting all data and relaunching, and by force-killing mid-upload); a full campaign can go create → brief → upload v1 → upload v2 → review → rework with note → upload v3 → approve → view history, relaunch the app, and have every version and every timestamped transition still present, byte-identical; deleting an asset mid-review and deleting a zero-asset campaign both complete without crash and without leaving orphaned state (no asset pointing at a dead campaign, no campaign stuck in a status it can't be in). If any of those four can't be demonstrated, the app isn't done, regardless of how much of the feature list got implemented.

**Gemma3 (local) (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

Okay, let’s do this.

Original prompt: “Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: https://www.tryordinal.com”

My strongest first-pass contract centers around building a local-first iOS app – let’s call it “Flow” – that acts as a streamlined campaign production cockpit for a solo marketing professional. The goal isn’t to *become* Ordinal, which is a multi-tenant, API-heavy SaaS; instead, Flow is a focused tool that replicates Ordinal’s core workflow: briefing, asset management, approval gating, and basic campaign tracking – all within a single, offline iOS app.

Hard requirements:

1.  **Briefing Input:** The app *must* provide a robust mechanism for creating and organizing campaigns. This includes defining campaign goals (e.g., “drive website traffic,” “generate leads”), target audiences, and overarching brand tones. Think a flexible, free-form text field, but with the capability to attach metadata tags (e.g., “summer campaign,” “social media focus”).
2.  **Asset Library:** Users need a way to upload and manage campaign assets (images, videos, documents).  The app should natively support common image and video formats, and allow for basic organization via folders and tagging. We don’t need advanced editing tools, just a streamlined upload and retrieval system.
3.  **Review & Approval Workflow:**  A critical component. The app needs to facilitate a structured review process where assets can be assigned to reviewers (initially, just the user themselves) and approvals/rejections can be tracked with timestamps and notes.  We’re aiming for a simple, intuitive state machine: Draft → In Review → Approved / Needs Rework.
4.  **Offline Persistence:** Everything – campaigns, assets, review history – *must* be persistently stored locally on the device using SwiftData or Core Data.  This is non-negotiable for a focused, local-first workflow.  No syncing with any external platforms.
5. **Basic Campaign Timeline:** The app should display a clear timeline view showcasing the stages of a campaign’s progression (e.g., “Brief Created,” “Assets Uploaded,” “Review In Progress,” “Approved”). This helps users visualize and manage their campaigns.

Non-goals:

*   **Multi-User Collaboration:** Absolutely no support for teams or shared workflows. This adds significant complexity that’s outside the scope of this prototype.
*   **Third-Party Integrations:** No integration with any social media APIs, Adobe Creative Cloud, Slack, or CRM systems. We're focused on a self-contained workflow.
*   **Advanced Analytics:** No sophisticated analytics or reporting features. The timeline view will provide basic campaign progress tracking.
*   **Automated Engagement:**  No features for automatically posting content or engaging with users on social media.

Output Shape:

I envision this as a polished iOS app artifact with a clean, intuitive user interface and a well-documented architecture. It needs to be demonstrable and usable – a single, coherent application that demonstrates a functional, albeit simplified, version of Ordinal’s core workflow.

Production-Readiness Definition:

“Production-ready” for this phase means the app is functional, stable, and usable by a real user, even with limited functionality. It should be able to run smoothly without crashing, data should be reliably saved and retrieved, and the core workflow – creating a campaign, uploading assets, and getting approval – should work consistently. It’s not a perfect product; it’s a prototype. We need to confirm the core loop is reliable offline.

Decision Rules for Later Phases:

*   Any feature request that implies external authentication, backend synchronization, or platform API writes will be deferred until a separate phase focuses on these aspects.
*   Any design decision that compromises the local-first, offline-capable nature of the app will be flagged and reconsidered.
*   If ambiguity arises regarding a specific feature, we’ll establish a clear, documented decision rule to avoid future disagreements.

Biggest Risk/Objection:

The greatest risk here is over-scoping. Trying to replicate every aspect of Ordinal’s platform will inevitably lead to a complex, bloated, and ultimately unusable app. We need to remain laser-focused on delivering a streamlined, functional workflow and resist the temptation to add “nice-to-have” features.

title: Per App Product Brief
output:
**Round 1 recap**

Codex, Claude, Gemma3, and Qwen all landed on the same product shape — no real fork this round. Qwen's contribution is a verbatim restatement of the already-locked prompt contract, so it's not adding independent signal, just confirming nothing here contradicts earlier phases.

The real work came from Codex, Claude, and Gemma3, and they filled in complementary pieces rather than clashing:

- **Target user/use case**: unchanged — solo freelancer/agency operator managing a handful of campaigns in short bursts, needing one trustworthy place where "what's current" and "what's approved" never get lost.
- **Paid value**: Claude did the sharpest work here, catching a real trap before it became a build-time contradiction. The locked contract says "no billing/subscriptions" as a non-goal — but that non-goal means no server-side payment backend, not "no conceptual Free/Pro split." Claude's fix: define a real Free/Pro split functionally, but implement it (if at all) via on-device StoreKit or an explicitly-labeled simulated "Pro unlock" toggle — same pattern the project already used for "simulated publish." No custom billing server, ever. Codex's paid-value framing (avoiding costly mistakes, time saved) and Claude's concrete gate (Free = 2 campaigns + 5 versions visible, older versions hidden-not-deleted; Pro = unlimited + encrypted local backup/export) are consistent and nobody pushed back.
- **Non-negotiable rule everyone implicitly agrees with**: caps must hide, never delete, so monetization can't violate the already-locked non-destructive-versioning guarantee. Same for campaign caps on downgrade — never delete or lock existing campaigns, only block new creation.
- **Core loop**: identical to what's already locked (brief → version → review → approve/rework → history), and Claude explicitly adds that the review-decision screen must never get a paywall interstitial.
- **Competitive wedge**: convergent — the wedge is zero setup/zero login/nothing-can-leak-because-there's-no-server, framed directly against Ordinal being a team SaaS that assumes accounts and connectivity.
- **Growth angle**: convergent — not viral, niche word-of-mouth via freelancer/agency communities, with Claude adding the concrete mechanism (a presentable client-facing export/review packet acting as an organic "what tool is this" prompt).
- **Local-first/cloud-ready**: convergent — v1 is fully local (SwiftData + file-backed assets, zero network calls), and the cloud-ready path is user-controlled manual export/import (encrypted backup file to Files/iCloud Drive), explicitly not automatic sync — any future CloudKit sync would need its own explicit justification later, same bar as a third-party package.

Gemma3's brief mostly restates the same territory but introduces one thing nobody else endorsed: "future cloud path" framed as a roadmap commitment, and success metrics (15-minute campaign creation, retention rate) that are looser than the 15-second/under-15-second creation bar already locked in earlier phases. That's a minor drift, not a real disagreement — the group already has a tighter, more specific version of both ideas from earlier phases and from Claude this round.

Nothing here is actually contested. Everyone's reasoning is additive, not competing.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Target user and use case:** A solo marketing operator — freelancer, one-person in-house marketer, or agency contractor — managing 2–6 campaigns alone, working in short interstitial bursts (train, couch, between meetings). The app exists to be the single trustworthy place where "what's the brief," "what's the current asset version," and "what's approved" are always in sync, replacing a fragmented mess of Notes/email/chat/cloud folders.

**Paid value and subscription value (functional, not cosmetic):** Free and Pro run the identical core loop with identical reliability — money only touches two things. Free tier: capped at 2 active campaigns and visibility into the 5 most recent asset versions (older versions are hidden behind an "Upgrade to view full history" label, never deleted — the non-destructive versioning guarantee is never violated by monetization). Pro tier: removes both caps (unlimited campaigns, full version history) and adds encrypted local backup/export the user can save to Files/iCloud Drive/AirDrop and restore later — turning the app's own admitted single-device data-loss risk into the actual value a Pro user is buying. Since "billing/subscriptions" is locked as a non-goal (no backend), any purchase mechanism must be on-device StoreKit or an explicitly UI-labeled simulated "Pro unlock" toggle — never a server we run. The review-decision screen (Approve/Needs Rework) is never gated or interrupted by a paywall prompt.

**Core loop:** Create a campaign with a fast free-text brief (under 15 seconds) → upload an asset as v1 → append new versions non-destructively as work iterates → move the asset through Draft → In Review → Approved/Needs Rework (rework requires a non-empty note) → review the permanent, timestamped status history. Identical for Free and Pro, gated only by the caps above.

**Competitive wedge:** Ordinal is a team SaaS — accounts, login, connectivity, likely social/publishing automation. This app is the deliberate inverse: zero setup, zero login, works offline/on a plane, and — the sharpest pitch — a client's unreleased creative work literally cannot leak because there's no server to breach. That's a real value proposition for freelancers holding NDA'd pre-launch assets, not a workaround for missing a backend.

**Viral or niche growth angle:** Not viral — there's no second user to invite. Growth is niche word-of-mouth among freelancers and micro-agencies, driven by Pro users exporting a clean, presentable "campaign review package" (asset + version + approval status) to their own clients via Files/AirDrop/email. That artifact functions as an organic advertisement — someone on the receiving end asking "what tool is this" is the whole growth loop, so the export needs to look genuinely professional, not like a debug dump.

**Local-first and cloud-ready plan:** v1 ships fully local — SwiftData metadata, file-backed asset bytes, zero network calls of any kind, consistent with every earlier locked phase. The cloud-ready path is strictly user-controlled manual export/import of an encrypted backup file (Files/iCloud Drive) — no accounts, no server, no automatic sync, so it doesn't quietly reopen the multi-device-collaboration non-goal. Any future automatic CloudKit-style sync would need to be explicitly proposed and justified later, held to the same bar as introducing a third-party package.

# build-me-a-prototype-for-a-marketing-platform — Per App Product Brief

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-10 13:50:18._

## Original Prompt

```
Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: 

https://www.tryordinal.com
```

## Phase Purpose

Create a production-grade product brief for this specific app: target user, paid value, core loop, subscription value, competitive wedge, viral or niche growth mechanism, local-first behavior, cloud-ready extension path, and the real reason the app deserves to exist.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

A solo marketing operator needs one place on the phone where a campaign’s “what,” “what’s changed,” and “what’s approved” are always in sync, because that’s the exact set of things that breaks when they hop between Notes, email, chat threads, and cloud folders. This app is worth existing because it turns a fragmented local workflow into a single, durable decision system: one brief, one current versioned asset set, and an unambiguous local approval state that can’t be silently lost.

The target user is a freelancer, one-person in-house marketer, or agency contractor managing a handful of active campaigns, usually in short bursts (train, couch, hallway between calls), who needs reliable progress without setting up accounts, onboarding teammates, or logging into another web service. The use case is not “run a full social stack,” it is “reduce missed approvals and confusion about what version is current.”

The core loop is five concrete moves: create a campaign with a short free-form brief and optional metadata, attach/import an asset as version 1, iterate new versions that are always appended (never overwritten), move an asset through Draft → In Review → Approved/Needs Rework with required rework notes, then open the status log to verify every transition before the next action. Empty, loading, success, and error states are treated as first-class on every screen so a stalled import, failed write, or edge deletion always has a recovery path and never feels like a silent bug.

Paid value is real when the user avoids at least one costly failure mode each cycle: shipping the wrong asset, guessing which file is latest, or re-reading scattered messages to reconstruct history. In practice that is less “feature count” and more “time saved and fewer client mistakes,” which is exactly where solo operators pay. Subscription value in v1 planning is not cosmetic, it is utility: paid unlocks extra working bandwidth and reliability guarantees—larger campaign and version quotas, advanced local filters/search, stronger retention and archival rules for asset history, and a richer review queue (aging-in-review alerts, reusable briefs/templates, and compact handoff exports).

The competitive wedge is a narrow one: this is not a full SaaS clone; it is a disciplined, on-device workflow engine for solo operators who want the minimum overhead path to approved decisions. Compared with generic project tools, it wins on one thing: version + status certainty in a small footprint, with no login, no team choreography, and no drift between local decisions and the files actually on hand. Compared with photo/content apps, it wins by connecting each asset to campaign and review state instead of treating media as loose files.

A local-first behavior is the default contract: deterministic SwiftData metadata, file-backed media in app storage, strict status transitions, and a local timeline log as the source of truth. This means no network and no remote sharing are required to use the product; the downside is explicit: one-device data-loss risk if the device is lost. The cloud-ready path keeps that behavior intact and adds optional layers later, not at v1: encrypted backup and multi-device restore, then optional cross-device sync as a subscription feature.

The viral or niche growth angle is not social sharing by app mechanics; it is workflow portability. Users naturally share a complete “campaign review package” (approved asset + history export + rework trail) with clients and partners via Files/AirDrop/email. That makes the tool visible in existing handoffs and seeds word-of-mouth inside freelancer and micro-agency communities where this pain is common.

The highest objection to this direction is feature bloat: forcing a social or collaboration product onto a local review loop will reduce reliability and delay delivery. The detail this phase must preserve is the hard boundary that every versioning and state transition is append-only, single-device-usable, and edge-safe, especially around delete during review and orphan prevention.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

Here's my independent first pass, coming at this from the QA/risk seat: I want to name the failure modes in "paid value" before anyone locks a monetization story that either can't work under our own hard requirements or gets vetoed the moment someone checks it against the non-goals we already agreed to.

The target user is the same solo operator we've already locked — freelance marketer or one-person in-house/agency operator running 2-6 campaigns, reviewing on their phone in stolen moments. The use case that actually deserves money isn't "the app has more features," it's "this app is the only place my in-progress creative work lives, and I trust it not to lose it or leak it." That's the paid wedge: privacy and durability, not feature count.

Here's my problem with "subscription value" as a required output for this phase: our own locked contract lists "billing/subscriptions" as an explicit non-goal, meaning no server-side billing stack, no backend entitlement checks. That's correct and I don't want to reopen it. But this phase is asking for what a paid tier would functionally unlock, not asking us to build a live payment backend. Those are different questions and I want that distinction written into the brief explicitly, otherwise whoever builds next either silently ignores this phase's requirement or silently violates the locked non-goal — that's exactly the kind of undocumented judgment call this project keeps almost tripping on. My call: the brief defines a real Free/Pro split conceptually, and if a build phase wants to demo it, it uses on-device StoreKit (Apple's own purchase framework, not a server we run) or, more honestly for a prototype with no App Store account, a locally simulated "Pro unlock" toggle that is clearly labeled as simulated in the UI — same rule this project already applied to "simulated publish." No custom billing server, ever.

Now the actual gate, and I want to pick one instead of leaving it vague: Free tier caps you at 2 active campaigns and keeps only the 5 most recent versions per asset, silently retaining older versions but greying them out behind an "Upgrade to view full history" label rather than deleting them. Pro removes both caps — unlimited campaigns, full unbounded version history — and adds one genuinely useful thing free doesn't get: an encrypted local backup/restore file the user can export to Files/AirDrop/iCloud Drive themselves and restore from later. That last one is the real hook, because we already flagged in research that this app has zero sync and losing the phone means losing everything — Pro is the one that actually lets a user protect themselves against the risk we already admitted exists. That's functional value tied to a real, named risk, not a coat of paint on the same free experience.

I want to flag the trap before someone builds it: the version cap must never delete data a user already has — it hides, it doesn't destroy, because that would directly break the non-destructive versioning guarantee we spent three phases locking down. Same for campaign caps — if someone downgrades from Pro back to Free with 5 campaigns already existing, none of them get deleted or become uneditable; only new-campaign creation is blocked past the cap. Get that inverted and you've got a monetization mechanic that quietly violates a hard requirement, which is worse than not monetizing at all.

Core loop stays exactly what's locked: brief in under 15 seconds, upload and version assets, push through Draft → In Review → Approved/Rework, permanent status history. Nothing about paywall gating should add friction to that loop itself — the review-decision screen doesn't get a paywall interstitial, ever; that's the one screen this whole product exists for and it must never be blocked by a monetization prompt mid-decision.

Competitive wedge against Ordinal specifically: Ordinal is a team SaaS — it assumes multiple people, an account, a login, an internet connection, and (per the caveat we still haven't resolved) probably some social/publishing automation layer we've deliberately excluded. Our wedge is the inverse of every one of those: zero setup, zero login, works on a plane, and — this is the sharpest one — no client's unreleased creative ever leaves the user's device. For a freelancer holding pre-launch client assets under NDA, "this literally cannot leak because there's no server to breach" is a real, defensible pitch, not a consolation prize for lacking a backend.

Growth angle has to be niche, not viral, and I want to be honest that this app has no built-in network effect — there's no second user to invite. The one real mechanism is the client-facing export: when Pro users generate a clean, branded review packet (asset + version + approval status) to hand off to their own client, that artifact is effectively a business card for the app. Someone on the receiving end asking "what tool is this" is the only organic growth loop this shape of product can have, so the export needs to look genuinely presentable, not like a debug dump — that's a real design requirement this phase should hand forward, not an afterthought.

Local-first and cloud-ready: v1 ships fully local exactly as locked — SwiftData metadata, file-backed asset bytes, no network calls of any kind. The cloud-ready path I'd bless for later is user-controlled export/import via the Files app or iCloud Drive — the user manually puts an encrypted backup file wherever they want, and manually restores it, possibly on a new device. That's meaningfully different from "we build sync" — there's no account, no server, no automatic anything, so it doesn't quietly reintroduce the backend/multi-device-collaboration non-goal we already ruled out. If a future phase wants real CloudKit private-database sync, that has to be proposed and justified explicitly the same way a third-party package would be, not assumed.

The thing I don't want anyone to fudge later: this brief has to state plainly that "subscription" in this build means a locally-defined feature gate, not a functioning payment pipeline, and that the version/campaign caps are the only place money touches the product — everywhere else, Free and Pro run the identical core loop with identical reliability guarantees.

**Gemma3 (local) (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 1**

Okay, here's my first-pass product brief for this phase, as Gemma3 (Product Strategist · Visionary):

**Product Interpretation:**

A single native iOS app, inspired by Ordinal’s marketing-workflow shape—campaign/brief intake, asset upload and review, and approval gating—reimagined as a local-first, single-user tool rather than a literal clone of a multi-tenant web SaaS. This isn’t about replicating Ordinal’s UI or features, but capturing the *essence* of their core workflow—a streamlined way for a solo marketer to manage campaigns, assets, and approvals, all offline.

**Hard Requirements:**

1.  **Campaign Foundation:** The app *must* let users define a campaign, centered around a clear goal.  It needs a text field for the goal (e.g., “Generate leads for Q4,” “Launch summer collection”), and a way to attach descriptive tags (e.g., “social media campaign,” “email marketing,” “product launch”). This isn’t about sophisticated campaign *management*; it’s about quickly setting up the foundation for a single creative push.
2.  **Asset Capture & Review:** Users need to upload creative assets—images, short videos, PDFs—and track their progress through a simplified review process.  The UI must support grouping assets under the campaign, and versions should be handled non-destructively.
3.  **Approval Gateway:**  The app *must* provide a single-user, controlled approval/rework flow.  An asset can be marked “Approved” or “Needs Rework,” with an open text field for the “Needs Rework” note. Sending something back to “Needs Rework” requires that field to be non-empty—no bypassing that step.
4.  **Offline Persistence (Crucial):** Everything—campaign details, asset uploads, review statuses—*must* be stored locally, using SwiftData or Core Data. No backend, no cloud sync. It's a single-device tool.
5. **Status-History & Versioning:** The app must track every stage of an asset’s lifecycle – Draft -> In Review -> Approved/Rework – as a time-stamped status log, associated directly with the asset, rather than a separate user activity feed.  And when we add a new version of an asset, we *must* append it to the version history – never overwrite existing versions.

**Non-Goals (Absolutely Key):**

*   **Team Collaboration:** This is *not* a team tool.  It’s built for the solo operator.
*   **Social/Platform Integration:** No integrations with LinkedIn, Instagram, Facebook, Slack, or any other social media platform. This adds unnecessary complexity and opens a huge security and compliance risk.
*   **Automated Posting/Engagement:**  No automation of content posting or engagement. This is about creating assets and getting approvals—not about driving automated actions.
*   **Billing/Subscriptions/User Accounts:**  No subscriptions, no user accounts, no billing system. This is focused on a single-user workflow.

**Competitive Wedge (What Makes This Worthwhile):**

The key differentiator isn’t the *features* (many of those are standard); it’s the *friction reduction*.  Existing campaign tools are complex, multi-layered, and often require multiple apps. This app is laser-focused on getting a user from “I have an idea” to “approved asset” in the *fewest* steps possible—reducing the cognitive load and wasted time.

**Viral/Niche Growth (How It Spreads):**

This isn't about going viral. It's about word-of-mouth within a niche community of independent marketers and small agency owners.  The value is in the focused workflow and the rapid campaign creation, making it something people will naturally recommend to others in a similar situation.

**Local-First & Cloud-Ready (The Strategic Layer):**

While the app is *initially* local-first, we’re building in a “future cloud path” – a well-defined, documented strategy for how to migrate to a cloud-based solution *if* it's ever needed. This provides a roadmap for future development and avoids technological debt.

**Decision Rules (For the Next Phase – Critical):**

*   **Versioning is Non-Negotiable:**  We *must* implement non-destructive versioning.  Any deviation from this will be a showstopper.
*   **Re-Fetch TryOrdinal.com:**  The next phase *must* attempt to re-fetch the TryOrdinal.com site and/or their documentation. If it fails, we fall back to secondary sources.  If the live site differs significantly, we must document that difference explicitly.
*   **“Deferred/Unverified” Bucket:** If a feature request can’t be traced to a specific need (e.g., a fancy reporting dashboard), it goes into a “deferred/unverified” bucket—we don’t build it.

**Production Readiness (Beyond Just “Working”):**

This app needs to be *reliable*.  It must handle errors gracefully, recover from data loss, and behave predictably. It's not about fancy animations or polished UX—it's about delivering a solid, functional tool that the user can depend on.

**Metrics of Success:**

*   Campaign creation time: Under 15 minutes, on average.
*   Average asset version count: Tracked (to identify potential bottlenecks).
*   User retention rate:  A key measure of whether the app is solving a genuine need.

This, frankly, is a simple product—but that’s the point. It's a tool that solves a very real problem for a specific audience.  Let’s build that tool.

---

Okay – I'm ready for the next round.

===== THIS PHASE'S DISCUSSION SO FAR =====
(You are the first speaker.)


===== PHASE PLAYBOOK (editable phase_rules.json) =====
Global app-build rules:
- Prefer a smaller finished app over a larger half-built app.
- Every generated app must have real user-visible behavior, not only placeholder screens.
- For iOS apps, default to SwiftUI, deterministic local state first, Apple system frameworks, and no third-party package unless it materially reduces risk.
- Every screen needs empty, loading, success, and error states when the workflow can reach those states.
- Treat accessibility, privacy, persistence, and testing as product requirements, not polish.
- Do not declare the app done until build verification and the final review agree on what works and what remains limited.

Phase rules:
- Write a concrete brief for this specific app, not a generic category description.
- Explain why users would pay and what the subscription unlocks as real utility.
- Include local-first behavior and a future cloud path without making cloud a dependency for v1.

Required output:
- Target user and use case.
- Paid value and subscription value.
- Core loop.
- Competitive wedge.
- Viral or niche growth angle.
- Local-first and cloud-ready plan.

Acceptance checks before consensus:
- The app’s value is specific enough to build.
- Subscription value is functional, not cosmetic.
- The brief can drive design without more ideation.

===== YOUR TURN — this is Gemma3 (local) talking (round 1) =====

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 1**

**Original Prompt:**

Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: [https://www.tryordinal.com](https://www.tryordinal.com)

**Product Interpretation:**

A single native iOS app, inspired by Ordinal's marketing-workflow shape — campaign/brief intake, asset upload and review, and approval gating — reimagined as a local-first, single-user tool rather than a literal clone of a multi-tenant web SaaS.

**Hard Requirements:**

1. One SwiftUI iOS app, single-user, fully offline, deterministic local persistence (SwiftData/Core Data or equivalent) — no backend, no login system.
2. Campaign and Asset as first-class persisted objects.
3. A brief-intake screen (goals/audience/tone/platform context, free-text is fine).
4. Asset upload and organization, grouped under a Campaign/Project.
5. Asset versioning that's non-destructive: new versions append to a history list, the current version is always clearly marked, nothing gets silently overwritten.
6. A review/approval flow with an explicit state machine: Draft → In Review → Approved / Rework, where sending something back to Rework requires a non-empty note.
7. A timestamped status-history log per campaign/asset — this is a single-actor audit trail (there's only ever one user), not a multi-person activity feed.
8. Full CRUD with safe/undoable deletion, including edge cases like deleting an asset mid-review or a campaign with zero assets.
9. Every screen needs real empty, loading, success, and error states — no placeholders, no stub screens.
10. Accessibility (labels, dynamic type, contrast, focus order) and privacy defaults (no background collection, no remote data sharing) from day one.
11. Data must survive app relaunch reliably.

**Non-Goals:**

1. Multi-user collaboration, team accounts, roles/RBAC.
2. Any real integration with external platforms — social media APIs/OAuth, Slack, Adobe, ad platforms, CRMs, webhooks/MCP.
3. Any real posting or engagement automation against live accounts (this is a genuine safety/ToS risk, not just a scope call — nothing in this app touches real credentials or real platform APIs).
4. Billing/subscriptions, multi-tenant workspaces, server-side job queues.
5. Any web-parity claim against tryordinal.com's actual site.
6. NFT, wallet, or Bitcoin-Ordinals-related functionality of any kind — dead, do not resurrect.
7. Third-party packages by default. One may be introduced later only if Apple's own frameworks genuinely can't do the job, and it must be justified explicitly in-contract when proposed.

**Production-readiness definition:**

Not a mockup or wireframe shell — a complete, working loop where a user can create a campaign, submit assets, iterate on feedback, approve/reject items, and reopen/rework without crashes or data-loss, while every flow communicates state clearly. It’s ready when the onboarding-to-delivery loop is reliable offline, states are testable manually at happy/edge/error paths, data is persisted across launches, and there are explicit documented limitations aligned with the local-first + no-third-party-default rule.

 **Decision Rules for Later Phases:**

1. Any feature request that implies external authentication, backend synchronization, or platform API writes will be deferred until a separate phase focuses on these aspects.
2. Any design decision that compromises the local-first, offline-capable nature of the app will be flagged and reconsidered.
3. If ambiguity arises regarding a specific feature, we’ll establish a clear, documented decision rule to avoid future disagreements.

**Biggest Risk/Objection:**

The greatest risk here is over-scoping. Trying to replicate every aspect of Ordinal’s platform will inevitably lead to a complex, bloated, and ultimately unusable app. We need to remain laser-focused on delivering a streamlined, functional workflow and resist the temptation to add “nice-to-have” features.

**Coordinator Decision:**

See the coordinator's message above.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

**Round 1 recap**

Codex, Claude, Gemma3, and Qwen all landed on the same product shape — no real fork this round. Qwen's contribution is a verbatim restatement of the already-locked prompt contract, so it's not adding independent signal, just confirming nothing here contradicts earlier phases.

The real work came from Codex, Claude, and Gemma3, and they filled in complementary pieces rather than clashing:

- **Target user/use case**: unchanged — solo freelancer/agency operator managing a handful of campaigns in short bursts, needing one trustworthy place where "what's current" and "what's approved" never get lost.
- **Paid value**: Claude did the sharpest work here, catching a real trap before it became a build-time contradiction. The locked contract says "no billing/subscriptions" as a non-goal — but that non-goal means no server-side payment backend, not "no conceptual Free/Pro split." Claude's fix: define a real Free/Pro split functionally, but implement it (if at all) via on-device StoreKit or an explicitly-labeled simulated "Pro unlock" toggle — same pattern the project already used for "simulated publish." No custom billing server, ever. Codex's paid-value framing (avoiding costly mistakes, time saved) and Claude's concrete gate (Free = 2 campaigns + 5 versions visible, older versions hidden-not-deleted; Pro = unlimited + encrypted local backup/export) are consistent and nobody pushed back.
- **Non-negotiable rule everyone implicitly agrees with**: caps must hide, never delete, so monetization can't violate the already-locked non-destructive-versioning guarantee. Same for campaign caps on downgrade — never delete or lock existing campaigns, only block new creation.
- **Core loop**: identical to what's already locked (brief → version → review → approve/rework → history), and Claude explicitly adds that the review-decision screen must never get a paywall interstitial.
- **Competitive wedge**: convergent — the wedge is zero setup/zero login/nothing-can-leak-because-there's-no-server, framed directly against Ordinal being a team SaaS that assumes accounts and connectivity.
- **Growth angle**: convergent — not viral, niche word-of-mouth via freelancer/agency communities, with Claude adding the concrete mechanism (a presentable client-facing export/review packet acting as an organic "what tool is this" prompt).
- **Local-first/cloud-ready**: convergent — v1 is fully local (SwiftData + file-backed assets, zero network calls), and the cloud-ready path is user-controlled manual export/import (encrypted backup file to Files/iCloud Drive), explicitly not automatic sync — any future CloudKit sync would need its own explicit justification later, same bar as a third-party package.

Gemma3's brief mostly restates the same territory but introduces one thing nobody else endorsed: "future cloud path" framed as a roadmap commitment, and success metrics (15-minute campaign creation, retention rate) that are looser than the 15-second/under-15-second creation bar already locked in earlier phases. That's a minor drift, not a real disagreement — the group already has a tighter, more specific version of both ideas from earlier phases and from Claude this round.

Nothing here is actually contested. Everyone's reasoning is additive, not competing.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Target user and use case:** A solo marketing operator — freelancer, one-person in-house marketer, or agency contractor — managing 2–6 campaigns alone, working in short interstitial bursts (train, couch, between meetings). The app exists to be the single trustworthy place where "what's the brief," "what's the current asset version," and "what's approved" are always in sync, replacing a fragmented mess of Notes/email/chat/cloud folders.

**Paid value and subscription value (functional, not cosmetic):** Free and Pro run the identical core loop with identical reliability — money only touches two things. Free tier: capped at 2 active campaigns and visibility into the 5 most recent asset versions (older versions are hidden behind an "Upgrade to view full history" label, never deleted — the non-destructive versioning guarantee is never violated by monetization). Pro tier: removes both caps (unlimited campaigns, full version history) and adds encrypted local backup/export the user can save to Files/iCloud Drive/AirDrop and restore later — turning the app's own admitted single-device data-loss risk into the actual value a Pro user is buying. Since "billing/subscriptions" is locked as a non-goal (no backend), any purchase mechanism must be on-device StoreKit or an explicitly UI-labeled simulated "Pro unlock" toggle — never a server we run. The review-decision screen (Approve/Needs Rework) is never gated or interrupted by a paywall prompt.

**Core loop:** Create a campaign with a fast free-text brief (under 15 seconds) → upload an asset as v1 → append new versions non-destructively as work iterates → move the asset through Draft → In Review → Approved/Needs Rework (rework requires a non-empty note) → review the permanent, timestamped status history. Identical for Free and Pro, gated only by the caps above.

**Competitive wedge:** Ordinal is a team SaaS — accounts, login, connectivity, likely social/publishing automation. This app is the deliberate inverse: zero setup, zero login, works offline/on a plane, and — the sharpest pitch — a client's unreleased creative work literally cannot leak because there's no server to breach. That's a real value proposition for freelancers holding NDA'd pre-launch assets, not a workaround for missing a backend.

**Viral or niche growth angle:** Not viral — there's no second user to invite. Growth is niche word-of-mouth among freelancers and micro-agencies, driven by Pro users exporting a clean, presentable "campaign review package" (asset + version + approval status) to their own clients via Files/AirDrop/email. That artifact functions as an organic advertisement — someone on the receiving end asking "what tool is this" is the whole growth loop, so the export needs to look genuinely professional, not like a debug dump.

**Local-first and cloud-ready plan:** v1 ships fully local — SwiftData metadata, file-backed asset bytes, zero network calls of any kind, consistent with every earlier locked phase. The cloud-ready path is strictly user-controlled manual export/import of an encrypted backup file (Files/iCloud Drive) — no accounts, no server, no automatic sync, so it doesn't quietly reopen the multi-device-collaboration non-goal. Any future automatic CloudKit-style sync would need to be explicitly proposed and justified later, held to the same bar as introducing a third-party package.

It satisfies all three acceptance checks: the value is specific enough to build (exact caps, exact gate mechanism, exact StoreKit-vs-simulated-toggle rule), subscription value is functional not cosmetic (unlocks real capability — full history + backup — not a skin), and the brief is detailed enough to drive design without further ideation.

It also correctly handles the one real risk this phase carried — reconciling "no billing/subscriptions" as a locked non-goal with this phase's requirement to define subscription value — by drawing the right line (conceptual tier definition and on-device/simulated purchase mechanism are fine; a server-run billing backend is not) and explicitly protects two previously-locked guarantees (non-destructive versioning caps hide-not-delete; downgrade never deletes/locks existing campaigns). Gemma3's looser success metrics and "future cloud path as roadmap" framing are correctly identified as minor, non-contradictory drift rather than a real disagreement, and the coordinator didn't paper over it — it named the discrepancy and explained why the sharper version from Claude/earlier phases wins.

No repairs needed — this is a usable artifact for the next phase.

title: Next Steps Small
output:
Everyone converged on the same shape, with genuine sharpening rather than conflict.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

The room agrees the MVP is one complete, end-to-end loop for a single campaign and a single asset — not four partially-built screens. Concretely: launch the app, create a campaign with just a one-line brief, import one photo/video as v1, preview it in QuickLook, submit for review, get sent to rework with a required note, upload v2, submit again, approve it with a deliberate confirm-tap, and see every one of those steps sitting in a real, timestamped status history — all of it surviving a force-quit and relaunch.

Codex and Claude did the heavy lifting and ended up in the same place from different angles: Codex nailed the mechanical guardrails (append-only versioning, auto-revert-to-Draft-with-log-line if the only in-review asset gets deleted, a 10-second undo window on destructive actions), while Claude pushed on making sure this doesn't ship as a flat, checkbox-complete-but-lifeless demo — the review-decision screen and the status-history screen are the emotional core of the product and need to actually feel weighty (unmistakable version badges, a real confirm step for Approve, a status history that reads like a story). Nobody disagreed with either framing; they're additive.

Gemma3 pushed for an even thinner slice (drop version history entirely, just "approve" a single upload) — that's a real, if minor, disagreement with the rest of the room, since Codex/Claude/Qwen all treat the rework→v2→approve cycle as non-negotiable for proving the concept (a single-tap-approve with no version history doesn't actually validate the thing this app exists to prove). Qwen's contribution is a verbatim restatement of the locked contract, so it doesn't add or contest anything.

**MVP slice:** Campaign List → Campaign Detail → Asset Detail → Status History, but validated through exactly one campaign and one asset taken through the full cycle: brief → v1 → submit → rework (with note) → v2 → submit → approve → history. A list screen with a single item still counts as done.

**Must-have interactions:** one-tap "new campaign" from an empty state; single required free-text brief field (audience/tone/platform exist as unused schema, not rendered UI); asset import via Photos/Files picker; QuickLook preview of the current version; non-destructive version bump (old versions never overwritten, current version clearly marked); Draft → In Review → Approved/Needs Rework with asymmetric friction (Approve requires tap-and-confirm, Rework is a single tap but blocks on an empty note); a status-history log showing both user decisions and system-generated lines (e.g., the auto-revert-to-Draft case); deleting the only in-review asset auto-reverts its campaign to Draft with a logged line; every destructive action gets a ~10-second undo.

**Cut list (deferred, not wrong):** Free/Pro tiering and any purchase UI, encrypted backup/export, local notifications, multiple concurrent campaigns as a validation requirement, tags/search/filtering, campaign-type dropdowns, the About/limitations screen (nice to have, not load-bearing), any dashboard/timeline/kanban view beyond the four core screens.

**Validation criteria:** cold launch to a created campaign with a brief in well under 15 seconds; the full v1→rework→v2→approve cycle survives a force-quit/relaunch with every version and timestamped transition intact and correctly ordered; Approve cannot be fat-fingered (confirm-tap required) and Rework cannot submit with an empty note; deleting the in-review asset mid-flow reverts the campaign to Draft without crashing or orphaning state, and is undoable within the window; every one of the four screens shows a real (non-stub) empty/loading/error state, provable by wiping the data store and by killing the app mid-upload.
