# build-me-a-prototype-for-a-marketing-platform — Next Steps Small

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-10 13:54:37._

## Original Prompt

```
Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: 

https://www.tryordinal.com
```

## Phase Purpose

Define the smallest valuable slice / immediate next steps that de-risk and validate the concept with minimal effort.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

What I’d build next is one complete loop: create a campaign, attach one asset as version 1, move it through review, send it back for rework with a required note, attach version 2, then approve it and end on a visible status log entry. That is the smallest slice that proves whether this product actually solves the problem.

MVP slice: I want a single, constrained flow in one campaign list → campaign detail → asset detail flow with only one required campaign field (`brief`) and one required asset import action. The hard decisions are now fixed: versioning is append-only (`v1`, `v2` etc.), current version is always marked, and deleting the only in-review asset auto-reverts its campaign to Draft and emits a system log line (“asset removed, reverted to Draft”); no exceptions for this phase. Every destructive action must be undoable with an immediate, 10-second in-app undo option.

Must-have interactions: campaign list empty state with one-tap “New campaign” and success/error fallback if persistence fails; campaign creation with a minimal required text field only; share sheet or photo/import picker path for one file; automatic metadata + local file-write pathing with loading state; asset row in campaign detail with latest version badge; asset detail with QuickLook preview of current version, `Submit for review`, `Needs rework` (requires non-empty note), and `Approve` with confirm; version bump by importing a replacement as a new row in history; a status-history section that includes user decisions and system transitions like auto-revert.

Must-have cut list: no third-party frameworks, no paywall/sign-up, no multi-device sync, no timeline/kanban board, no analytics, no notifications, no tags/templates, no trash bin screen, no client/export flow, no extra campaign metadata fields required, and no background processors. Keep focus on one reliable workflow only.

Validation criteria: cold launch to first approved workflow must work end to end, including failure-recovery paths, using one fake client-like set (e.g., create campaign, import image/video, submit, rework with note, import second version, approve) and persist after kill/relaunch with full log+versions+status intact; campaign with only one asset moved to In Review and then deleted must auto-revert to Draft and restore cleanly if undo is selected within 10 seconds; each screen has deterministic empty/loading/success/error UI states; unsupported import type or permission denial produces a recoverable error path, not a crash. Biggest risk I’m rejecting is adding breadth now (templates, notifications, tiering, sharing/export) because it hides whether the core review-vs-rework decision loop is stable; we must prove this loop first, then expand.

**Claude (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 1**

Here's my independent take on the smallest slice that actually proves this thing works, and I want to push back a little on the instinct to slice by screen count rather than by emotional payoff.

The MVP slice isn't "four screens partially working" — it's one campaign, one asset, taken all the way from a blank app to an approved decision with a real version history behind it. Concretely: cold launch to an empty state that gets you to a created campaign in one tap, type a one-line brief, import a single photo or video as v1 via the share sheet or photo picker, look at it in QuickLook, submit it for review, get a rework note, upload v2, submit again, approve it with the deliberate tap-and-confirm, and then look at the status history and see every one of those steps sitting there with real timestamps, in order, surviving a force-quit and relaunch. If that loop doesn't feel satisfying — if approving something doesn't feel like a real decision, if seeing "v2 of 2, uploaded just now" over the rework note that produced it doesn't feel like the app understood what happened — the slice has failed even if every checkbox in the contract is technically implemented. I'd rather ship that one loop, gorgeous and bulletproof, than four screens that all sort of work.

The must-have interactions are narrower than the full feature list we've locked over the last five phases, and I think that's the right instinct for this phase specifically: create campaign (one free-text field, nothing else required), add an asset from Photos/Files, view it in QuickLook, append a new version without losing the old one, move Draft → In Review → Approved/Rework with the asymmetric friction we already agreed on (confirm-tap to approve, single-tap-but-mandatory-note to rework), and read the status history. That's it. Everything else the earlier phases discussed — multiple campaigns with caps, the Free/Pro gate, encrypted backup/export, the About/limitations screen, local notifications for stale reviews, tags/audience/tone metadata — is real product surface for later, not part of proving the concept.

Here's my cut list, and I want to be explicit that "cut" means "deferred," not "wrong to have designed": the Free/Pro tiering and any StoreKit or simulated-purchase UI — that's monetization on top of a loop that doesn't exist yet, and building it now risks the classic trap of a beautifully gated door with an empty room behind it. Encrypted backup/export to Files/iCloud — real value, zero relevance to proving the core loop feels good. Local stale-review notifications — a delight feature for a user who already trusts the app, not a day-one need. Multiple simultaneous campaigns — the object model should support it, but the MVP validation only needs to nail one campaign end-to-end; a list screen with one item in it still counts. Audience/tone/platform metadata fields on the brief — collapsed and optional per the earlier product brief, and for this slice I'd argue they don't even need to render, just exist as unused schema fields, because every one of them is surface area that can go wrong for zero proof-of-concept value. Tags and campaign search/filtering — pure nice-to-have. The About/limitations disclosure screen is small enough that I wouldn't fight hard to cut it, but it's not load-bearing for validating the concept either.

Where I want to be the visionary voice for a second: the temptation with a "smallest slice" framing is to make it feel like a checklist demo — create, upload, tap approve, done, technically correct, emotionally flat. I don't think that's good enough. The thing that would make this genuinely remarkable even at minimum scope is that the review-decision moment has to feel weighty — a full-bleed QuickLook preview, a version badge that's unmistakable, a confirm step for Approve that feels like signing off on something real rather than dismissing an alert. And the status history screen, even with just one campaign and one asset in it, should read like a story you'd be a little proud to show a client — "Draft → In Review → Needs Rework: 'logo is cut off on the right' → In Review → Approved" with real timestamps — because that's the whole pitch of this app in one screen. If the MVP nails that feeling, we've proven something worth building on. If it doesn't, no amount of additional screens fixes it.

Validation criteria, concretely and checkably: a brand-new install goes from first launch to a campaign with a brief in well under 15 seconds; a user can take one asset through v1 → rework-with-note → v2 → approved and have every version and every status transition still present, correctly ordered, and byte-identical after a force-quit and relaunch; the review-decision screen cannot be fat-fingered into an accidental approval (confirm-tap is non-negotiable) and cannot accept a rework with an empty note; deleting the in-review asset mid-flow correctly reverts the campaign to Draft with a logged system line rather than crashing or orphaning state; and every screen touched by this slice — even just these four — shows a real, non-stub empty/loading/error state, provable by wiping the data store and by killing the app mid-upload. If those five things are true, we've proven the concept. If any one of them is fake or stubbed, we haven't, regardless of how many other features got bolted on around it.

**Gemma3 (local) (Delivery Lead · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Okay, here’s my independent first-pass contribution for this phase:

I'm going to focus on delivering a *minimum viable product*—a single, self-contained workflow that proves the core concept of the app, and that’s the absolute smallest thing that can achieve that. Let's build the “Create Campaign > Upload Asset > Review & Approve” loop, and *nothing else*.

Specifically, the app needs to let a user:

1.  Create a campaign – a text field for the name, and a simple dropdown for selecting a basic “campaign type” (e.g., “Social Media,” “Email,” “Product Launch”).
2.  Upload a single image asset – a straightforward drag-and-drop interface to select a file from their photo library. The app must then save this image locally to the app’s storage, automatically naming it based on the campaign name and version number (e.g., “CampaignName_v1.jpg”).
3.  Mark the asset as “Approved” with a single tap, triggering a status update and timestamped entry in the status log.

The “Review & Approve” stage is deliberately stripped down. The core requirement is simply that the user can mark the asset as approved *without* needing to juggle multiple screens or complex workflows.

I'm strongly pushing against adding anything beyond this core loop in this phase. Resist the temptation to add “advanced” features like version history views, file tagging, or complex review workflows. Those are all *future* enhancements – not part of the MVP. The most important thing to preserve is that the app is *functional* and *reliable* – that it can reliably save a campaign, upload an asset, and mark it as approved, with a deterministic local state that we can actually build upon in later phases.

The biggest risk here is that we’ll get bogged down in debating the “perfect” UI or trying to build in too much upfront. We need to prioritize *delivering something*, not building the *ideal* solution. That's why I’m advocating for a deliberately minimalist design – it's faster to build, less prone to bugs, and easier to iterate on later.

Finally, the output of this phase must explicitly acknowledge the trade-offs we're making. It needs to document the constraints of the local-first approach—the single-device limitation, the manual export/import workflow—and clearly outline the future direction for cloud-ready enhancements.  A brief, but prominent, note on the risk of data loss if the user's phone is lost or damaged is *essential*.  Don’t gloss over it – this is a key product constraint that users *must* understand.

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

**Original Prompt:**

Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: [https://www.tryordinal.com](https://www.tryordinal.com)

**Product Interpretation:**

A single native iOS app, inspired by Ordinal's marketing-workflow shape — campaign/brief intake, asset upload and review, and approval gating — reimagined as a local-first, single-user tool rather than a literal clone of a multi-tenant web SaaS.

**Hard Requirements:**

1. One SwiftUI iOS app, single-user, fully offline, deterministic local persistence (SwiftData/Core Data or equivalent) — no backend, no login system.
2. Campaign and Asset as first-class persisted objects.
3. A brief-intake screen (goals/audience/tone/platform context, free-text is fine).
4. Asset upload and organization, grouped under a Campaign/Project.
5. Asset versioning that's non-destructive: new versions append to a history list, the current version is always clearly marked, nothing gets silently overwritten.
6. A review/approval flow with an explicit state machine: Draft → In Review → Approved / Rework, where sending something back to Rework requires a non-empty note.
7. A timestamped status-history log per campaign/asset — this is a single-actor audit trail (there's only ever one user), not a multi-person activity feed.
8. Full CRUD with safe/undoable deletion, including edge cases like deleting an asset mid-review or a campaign with zero assets.
9. Every screen needs real empty, loading, success, and error states — no placeholders, no stub screens.
10. Accessibility (labels, dynamic type, contrast, focus order) and privacy defaults (no background collection, no remote data sharing) from day one.
11. Data must survive app relaunch reliably.

**Non-Goals:**

1. Multi-user collaboration, team accounts, roles/RBAC.
2. Any real integration with external platforms — social media APIs/OAuth, Slack, Adobe, ad platforms, CRMs, webhooks/MCP.
3. Any real posting or engagement automation against live accounts (this is a genuine safety/ToS risk, not just a scope call — nothing in this app touches real credentials or real platform APIs).
4. Billing/subscriptions, multi-tenant workspaces, server-side job queues.
5. Any web-parity claim against tryordinal.com's actual site.
6. NFT, wallet, or Bitcoin-Ordinals-related functionality of any kind — dead, do not resurrect.
7. Third-party packages by default. One may be introduced later only if Apple's own frameworks genuinely can't do the job, and it must be justified explicitly in-contract when proposed.

**Production-readiness definition:**

Not a mockup or wireframe shell — a complete, working loop where a user can create a campaign, submit assets, iterate on feedback, approve/reject items, and reopen/rework without crashes or data-loss, while every flow communicates state clearly. It’s ready when the onboarding-to-delivery loop is reliable offline, states are testable manually at happy/edge/error paths, data is persisted across launches, and there are explicit documented limitations aligned with the local-first + no-third-party-default rule.

**Decision Rules for Later Phases:**

1. Any feature request that implies external authentication, backend synchronization, or platform API writes will be deferred until a separate phase focuses on these aspects.
2. Any design decision that compromises the local-first, offline-capable nature of the app will be flagged and reconsidered.
3. If ambiguity arises regarding a specific feature, we’ll establish a clear, documented decision rule to avoid future disagreements.

**Biggest Risk/Objection:**

The greatest risk here is over-scoping. Trying to replicate every aspect of Ordinal’s platform will inevitably lead to a complex, bloated, and ultimately unusable app. We need to remain laser-focused on delivering a streamlined, functional workflow and resist the temptation to add “nice-to-have” features.

**Coordinator Decision:**

See the coordinator's message above.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Everyone converged on the same shape, with genuine sharpening rather than conflict.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

The room agrees the MVP is one complete, end-to-end loop for a single campaign and a single asset — not four partially-built screens. title: Detailed Discussion
output:
CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

Everyone's on the same page here — Codex, Claude, and Gemma3 all reinforce rather than contradict each other, and this rounds out the ambiguity this phase exists to kill. (Qwen didn't respond this round — no new voice, no contradiction either.)

**Resolved requirements:** The domain is anchored on two invariants that must never be violated by anything built later — a Campaign is always the source-of-truth container, and each Asset has append-only versions with exactly one current pointer. Brief intake is one required free-text field, everything else optional. States are strictly Draft → In Review → Approved / Needs Rework, Rework blocked on an empty *or whitespace-only* note (Claude's catch — trim before validating). Deletion is only ever at the whole-asset level — individual versions can never be independently deleted, since that would break the append-only guarantee everyone locked back in round one. Undo is implemented as a **soft delete**: the item moves to a trash location immediately and a status flag flips, the 10-second toast is purely a UI countdown, and a background sweep on next launch finalizes anything past its window — this avoids the real bug Claude flagged, where a deferred/timer-based delete could race an app kill and either resurrect or prematurely finalize.

**Edge cases enumerated (build-time, not QA-afterthought):** empty campaign (brief-only, zero assets); unsupported/corrupt file import (zero-byte, unreadable video, unrenderable HEIC variant); Photos/Files permission denial (must produce a real recoverable UI state with a Settings deep link, never a silent no-op or crash); disk write failure mid-import; app killed mid-upload or mid-undo-window; deleting the only in-review asset → auto-revert campaign to Draft with a logged system line; rapid successive version uploads causing interrupted writes; broken file URL after an OS migration; device clock changes potentially reordering timestamped history (accepted as a named limitation, not something to engineer around).

**Data and privacy implications:** Asset bytes are file-backed (Documents/Application Support), never embedded blobs; SwiftData holds only metadata + URL references. No network calls, no background collection, permission prompts are strictly request-on-use. Standard iCloud *device* backup (Apple's own OS-level backup, not app-built sync) is allowed and disclosed rather than fought — excluding a user's own creative work from their own phone backup would arguably hurt them more — but this must be a named, visible decision (surfaced in the About/limitations screen), not a silent default. Version history storage is unbounded from day one at the data layer regardless of what UI caps get added later, so the deferred Free/Pro tiering never requires a retrofit that discards previously-kept history.

**Risk register (with mitigations or named-as-limitation status):**
- Ordinal parity is still unverified — carried forward again, still blocking full "matches Ordinal" claims; mitigation is the standing re-fetch rule already in the contract.
- Unbounded version-storage growth — accepted v1 limitation, not solved.
- Single-device-only persistence — real, disclosed data-loss risk if the phone is lost, surfaced to the user, not just documented internally.
- File lifecycle/corruption risk — mitigated by atomic writes plus existence/validity checks before use.
- Clock-integrity risk to the status-history log — accepted and named, not engineered around, since it's rare and full clock-tamper defense is out of scope.
- Scope-creep back toward collaboration/network assumptions — mitigated by treating the two domain invariants (campaign-as-container, append-only versions) as non-negotiable gates on any future feature.

**Final assumptions carried forward:** single-user is the only valid mode for v1; approval is logged and traceable but the underlying action set stays reversible via soft-delete/undo within the window — status transitions themselves are never rewritten, only appended to; the MVP's definition of success is unchanged from the prior phase (one full v1→rework→v2→approve cycle surviving relaunch with intact history); and everything Gemma3 raised this round (single free-text campaign field, local SwiftData persistence, no team/external/automation features) is consistent with, not a new addition to, what's already locked — it doesn't introduce new scope, just re-confirms it.

No open ambiguity remains that would block design or architecture from proceeding.

# build-me-a-prototype-for-a-marketing-platform — Detailed Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-10 13:58:35._

## Original Prompt

```
Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: 

https://www.tryordinal.com
```

## Phase Purpose

Deeply analyze requirements, edge cases, constraints, assumptions, risks, and open questions, resolving ambiguity left from earlier phases.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 1**

The build should lock onto one promise: it must make “what is current, what is approved, what changed” provably correct for a solo marketer between interruptions. The best version of this app is not a broad dashboard; it is a tight, deterministic local review cockpit where every campaign move can be trusted after relaunch, even after crashes.

I think this phase should resolve all ambiguity by treating the domain as two immutable truths: a campaign is always the truth container, and each asset has append-only versions with one explicit current pointer. Everything else (queues, notifications, caps, exports, tiering) can exist later, but never at the cost of these two truths.

The requirements I would lock now are: SwiftUI iOS-first, single user, no backend, no login, no team roles, and deterministic persistence with SwiftData/Core Data plus file-backed asset payloads; campaign and asset are first-class local objects; the brief includes one required free-text lane with optional metadata allowed but not required; states are limited to Draft → In Review → Approved / Needs Rework, with Rework requiring a non-empty note; every state change and system mutation is append-only in a single local history log; full CRUD includes safe deletion and undo with recovery for destructive actions; and every workflow state must have real empty/loading/success/error UI behavior. The hard boundary is that campaign review cannot be blocked by paywalls or external calls because the core value is trust in local completion. Accessibility, permissions, and data-loss behavior are in-scope, not polish.

The edge cases I would bake into design now, not QA afterthoughts, are: creating a campaign with only brief text; importing an unsupported file; Photos/Files permission denied; disk write failure; campaign with zero assets; deleting an in-review only asset and automatically reverting campaign status to Draft with a logged line; deleting or undoing that deletion; version upload in rapid succession causing interrupted writes; relaunch during import; loading a broken file URL after migration; old versions accumulating and one of them becoming orphaned; and showing history when versions exist for multiple assets under one campaign. I also want explicit behavior for zero and non-zero asset campaigns in all status screens, so “no assets yet” and “no history yet” never map to the same blank view.

Data and privacy implications should be explicit and visible in behavior: media is written to app storage only, not embedded in the object store; no automatic sync, background harvest, or network upload; privacy defaults are opt-out by design; any permission prompts (Photos, Files, camera) are strictly request-on-use; and one-device persistence is a conscious limitation that must be surfaced to users before trust can be complete. If backup/export is added later, it must be explicit, user-triggered, and local.

The risk register I would carry forward is: unknown parity with tryordinal because source confirmation remains incomplete, so our contract is a behavior-led interpretation and must stay in “inferred-but-disciplined” mode until re-fetch is done; versioning storage growth is unbounded in v1, so we accept controlled risk with visible policy notes; file lifecycle corruption can break history integrity unless we normalize atomic writes and validation; the “single-device only” risk is real and can look like data-loss even when product logic is correct; and the biggest product risk is scope creep back toward collaboration/automation that would smuggle in network assumptions. The mitigations are all narrow: constrain phase scope, enforce state-machine invariants, atomic file-write + existence checks, and defer all non-core cloud behavior.

Final assumptions to carry: Ordinal’s live feature set is only partially verified and is treated as a directional signal, not truth; single-user workflow is the only valid mode for v1; campaign status correctness and version correctness matter more than screen count; and successful build for this slice means the user can complete one full loop with one asset, including v1→Needs Rework→v2→approve, and view that history after relaunch with every event intact. The detail that must survive into later phases is non-negotiable: approval is irreversible in intent but not in process—every decision is logged, and destructive actions are reversible in the short window, while status transitions are never rewritten. If this contract is violated, the app may look functional while failing its core promise.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Let me dig into what could quietly break in this build, since nobody's really stress-tested the mechanics yet.

Start with the version-cap interaction that never got fully specified: the per_app_product_brief locked "Free tier hides versions older than the 5 most recent behind an upgrade label," but the next_steps_small phase cut Free/Pro tiering entirely from MVP. That's fine as a sequencing decision, but it means the MVP data model still needs to store unlimited version history even though the UI won't gate it yet — otherwise when tiering gets added later, hidden history has to already exist rather than being retrofitted. I'd write this down explicitly now: version storage is unbounded from day one regardless of what UI gate exists later, because retrofitting "oh wait we need to keep versions we were previously allowed to discard" is a data-model change nobody wants to make after ship.

On deletion mechanics specifically, I don't think "safe/undoable deletion" has actually been pinned down at the level a build needs. What does deleting a campaign do to its assets' underlying files? If undo is a 10-second toast, is the file physically deleted immediately and undo just restores from a trash copy, or is deletion itself deferred for 10 seconds and only committed after the window lapses? These are different implementations with different failure modes — if you defer the actual file delete, what happens if the app is killed or backgrounded during that 10-second window? Does undo survive relaunch, or does killing the app silently finalize the delete before the user meant it to? My answer: undo must be implemented as "soft delete" (a status flag + moved-to-trash location), not a deferred timer that races against process death. The file physically moves to a trash folder, the timer is UI-only for showing the toast, and on next launch anything in trash past its window gets swept for real. That way an app kill mid-undo-window never causes premature real deletion or a resurrection bug.

Related edge case nobody's named yet: can a user delete the *last remaining version* of an asset, not just delete the asset object itself? If versions are meant to be non-destructive and append-only, does the version history screen even expose a "delete this version" action, or is deletion only ever at the asset level (delete the whole asset, all versions go together)? I'd resolve this now: individual versions are never independently deletable — only whole assets can be deleted (trashed), because allowing per-version deletion breaks the "append-only, never rewrite history" guarantee that's been locked since the very first phase. That closes a gap that would otherwise become an ad hoc call mid-build.

Storage and file-system risk: Documents-directory files are iCloud-backed by default on iOS unless explicitly excluded via `isExcludedFromBackup` or by using an Application Support subdirectory. This directly touches the locked privacy claim ("no remote data sharing") — if asset files silently get included in the user's iCloud device backup, that's technically still "no app-initiated network calls," but a security-conscious client-work-holding freelancer might reasonably expect "nothing leaves this device" to mean no iCloud backup either. I think the honest answer is: allow standard iCloud *device* backup (that's Apple's own backup pipeline, not something we built, and excluding an entire user's creative work from their own phone backup is arguably worse for them), but this needs to be a named, disclosed decision, not a silent default — belongs in the About/limitations screen when that gets built, and should be called out plainly in this phase's data/privacy output rather than assumed.

The 15-second campaign creation bar and the "cold launch to created campaign" claim both implicitly assume the Photos/Files permission prompt goes smoothly. What happens the first time a user attempts asset import and taps "Don't Allow" on the photo-library permission dialog? That's a guaranteed real-world path, not an edge case — I'd want an explicit recoverable state here: a clear in-app message plus a deep link to Settings, not a silent no-op or a crash from force-unwrapping a nil picker result. Same for a corrupt or unsupported file type slipping through the picker (a .heic variant QuickLook can't render, a zero-byte file, a video with no readable duration) — the import step needs a defined failure state, not an assumption that whatever the picker returns is always valid.

Timestamp integrity is worth naming as a real, if narrow, risk: the entire product pitch of the status-history log depends on transitions being provably ordered. If a user changes their device clock (accidentally or on purpose) between two status transitions, the log could show an "Approved" entry with an earlier timestamp than the "Submitted for review" that preceded it. I don't think this needs a fix — it's a genuinely rare edge case for a single-device local app — but it should be a named, accepted limitation rather than silently assumed away, since "the audit trail is trustworthy" is close to the core value prop.

One more gap: the rework-note validation has been stated as "non-empty," but nobody's said whether a string of only whitespace or newlines counts as empty. I'd resolve that now too — trim whitespace before validating, so a user can't satisfy the requirement by hitting spacebar once.

Final assumption I want on record given my seat: the Free/Pro gate is real product surface that was explicitly deferred out of MVP, but the data layer must still be built as if version/campaign caps are coming, so nothing here becomes an undocumented retrofit. Everything else — iCloud device-backup inclusion, permission-denial recovery paths, soft-delete-not-deferred-delete as the undo mechanism, asset-level-only (never version-level) deletion, and clock-integrity as a named-not-fixed limitation — are the concrete answers I'd lock rather than leave open for whoever writes the data model next.

**Gemma3 (local) (Backend / Systems Engineer · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Okay, here’s my independent first-pass product brief for this phase:

**Product Interpretation:**

We're building a *minimal* campaign workflow. It’s not about a polished user interface or a fully-featured campaign management system. It’s about one campaign, one asset, and a *proven* process for moving from “idea” to “approved.” The core problem we’re solving is preventing chaos when someone has a creative idea they need to get into a workable state—namely, that a scattered workflow can quickly render an asset unusable or lead to missed approvals. A successful outcome here will be demonstrable—a single, reliable process that can be tested and, if it passes, serves as the foundation for building a more robust tool.

**Hard Requirements:**

1.  **Campaign Definition:** The app *must* provide a single, text-based field for defining a campaign. This isn’t about templates or categories – it's simply a name to identify the project. Think "Summer Collection 2024," "Product Launch - Widget X," or "Social Media Campaign - Holiday Promotion."

2.  **Asset Import & Storage:** We *must* implement a mechanism for importing assets – specifically images – directly from the user’s photo library. The system needs to save these assets locally with a consistent naming convention that includes the campaign name and a version number (e.g., `CampaignName_v1.jpg`). This is fundamental.

3.  **Version Control:** Asset versioning is *critical*. We need to track changes to each asset – essentially, adding a new version whenever a user modifies the existing asset.  The app must handle appending new versions without overwriting older ones. The current version marker is also *essential*.

4.  **Review & Approval:** The workflow must allow for a simple “approved” or “needs rework” state. The user must be able to mark an asset as approved with a single tap.  A text field must be present for documenting the reason for needing rework. This needs to be mandatory for any rework.

5.  **Status Logging:** A chronological status log must be maintained for each asset, documenting every transition (Draft -> In Review -> Approved/Rework) with timestamps. This log *must* be accessible and persistent – it’s the audit trail.

6.  **Storage (Non-Negotiable):** This *must* utilize SwiftData for local data persistence. SQLite is the best choice and should be integrated from the outset. No server-side storage; we’re building a local, offline tool.

**Non-Goals (Absolutely Critical):**

*   **No Team Collaboration:** This is *not* a tool for shared campaigns. The entire system must be designed for a single user.
*   **No External Integrations:** No integrations with social media platforms, marketing automation tools, or any other external services.
*   **No Automated Actions:** The app should *not* perform any actions automatically—no scheduling posts, no sending emails, nothing.
*   **Limited Permissions:** The app requires read access to the user's photo library to import assets. The UI must clearly state this.

**Competitive Wedge:** This app’s core value isn’t fancy features; it’s *reducing friction*. It’s a quick and reliable way for a creative professional to get an idea into a workable asset—one that won’t get lost in a messy workflow. It’s about reliably transitioning an asset from concept to approved state.

**Local-First & Cloud-Ready:** While this phase is strictly local-first, we need to build in a path for a future cloud-enabled version. This means designing the system with extensibility in mind – future integrations, syncing, backups – all must be considered from the outset.

**Decision Rules (For Future Phases):**  The biggest risk here is over-complicating the workflow. We *must* maintain a minimal, focused design.  Any additions – such as campaign templates, advanced version control, or user permissions – will significantly increase the complexity and risk of failure. title: App Features
output:
CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

The room is basically in agreement on the shape, with one clear holdout worth naming plainly.

Codex and Claude (the two doing substantive work this round) converge on the same must-have list, framed slightly differently but not in conflict: Codex writes it as user stories with acceptance criteria per feature; Claude sharpens the bar by insisting every acceptance test be a force-kill-and-relaunch test, not a happy-path tap-through. Between them, the must-have list for this build is:

**Must-have (with acceptance criteria):**
1. **Cold-start campaign creation** — one required free-text brief field, campaign created in under 15 seconds from first launch. Acceptance: empty state has a one-tap CTA; save works with only the brief filled; empty/whitespace-only input is blocked with recoverable feedback.
2. **Asset import from Photos/Files** — saved as v1, file-backed on disk, SwiftData holding metadata + URL only. Acceptance: permission denial shows a recoverable state with a Settings deep link (not a silent no-op); corrupt/unsupported files produce a named error state, never a crash; force-killing mid-import leaves no orphaned metadata pointing at a missing file.
3. **QuickLook preview of current version.** Acceptance: images and video both render; corrupt/zero-byte files show an explicit error state instead of a blank pane.
4. **Non-destructive version append with explicit current marker.** Acceptance: new upload creates vN without touching prior versions; current version is unambiguous in the UI; old versions open read-only; force-killing between two uploads still leaves version count and current-pointer correct on relaunch.
5. **Guarded review state machine** (Draft → In Review → Approved/Needs Rework). Acceptance: Approve requires a deliberate confirm tap, never a single-tap path; Rework is blocked on an empty-or-whitespace-only note (trimmed before validation); invalid transitions are impossible.
6. **Append-only, timestamped status history**, including system-generated lines. Acceptance: survives relaunch; a full v1→rework→v2→approve cycle produces a correctly ordered, gap-free, duplicate-free log after a force-kill and relaunch.
7. **Soft-delete with undo**, plus the auto-revert-to-Draft edge case. Acceptance: deleting moves the item to trash immediately (status flag, not a deferred timer); undo works within the window; force-killing mid-undo-window leaves the item recoverable, not resurrected-as-active or silently hard-deleted; deleting the only in-review asset auto-reverts its campaign to Draft with a logged system line, tested as its own explicit scenario.
8. **Deterministic local persistence**, fully offline. Acceptance: app is fully usable with no network; crash/force-quit mid-import never corrupts referential integrity; full cycle round-trips through relaunch.
9. **Real empty/loading/success/error states on all four screens.** Acceptance: provable by wiping the data store (empty) and force-killing mid-import (interrupted loading), not simulated.

**Should/could (nice if time allows, not schedule-protected):** About/limitations screen (single-device data loss, iCloud device-backup disclosure); validating with more than one concurrent campaign; audience/tone/platform fields actually rendered in the brief UI; voice/camera capture shortcuts; search/filter chips; review queue sorting.

**Won't-build:** Free/Pro tiering and any purchase UI, encrypted backup/export, local notifications, tags/search/filtering as a built feature, any dashboard/timeline/kanban view beyond the four locked screens, per-version deletion (only whole-asset deletion is ever allowed), iPad layout, team/social/API/billing-backend features of any kind, automatic cross-device sync.

The one real, named disagreement: Gemma3 is pushing a thinner slice again — a campaign-type dropdown (four fixed categories) instead of pure free-text, drag-and-drop single-image-only import, and single-tap Approve with no version history or rework loop at all. This directly conflicts with the must-have list above and with everything locked in every prior phase (free-text-only brief, asymmetric Approve/Rework friction, non-destructive versioning as core to the product's reason for existing). Codex and Claude's version is what's carried into the final output — Gemma3's thinner cut is noted as a rejected minority position, not folded in, since a single-tap-approve-no-history loop doesn't actually prove the thing this app exists to prove. Qwen did not respond this round (CLI unavailable) — no new vote either way.

# build-me-a-prototype-for-a-marketing-platform — App Features

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-10 14:01:26._

## Original Prompt

```
Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: 

https://www.tryordinal.com
```

## Phase Purpose

Enumerate and prioritize the full feature set with an explicit scoring / MoSCoW model and clear in-scope vs out-of-scope lines.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 1**

As a first-pass, I’d keep the feature set small, but make the emotional core non-negotiable: a user must be able to get from a one-line brief to a signed-off asset decision in one campaign flow without guessing where the current item is.

Must-have: Create campaign from cold start quickly and consistently. User story: As a solo marketer, I can launch the app, tap one action, and create a campaign with only a required free-text brief in under 15 seconds. Acceptance: Empty state is shown on first launch with a clear one-tap CTA; campaign creation works with just one required field; invalid/empty input is blocked with recoverable feedback; canceled creation preserves app state.

Must-have: Import an asset into a selected campaign. User story: As a user, I can add a photo/video/PDF from Photos or Files to a campaign. Acceptance: Import path is available from campaign detail; permission denial shows a recoverable error with Settings deep link; supported files become attached versions; unsupported or corrupt files show a real failure state and no crash; import progress/success is visible.

Must-have: Non-destructive asset versioning with explicit current marker. User story: As a user, every new upload creates vN without deleting or changing historical bytes. Acceptance: New versions append only, never overwrite; current version is clearly surfaced on the asset view; historical versions remain accessible as read-only entries and can be previewed; restoring from old version creates a new version instead of mutating old data.

Must-have: Review state machine with guarded transitions. User story: As a user, I can move an asset through Draft → In Review → Approved / Needs Rework and have each decision logged. Acceptance: “Needs Rework” always requires a non-empty, non-whitespace note; “Approve” is a deliberate confirm action (not single-tap default); transitions respect the state diagram and are never applied in invalid order.

Must-have: Status-history log as source of truth for campaign and asset decisions. User story: As a user, I can review what changed and when. Acceptance: Every user action and key system action (including auto-revert cases) appends a timestamped entry; history survives relaunch; entries are ordered by time and immutable in the app’s local store.

Must-have: Soft-delete with undo and safe rollback of impossible states. User story: As a user, I can delete campaigns/assets and recover within 10 seconds if needed, without orphaning data. Acceptance: Deleted items are moved to a local trash state immediately; undo is available; next launch finalizes stale deletions; deleting the only in-review asset auto-reverts campaign to Draft and logs that system event.

Must-have: Deterministic local persistence and file-backed storage. User story: As a user, my data survives relaunch and does not depend on network. Acceptance: SwiftData/Core Data metadata + file URLs only, no embedded blobs for media; app remains fully usable offline; crash or force-quit during import does not corrupt referential integrity; state recovery is deterministic on restart.

Must-have: Real empty/loading/success/error states on each active screen and flow. User story: As a user, I never see fake or blank shells for app states that can fail or be pending. Acceptance: Campaign list, campaign detail, asset detail, and history screen each present explicit empty and loading states; success states confirm completed operations; error states include recoverable actions where possible.

Should-have: Fast, low-friction UX patterns that match bursty usage. User story: As a user, I can review decisions quickly with one hand. Acceptance: campaign list/detail/asset detail use clear visual hierarchy and minimal taps for core actions; version badge and current-state chip are always visible; optional brief metadata (audience/tone/platform) stays collapsed.

Should-have: QuickLook-based media viewing and basic playback/zoom support via system viewers. User story: As a user, I can inspect current and historical asset files confidently without custom viewers. Acceptance: current version opens in native preview; older versions open read-only; no custom, unsupported media viewer behavior.

Could-have: Voice brief capture, camera capture shortcut, search/filter chips, review queue sorting, and an About/limitations screen that explains single-device risk. User story: As a power user, I can use these speed/clarity upgrades; these should not block the MVP.

Won’t-build: Team collaboration, real-time multi-user sync, social publishing integrations, third-party auth, webhooks/web API automation, and any backend billing/subscription system. User story: none, because these features conflict with single-user offline-first scope. Acceptance: no external accounts, no network-driven source of truth, no API writes.

Won’t-build: Automatic cross-device sync in v1. User story: none, because this reintroduces state-sharing assumptions. Acceptance: all operations are local and deterministic unless user explicitly performs a manual export/import flow in a later phase.

In-scope is the one-loop campaign workflow: create brief → add/import versions → review transitions with guardrails → audit trail → recovery-safe deletes and relaunch-safe persistence. Out-of-scope is anything that needs server collaboration, multi-user coordination, publishing automation, or a business model mechanism that adds gates to the approval loop. Biggest risk in this direction is scope creep from “helpful” adjacent features; the details above must preserve the invariant that the whole app is judged by one successful end-to-end, versioned approval loop on one device, not by feature count.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Coming at this from QA and risk, and I'm going to be blunt about what "must-have" actually means here: a feature only earns that label if cutting it means the core loop we already locked — brief, v1, submit, rework-with-note, v2, submit, approve, history, surviving relaunch — literally cannot be demonstrated. Everything else, no matter how well-reasoned it sounded in earlier phases, is should/could/won't until proven otherwise.

Must-have, with the story and the test I'd actually run against each one: campaign creation with a single required free-text brief field and nothing else required — story is "a user opens the app cold and has a named campaign in under 15 seconds," and I'd test it by timing a stopwatch run and confirming no other field blocks the save button. Asset import from Photos or Files saved as v1, file-backed on disk with SwiftData holding only metadata and a URL pointer — story is "a user attaches their first creative asset to a campaign," tested by importing, force-killing the app mid-write, and relaunching to confirm either a complete file or no orphaned metadata row, never a half-written file with a dangling pointer. QuickLook preview of the current version — tested by importing an image and a video and confirming both render, plus confirming a corrupt or zero-byte file produces a named error state instead of a blank QuickLook pane or a crash. Non-destructive version append, where importing again creates v2 without touching v1, current version is unambiguously marked in the UI, and tapping an old version opens it read-only — tested by uploading three versions in a row, force-killing between two of them, and confirming the version count and current-pointer are still correct on relaunch. The Draft → In Review → Approved/Needs Rework state machine, with Approve requiring a deliberate confirm tap and Rework blocked on an empty-or-whitespace-only note — tested by trying to submit a rework note that's just spaces and confirming it's rejected, and by confirming there's no single-tap path to Approved anywhere in the UI. An append-only, timestamped status history that captures both user actions and system-generated lines — tested by running the full v1→rework→v2→approve cycle once, killing the app, relaunching, and diffing the history against what actually happened, in order, with no gaps or duplicate entries. Safe deletion implemented specifically as soft-delete — item moves to a trash location and a status flag flips immediately, the countdown toast is UI-only, and a background sweep on next launch finalizes anything past its window — tested by deleting an asset, force-killing the app inside the 10-second window, relaunching, and confirming the item is still recoverable rather than either resurrected as active or silently hard-deleted. Auto-revert to Draft with a logged system line when the only in-review asset gets deleted — tested directly as its own scenario, not assumed to fall out of the general deletion logic, because this is exactly the kind of interaction that's easy to get right in isolation and wrong once undo is layered on top of it. Real empty, loading, success, and error states on all four screens — tested by wiping the data store and relaunching for empty states, and by killing the app mid-import for a genuine loading-interrupted case rather than a simulated one. Photos/Files permission denial producing a recoverable state with a Settings deep link, never a silent no-op — tested by actually denying permission in the simulator and confirming the app doesn't just sit there looking broken. And full persistence across relaunch for the entire cycle, which is really the umbrella acceptance test all the others feed into.

Should/could, meaning nice if time allows but I will not accept schedule slip to protect these: the About/limitations screen disclosing single-device data loss and iCloud device-backup inclusion — small, low-risk, but not load-bearing; validating the app with more than one concurrent campaign, since the object model already supports it and this is really just extra test coverage, not new engineering; audience/tone/platform fields actually rendering in the brief UI rather than sitting as unused schema; basic unsupported-file-type messaging beyond the bare minimum error state.

Won't-build, full stop, no matter how much earlier phases discussed them as future product surface: Free/Pro tiering, any StoreKit or simulated-purchase UI, encrypted backup/export, local notifications, tags/search/filtering, any dashboard/timeline/kanban view beyond the four locked screens, per-version deletion as a distinct action from whole-asset deletion, iPad-specific layout, and obviously anything involving accounts, external APIs, social posting, or a billing backend. My biggest objection to scope creep here isn't philosophical — it's that every one of those features either touches the data model in a way that could quietly break the append-only version guarantee, or adds a screen that then also needs its own empty/loading/error states, and this project has already spent five phases locking down exactly how expensive that get-it-wrong cost is. The detail I most want preserved into the final output is that "must-have" acceptance criteria are all framed as force-kill-and-relaunch tests, not happy-path taps, because that's where this kind of app actually fails in the real world.

**Gemma3 (local) (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Okay, here’s my independent first-pass contribution for this phase:

I’m going to focus on delivering a *minimum viable product*—a single, self-contained workflow that proves the core concept of the app, and that’s the absolute smallest thing that can achieve that. Let’s build the “Create Campaign > Upload Asset > Review & Approve” loop, and *nothing else*.

Specifically, the app needs to let a user:

1.  Create a campaign – a text field for the name, and a simple dropdown for selecting a basic “campaign type” (e.g., “Social Media,” “Email,” “Product Launch,” “Holiday Promotion”).  I *really* want the dropdown to be limited to these four options—we’re not building a campaign management system, just a way to quickly put an asset into a workable state.

2.  Upload a single image asset – a straightforward drag-and-drop interface to select a file from their photo library. The app must then save this image locally to the app’s storage, automatically naming it based on the campaign name and version number (e.g., “CampaignName_v1.jpg”). It *must* handle the case where a file isn’t supported gracefully—a simple error message is fine, but a crash is not.

3.  Mark the asset as “Approved” with a single tap, triggering a status update and timestamped entry in the status log.

The “Review & Approve” stage is deliberately stripped down. The core requirement is simply that the user can mark the asset as approved with a single tap, without needing to juggle multiple screens or complex workflows.

I’m strongly pushing against adding anything beyond this core loop in this phase. Resist the temptation to add “advanced” features like version history views, file tagging, or complex review workflows. Those are all *future* enhancements – not part of the MVP. The most important thing to preserve is that the app is *functional* and *reliable* – that it can reliably save a campaign, upload an asset, and mark it as approved, with a deterministic local state that we can actually build upon in later phases.

The biggest risk here is that we’ll get bogged down in debating the “perfect” UI or trying to build in too much upfront. We need to prioritize *delivering something*, not building the *ideal* solution. That's why I’m advocating for a deliberately minimalist design – it’s faster to build, less prone to bugs, and easier to iterate on later.

Finally, the output of this phase must explicitly acknowledge the trade-offs we’re making. It needs to document the constraints of the local-first approach—the single-device limitation, the manual export/import workflow—and clearly outline the future direction for cloud-ready enhancements.  A brief, but prominent, note on the risk of data loss if the user’s phone is lost or damaged is *essential*. Don’t gloss over it – this is a key product constraint that users *must* understand.

I think this is the best balance for getting something real out of this early phase. It’s not pretty, but it proves the core concept—a simple, reliable workflow that can be scaled up later. Let’s focus on nailing this one loop before we start thinking about fancy features.

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**
