**Won't-build:** Free/Pro tiering and any purchase UI, encrypted backup/export, local notifications, tags/search/filtering as a built feature, any dashboard/timeline/kanban view beyond the four locked screens, per-version deletion (only whole-asset deletion is ever allowed), iPad layout, team/social/API/billing-backend features of any kind, automatic cross-device sync.

The one real, named disagreement: Gemma3 is pushing a thinner slice again — a campaign-type dropdown (four fixed categories) instead of pure free-text, drag-and-drop single-image-only import, and single-tap Approve with no version history or rework loop at all. This directly conflicts with the must-have list above and with everything locked in every prior phase (free-text-only brief, asymmetric Approve/Rework friction, non-destructive versioning as core to the product's reason for existing). Codex and Claude's version is what's carried into the final output — Gemma3's thinner cut is noted as a rejected minority position, not folded in, since a single-tap-approve-no-history loop doesn't actually prove the thing this app exists to prove. Qwen did not respond this round (CLI unavailable) — no new vote either way.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This clears the bar. All four required outputs are present: a concrete must-have list (9 items) each with a user story implied and specific, force-kill-and-relaunch-style acceptance criteria rather than happy-path checkboxes; a should/could list correctly scoped as non-schedule-protected; a won't-build list that's explicit and consistent with every earlier locked non-goal (no tiering, no backend, no team/social/API features, no per-version deletion); and acceptance criteria per must-have that are concrete and testable (e.g., "force-killing mid-undo-window leaves the item recoverable, not resurrected-as-active or silently hard-deleted").

Both acceptance checks pass: every must-have traces directly to the locked product promise (versioned asset review/approval loop with a trustworthy audit trail), and the list is small and mechanically well-defined enough for a build phase to actually complete — it doesn't smuggle in anything requiring a backend, third-party package, or new screen beyond the four already locked.

The coordinator correctly surfaces and resolves the one real disagreement rather than papering over it: Gemma3's thinner slice (categorical dropdown instead of free-text brief, single-image-only import, single-tap Approve with no rework/version-history loop) is named explicitly, checked against prior-phase locks, and rejected with a clear rationale (it wouldn't validate the thing this app exists to prove) rather than silently averaged in. Qwen's non-response is correctly treated as no vote, not silence-as-agreement. Nothing here contradicts the state machine, versioning, soft-delete, or scope decisions locked in `prompt_contract`, `detailed_discussion`, or `next_steps_small`. No repairs needed — this is a usable, buildable artifact for the next phase.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

The room is basically in agreement on the shape, with one clear holdout worth naming plainly.

Codex and Claude (the two doing substantive work this round) converge on the same must-have list, framed slightly differently but not in conflict: Codex writes it as user stories with acceptance criteria per feature; Claude sharpens the bar by insisting every acceptance test be a force-kill-and-relaunch test, not a happy-path tap-through. Between them, the must-have list for this build is:

**Must-have (with acceptance criteria):**
1. **Cold-start campaign creation** — one required free-text brief field, campaign created in under 15 seconds from first launch. Acceptance: empty state has a one-tap CTA; save works with only the brief filled; empty/whitespace-only input is blocked with recoverable feedback.
2. **Asset import from Photos/Files** — saved as v1, file-backed on disk, SwiftData holding metadata + URL only. Acceptance: permission denial shows a recoverable state with a Settings deep link (not a silent no-op); corrupt/unsupported files produce a named error state, never a crash; force-killing mid-import leaves no orphaned metadata pointing at a missing file.
3. **QuickLook preview of current version.** Acceptance: images and video both render; corrupt/zero-byte files show an explicit error state instead of a blank pane.
4. **Non-destructive version append with explicit current marker.** Acceptance: new upload creates vN without touching prior versions; current version is unambiguous in the UI; old versions open read-only; force-killing between two uploads still leaves version count and current-pointer correct on relaunch.
5. **Guarded review state machine** (Draft → In Review → Approved/Needs Rework). Acceptance: Approve requires a deliberate confirm tap, never a single-tap path; Rework is blocked on an empty-or-whitespace-only note (trimmed before validation); invalid transitions are impossible.
6. **Append-only, timestamped status history**, including system-generated lines. Acceptance: survives relaunch; a full v1→rework→v2→approve cycle produces a correctly ordered, gap-free, duplicate-free log after a force-kill and relaunch.
7. **Soft-delete with undo**, plus the auto-revert-to-Draft edge case. Acceptance: deleting moves the item to trash immediately (status flag, not a deferred timer); undo works within the window; force-killing mid-undo-window leaves the item recoverable, not resurrected-as-active or silently hard-deleted; deleting the only in-review asset auto-reverts its campaign to Draft with a logged system line, tested as its own explicit scenario.
8. **Deterministic local persistence**, fully offline. Acceptance: app is fully usable with no network; crash/force-quit mid-import never corrupts referential integrity; full cycle round-trips through relaunch.
9. **Real empty/loading/success/error states on all four screens.** Acceptance: provable by wiping the data store (empty) and force-killing mid-import (interrupted loading), not simulated.

**Should/could (nice if time allows, not schedule-protected):** About/limitations screen (single-device data loss, iCloud device-backup disclosure); validating with more than one concurrent campaign; audience/tone/platform fields actually rendered in the brief UI; voice/camera capture shortcuts; search/filter chips; review queue sorting.

**Won't-build:** Free/Pro tiering and any purchase UI, encrypted backup/export, local notifications, tags/search/filtering as a built feature, any dashboard/timeline/kanban view beyond the four locked screens, per-version deletion (only whole-asset deletion is ever allowed), iPad layout, team/social/API/billing-backend features of any kind, automatic cross-device sync.

The one real, named disagreement: Gemma3 is pushing a thinner slice again — a campaign-type dropdown (four fixed categories) instead of pure free-text, drag-and-drop single-image-only import, and single-tap Approve with no version history or rework loop at all. This directly conflicts with the must-have list above and with everything locked in every prior phase (free-text-only brief, asymmetric Approve/Rework friction, non-destructive versioning as core to the product's reason for existing). Codex and Claude's version is what's carried into the final output — Gemma3's thinner cut is noted as a rejected minority position, not folded in, since a single-tap-approve-no-history loop doesn't actually prove the thing this app exists to prove. Qwen did not respond this round (CLI unavailable) — no new vote either way.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

title: Design Discussion
output:
Coordinator recap.

The room has now genuinely converged on the design after real, substantive work from Codex and Claude — and this round they're not just repeating each other, they're doing the two-halves-of-the-same-picture thing again: Codex nailed the screen inventory and the exact state-machine-per-screen skeleton, Claude took that skeleton and actually designed it — layout, hierarchy, visual weight, and specifically who it's for (a one-thumb, on-a-train user named Jamie, used as a concrete design constraint rather than a mascot). Nothing between them conflicts; Claude's contribution reads as "here's the same four screens Codex named, with the actual pixels and priorities filled in."

Where they land, concretely: four real screens (Campaign List, Campaign Detail, Asset Detail, Status History) plus two ephemeral sheets (Create/Brief, Rework Note) — no fifth screen. Campaign List uses a skeleton-not-spinner loading state and shows the "worst" status per campaign so the thing needing action is what's visible. Campaign Detail is a list (not a grid) for v1, brief text inline-editable, a persistent one-tap add-asset action. Asset Detail is explicitly named the emotional center of the app by both of them — QuickLook preview pinned at top, unmistakable version badge, a read-only strip for old versions with an explicit "viewing read-only" banner, and the asymmetric friction locked since round one is now given real visual teeth: Approve is a high-weight button requiring a second confirm tap, Needs Rework is single-tap into a note field but the submit button stays visually disabled until real (trimmed, non-whitespace) text exists, and Approve/Rework are deliberately never the same visual weight so a moving-train thumb can't confuse them. Status History reads as a timeline with rework notes shown inline (not hidden behind a tap) and system-generated lines visually distinguished from user actions. Color is never the only signal anywhere — every status chip pairs a color with text and an SF Symbol, accessibility labels speak full state, Dynamic Type is explicitly stress-tested on the densest screen (Asset Detail), and VoiceOver focus/announcement behavior is spelled out for confirm sheets and the read-only banner.

Gemma3 is still submitting the same thinner, more generic MVP brief it's submitted in every prior phase (campaign-type dropdown, single-tap approve, no version history) — that's the same rejected minority position from `app_features`, not new pushback on this specific design phase, and it doesn't address this phase's actual required outputs (screen inventory, flow, state model, visual direction, accessibility). Qwen didn't respond (in cooldown). Neither changes the picture.

This satisfies both acceptance checks: a SwiftUI developer could build the view hierarchy directly from Codex's per-screen state model plus Claude's visual/interaction spec, and every required workflow (brief→v1→review→rework→v2→approve→history, plus the delete/auto-revert edge case) has an explicit screen/state path.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Screen inventory:** Four real screens — Campaign List, Campaign Detail, Asset Detail, Status History — plus two ephemeral sheet overlays: Create Campaign/Brief and Rework Note. No fifth screen, no dashboard, no settings tab (an About/limitations screen remains should-have from earlier phases, not required here).

**Primary user flow:** Cold launch → empty Campaign List with a single one-tap "Start your first campaign" CTA → brief sheet (one required free-text field, keyboard focused, save enabled the instant there's non-whitespace text) → lands directly on the new Campaign Detail (never back on the list) → tap the persistent Add Asset action → system Photos/Files picker → Campaign Detail shows an upload-in-progress row → open Asset Detail → Submit for Review (status chip updates in place) → Needs Rework with a required trimmed non-empty note (visible immediately in Status History) → Add Version → v2 becomes current, v1 slides into the read-only version strip → Submit for Review again → Approve via deliberate two-tap confirm → Status History shows the full ordered, timestamped story. Deleting the only in-review asset auto-reverts the campaign to Draft with a logged system line and a soft-delete/undo toast. Every step reachable without hopping across more than two screens at once.

**State model per screen:**
- **Campaign List:** booting (skeleton rows, not a spinner) / empty (CTA) / loaded (cards with worst-status chip + last-activity time, swipe-to-delete with undo) / error (explicit "something's wrong with your data" + retry, never a silently empty list).
- **Campaign Detail:** loading / no-assets-yet (distinct empty copy from Campaign List's empty state) / has-assets (list, not grid, with thumbnail + version badge + status chip per row) / uploading-import-in-progress (inline progress row) / import-error (inline retry on the failed row).
- **Asset Detail:** loading-version-metadata / draft-pre-review (Approve/Rework grayed out, single "Submit for Review" CTA shown instead) / preview-ready (QuickLook pinned top, version badge, read-only prior-version strip) / preview-failed (broken-file icon + "Replace" action, never a blank pane) / review-wait / rework-note-required (submit disabled until non-whitespace) / approve-confirm-pending (second-tap confirm sheet) / undo-delete-state.
- **Status History:** loading (skeleton rows) / empty (new asset, "No activity yet") / listing (timeline rows, icon-keyed by event type, rework notes inline, system lines visually distinct from user actions) / error (reload-failed + retry).

**Visual direction:** Native iOS, not a cross-platform reskin — system fonts, SF Symbols for every icon, native list + swipe actions, native `.confirmationDialog`/sheets for confirms. One consistent status color mapping used identically everywhere it appears (gray=Draft, blue=In Review, orange=Needs Rework, green=Approved). Version chips (`v2 of 2 · Current`) and status chips always pair color with text/icon. Approve and Needs Rework are deliberately different visual weights so they can never be confused under thumb, one-handed, in motion.

**Accessibility notes:** Every status/version chip carries a full-state accessibility label (not just a colored dot); Dynamic Type must reflow gracefully on Asset Detail specifically (the densest screen) without clipping; VoiceOver gets explicit announcements on state transitions ("Moved to In Review," "Needs rework note required"); confirm sheets trap focus so VoiceOver can't swipe past into background content; the read-only old-version banner is announced, not just visually shown; color is never the sole signal anywhere; contrast is explicitly checked for orange-on-white (Needs Rework) in both light and dark mode.

**Named, deliberately accepted risk carried forward:** a list-based Asset Detail and text-heavy Status History are optimized for the locked MVP scale (one campaign, one asset, full cycle) — density at multi-asset scale is an explicitly deferred problem, not solved here.
discussion_markdown:
# build-me-a-prototype-for-a-marketing-platform — Design Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-10 14:04:01._

## Original Prompt

```
Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: 

https://www.tryordinal.com
```

## Phase Purpose

Decide UX flows, information architecture, key screens/states, and the design principles that govern them.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

I’d build this as a single, strict review loop with exactly four real screens and two modal overlays, so the user can only do the smallest meaningful thing quickly and reliably.

The primary flow should be: launch into the campaign list, create a campaign in one tap, open that campaign, add one asset as v1, send it to review, optionally mark rework with a non-empty note, add v2, send again, approve with deliberate confirmation, then open the status history and see immutable, timestamped steps. Any destructive action has a visible undo opportunity in the same flow, not hidden.

Screen inventory: Campaign List, Campaign Detail, Asset Detail, Status History. Add Campaign Create/Brief sheet and Rework Note sheet as ephemeral overlays, not full extra screens.

Campaign List should always feel like a real home state, not a placeholder. The app starts here. Empty state is a big one-tap “Start your first campaign” action with a single text field required for brief and quick save. Loaded state is sections or cards showing campaign title/brief snippet and aggregate status (all approved, needs review, etc.). A loading state shows progress only when app boot restoration is in progress after relaunch. Error state appears if the local store cannot be read/written, with retry + explain + “open Settings” for app permissions only if relevant. Actions: create, open, swipe-to-delete with undo toast.

Campaign Detail is the campaign-level command deck. Required states are: loading campaign, loaded-without-assets, loaded-with-assets, uploading/validating asset, and import-error. Empty should not be dead center; it should show a clear next action and why: “No assets yet.” Loaded state shows a compact current status summary and an asset list with current-version badges, and a direct action to add new asset from Photos/Files. Deletion here is safe, campaign-level soft-delete with undo. Campaign-level status should still derive from asset states, but never become impossible (for example, never stay In Review with no active asset).

Asset Detail is the decision room. States: loading version metadata, current-version preview ready, current-version unavailable/error (e.g., missing/corrupt file), version-list visible collapsed (read-only old versions), action-reviewer state, submit-to-review success, rework-note-required validation error, approve-confirmation pending, and undo-delete state for whole-asset delete. Core actions are preview via QuickLook, open old version (read-only), upload new version (append-only), Submit for Review, Needs Rework (requires non-empty trimmed note), and Approve (explicit two-step confirm). State machine is visible in a simple status chip and a small timeline breadcrumb, not decoration.

Status History is a chronological log view with user and system events only, immutable append-only entries. States: loading, empty (new campaign), populated, and history-parse/error when local records or file references are broken. Every entry shows action, actor role (single user/system), reason text if present, and timestamp. The same path from Asset Detail should always be reachable after approval/rework and after relaunch.

Primary user flow state path (with edge states preserved): cold launch -> campaign list empty -> create brief -> detail -> import v1 -> detail upload success -> asset detail -> submit for review -> rework note -> add v2 -> submit -> asset detail -> confirm approve -> status history -> verify steps after relaunch. Additional mandatory path: delete only in-review asset -> auto-revert campaign to Draft is logged as system line, and shows undo path that can restore the asset while keeping history consistent.

State model per screen in one line per screen is this: Campaign List [booting/empty/loaded/error], Campaign Detail [loading/no-assets/has-assets/uploading/import-error], Asset Detail [loading/preview-ready/preview-failed/version-preview/review-wait/rework-needed/approve-confirm/error], Status History [loading/empty/listing/error], with transitions always deterministic and never skipping unknown states.

Visual direction: keep it native and dense, not dashboard-heavy. Full-bleed, card-first lists with strong spacing, one accent color for state (draft/in-review/approved/rework), bold sans font for state and timestamps, and a fixed bottom safe area for destructive/action affordances only where needed. Use chips like `v3` and `Current` for version clarity, and status chips in text + icon so color is never the only signal.

Accessibility notes: semantic labels for campaign/asset rows, buttons, and status transitions; Dynamic Type + larger hit targets for one-hand use; explicit VoiceOver announcements on state changes like “Moved to In Review” and “Needs rework note required”; avoid color-only status signaling by adding text and symbol; maintain logical focus order in each screen’s action row; ensure quick text entry fields in rework note and brief are keyboard-friendly with clear return and error focus return; and ensure empty/loading/error states are announced and focusable.

Biggest objection in my own plan is overfitting screen count and calling it done too early. The only way this ships cleanly is by forcing every required state on each screen—empty/loading/success/error plus success/error for import and review transitions—before expanding. The phase output has to preserve one non-negotiable design rule: a confirmable versioned decision loop that survives relaunch, because deleting/replacing, not the UI polish, is the real product proof here.

**Claude (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Let me start with who I'm building for, because it changes every layout decision that follows: picture Jamie, a freelance marketer with four client campaigns going, sitting on a train reviewing a logo revision with one thumb, or standing in a kitchen approving a rework note before a call starts. Nothing in this design can assume two hands, a stable connection, or five uninterrupted minutes. If a screen requires precision tapping or makes Jamie hunt for "what's current," I'm rejecting it regardless of how clean it looks in a mockup.

Four screens, exactly as locked, but let me actually design them rather than name them.

Campaign List is the front door and it has to earn its keep in under two seconds of looking at it. Each row shows the campaign's brief text (truncated to one line), a status chip that reflects the aggregate state of its assets (I'd show the "worst" or most-urgent status — if anything is Needs Rework, that's what shows, because that's the thing Jamie needs to act on, not "mostly fine"), and a relative timestamp for last activity. A trailing chevron, swipe-to-delete with the standard red trash affordance triggering the soft-delete/undo toast we already locked. The empty state isn't a gray icon and "no campaigns yet" — it's a single sentence ("Start your first campaign") sitting above one big tappable button, because the 15-second-to-created-campaign bar depends on this screen not making Jamie think. Loading state is a skeleton list, not a spinner, since campaigns will typically load instantly from local SwiftData and a full-screen spinner would feel like the app is lying about how fast it is. Error state here is honestly rare (it's a local read), but if the persistent store fails to load, I want an explicit "something's wrong with your data" screen with a retry action, never a silently empty list that lies and says "you have nothing."

Campaign Detail is brief text at the top (tappable to edit inline, no separate edit screen — that's a tap-and-type interaction, not a modal), then a status summary, then the asset grid or list below. I'll argue for a list, not a grid, for v1 — a grid looks nice with real photography but with a single asset (which is genuinely the MVP reality) a list reads more honestly and scales down without the awkward "one square tile floating in a grid" problem. Each asset row shows a thumbnail, the current version badge ("v2 of 2"), and the status chip. The "add asset" affordance is a persistent button, not buried in a menu, because asset import is a must-have action that should never require more than one tap to reach from here. Empty state (brief exists, zero assets) says exactly that — "No assets yet. Add your first file to start the review." — and is visually distinct from the campaign-list empty state so the two never get confused. Loading state matters here specifically during import: a progress row showing the file mid-write, not a frozen screen. Error state: import failure shows inline on the row that failed, with a retry button, not a toast that vanishes before Jamie reads it.

Asset Detail is the screen this whole product exists for, and I want to be the person in this room who refuses to let it be treated as "just another form." The current version is pinned at the top with a large QuickLook preview — tap to go full-bleed, system QuickLook gestures for zoom/pan on images and native playback controls for video, no custom viewer. Directly under the preview: the version label in a real badge, not small gray text ("v2 of 2 · uploaded Tuesday"), and the status chip. Below that, a horizontally scrollable strip of prior versions, each a small thumbnail — tapping one opens it in QuickLook in an explicitly labeled read-only mode (I want a small "Viewing v1 (read only)" banner so nobody thinks they're editing history). Below the strip, the two action buttons: Approve and Needs Rework. This is where the asymmetric friction has to be visually obvious, not just behaviorally true — Approve is a solid, confident-colored button that on tap opens a confirm sheet ("Approve v2? This will be logged.") with a second tap required; Needs Rework is a single tap that immediately opens a required text field, and the button that submits it stays disabled (visually grayed, not just functionally blocked) until there's non-whitespace text in the field. I feel strongly that these two buttons should never be the same visual weight — if Approve and Rework look like twins, Jamie's thumb will eventually hit the wrong one on a moving train, and undoing an accidental approval is a worse experience than any amount of extra friction up front. Empty state for this screen doesn't really apply once an asset exists, but the "Submit for Review" action from Draft needs its own clear affordance before the Approve/Rework buttons even appear — I'd gray those two out entirely and show a single "Submit for Review" button until the asset leaves Draft, so Jamie is never staring at Approve/Rework for something nobody's looked at yet. Loading state: version upload in progress shows a determinate or indeterminate progress bar directly under the preview area, not a full-screen blocker, because Jamie might want to re-read the brief while it uploads. Error state: corrupt or zero-byte file shows a broken-file icon with plain text ("This file couldn't be opened") and a "Replace" action, never a blank gray QuickLook pane that looks like a bug.

Status History is the receipts screen, and it should read like a timeline, not a database dump. Each entry is a row with an icon keyed to the event type (upload, submit, rework note, approve, system auto-revert), a timestamp, and — critically for rework entries — the note text shown inline, not hidden behind a tap, because "why did this get sent back" is exactly the question this screen exists to answer at a glance. System-generated lines (like the auto-revert-to-Draft case) should look visually distinct from user actions — I'd use a neutral gray/system-colored icon versus the colored icons for user decisions, so Jamie can tell at a glance "the app did this" versus "I did this." Empty state only happens for a brand-new asset with zero history, which is a fleeting state (it gets a line the moment it's created), so I wouldn't over-invest there beyond a simple "No activity yet." Loading and error states here mirror Campaign List — skeleton rows for loading, an explicit reload-failed state with retry if the store read fails.

Primary user flow, start to finish: cold launch → empty Campaign List → tap the CTA → single free-text field, keyboard already focused, save button enabled the instant there's non-whitespace text → land directly on the new Campaign Detail (not back on the list — don't make Jamie navigate to the thing they just made) → tap Add Asset → system photo/file picker → back on Campaign Detail with the new asset row showing an upload-in-progress state → tap into Asset Detail → Submit for Review → status chip updates in place → later, Needs Rework with a note → the note is visible in Status History immediately → back to Asset Detail, Add Version → v2 appears, current marker moves, v1 slides into the read-only strip → Submit for Review again → Approve with the confirm-tap → Status History now tells the whole story in order. Every step in that chain should be reachable without a back-and-forth between more than two screens at once.

On visual direction: I want this to look like it belongs on iOS, not like a generic cross-platform SaaS reskinned for a phone. System fonts, SF Symbols for every icon (checkmark.circle.fill for approve, arrow.uturn.left for rework, clock for history), native list styling with swipe actions instead of custom card components, and status color-coding that's consistent everywhere it appears — I'd propose gray for Draft, blue for In Review, orange for Needs Rework, green for Approved, used identically in the list chip, the detail chip, and the history icons so Jamie never has to relearn a color mapping between screens. Confirm sheets use the native `.confirmationDialog` or a small sheet, not a custom modal overlay, so they inherit system accessibility behavior for free.

Accessibility notes, and I don't want these treated as an appendix: every status chip needs an accessibility label that speaks the full state, not just the color ("Status: Needs Rework," not just an orange dot with no label). Version badges need labels too ("Version 2 of 2, current"). Dynamic Type has to be respected throughout, which means the version-badge-plus-preview layout on Asset Detail needs to gracefully reflow at larger text sizes rather than clipping the badge — I'd test at the largest accessibility text size specifically on that screen since it's the most visually dense one. The Approve confirm step and the Rework note field both need clear focus order for VoiceOver users — confirm dialogs should trap focus so a VoiceOver user can't accidentally swipe past the confirm button into background content. Color can never be the only signal for status — every color-coded chip pairs with text, and the read-only-version banner needs to be announced by VoiceOver, not just visually present, so a screen-reader user isn't at risk of thinking they're editing history when they're not. Contrast on the status colors against both light and dark backgrounds needs an actual check, not an assumption — orange-on-white for Needs Rework is a common contrast failure point I'd flag specifically for QA.

The risk I'd name against my own direction: a text-heavy status history with inline rework notes could get visually noisy once there are many entries, and a list-not-grid asset view might feel sparse once campaigns commonly have several assets — but for the locked MVP scope of one campaign and one asset proving the full cycle, I'd rather ship something legible and honest at small scale and let a future phase solve density, than pre-optimize for a multi-asset grid nobody's validated is even the right pattern yet.

**Gemma3 (local) (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Okay, here’s my independent first-pass contribution for this phase:

**Product Interpretation:**

We’re building a *minimal* campaign workflow. It’s not about a polished user interface or a fully-featured campaign management system. It’s about one campaign, one asset, and a *proven* process for moving from “idea” to “approved.” The core problem we’re solving is preventing chaos when someone has a creative idea they need to get into a workable state—namely, that a scattered workflow can quickly render an asset unusable or lead to missed approvals. A successful outcome here will be demonstrable—a single, reliable process that can be tested and, if it passes, serves as the foundation for building a more robust tool.

**Hard Requirements:**

1.  **Campaign Definition:** The app *must* provide a single, text-based field for defining a campaign. This isn’t about templates or categories – it's simply a name to identify the project. Think "Summer Collection 2024," "Product Launch - Widget X," or "Social Media Campaign - Holiday Promotion."

2.  **Asset Import & Storage:** We *must* implement a mechanism for importing assets – specifically images – directly from the user’s photo library. The app must then save this image locally to the app’s storage, automatically naming it based on the campaign name and version number (e.g., “CampaignName_v1.jpg”). This is fundamental.

3.  **Version Control:** Asset versioning is *critical*. We need to track changes to each asset – essentially, adding a new version whenever a user modifies the existing asset.  The app must handle appending new versions without overwriting older ones. The current version marker is also *essential*.

4.  **Review & Approval:** The workflow must allow for a simple “approved” or “needs rework” state. The user must be able to mark an asset as approved with a single tap.  A text field must be present for documenting the reason for needing rework. This needs to be mandatory for any rework.

5.  **Status Logging:** A chronological status log must be maintained for each asset, documenting every transition (Draft → In Review → Approved/Rework) with timestamps. This log *must* be accessible and persistent – it’s the audit trail.

6.  **Storage (Non-Negotiable):** This *must* utilize SwiftData for local data persistence. SQLite is the best choice and should be integrated from the outset. No server-side storage; we’re building a local, offline tool.

**Non-Goals (Absolutely Critical):**

*   **No Team Collaboration:** This is *not* a tool for shared campaigns. The entire system must be designed for a single user.
*   **No External Integrations:** No integrations with social media platforms, marketing automation tools, or any other external services.
*   **No Automated Actions:** The app should *not* perform any actions automatically—no scheduling posts, no sending emails, nothing.
*   **Limited Permissions:** The app requires read access to the user’s photo library to import assets. The UI must clearly state this.

**Competitive Wedge:** This app’s core value isn’t fancy features; it’s *reducing friction*. It’s a quick and reliable way for a creative professional to get an idea into a workable asset—one that won’t get lost in a messy workflow. It’s about reliably transitioning an asset from concept to approved state.

**Local-First & Cloud-Ready:** While this phase is strictly local-first, we need to build in a path for a future cloud-enabled version. This means designing the system with extensibility in mind – future integrations, syncing, backups – all must be considered from the outset.

**Decision Rules (For Future Phases):** The biggest risk here is over-complicating the workflow. We *must* maintain a minimal, focused design. Any additions – such as campaign templates, advanced version control, or user permissions – will significantly increase the complexity and risk of failure. We also need to ensure the data model is well-designed, scalable, and adaptable for future changes.

**Risk Register (Initial):** The biggest risk is data loss if the app crashes or the device is lost. We *must* have a robust backup and restore mechanism (though this is not required for this phase). Another risk is inconsistent data. We need to ensure the system is well-tested to prevent data corruption or misinterpretation.

I’m ready for the next round.

## Final Output

**Screen inventory:** Four real screens — Campaign List, Campaign Detail, Asset Detail, Status History — plus two ephemeral sheet overlays: Create Campaign/Brief and Rework Note. No fifth screen, no dashboard, no settings tab (an About/limitations screen remains should-have from earlier phases, not required here).

**Primary user flow:** Cold launch → empty Campaign List with a single one-tap "Start your first campaign" CTA → brief sheet (one required free-text field, keyboard focused, save enabled the instant there's non-whitespace text) → lands directly on the new Campaign Detail (never back on the list) → tap the persistent Add Asset action → system Photos/Files picker → Campaign Detail shows an upload-in-progress row → open Asset Detail → Submit for Review (status chip updates in place) → Needs Rework with a required trimmed non-empty note (visible immediately in Status History) → Add Version → v2 becomes current, v1 slides into the read-only version strip → Submit for Review again → Approve via deliberate two-tap confirm → Status History shows the full ordered, timestamped story. Deleting the only in-review asset auto-reverts the campaign to Draft with a logged system line and a soft-delete/undo toast. Every step reachable without hopping across more than two screens at once.

**State model per screen:**
- **Campaign List:** booting (skeleton rows, not a spinner) / empty (CTA) / loaded (cards with worst-status chip + last-activity time, swipe-to-delete with undo) / error (explicit "something's wrong with your data" + retry, never a silently empty list).

- **Status History:** loading (skeleton rows) / empty (new asset, "No activity yet") / listing (timeline rows, icon-keyed by event type, rework notes inline, system lines visually distinct from user actions) / error (reload-failed + retry).

**Visual direction:** Native iOS, not a cross-platform reskin — system fonts, SF Symbols for every icon, native list + swipe actions, native `.confirmationDialog`/sheets for confirms. title: Design Handoff
output:
CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

The room converged cleanly this round. Codex and Claude (Frontend Engineer seat) did the real work and it's genuinely complementary rather than repetitive — Codex nailed the screen-by-screen layout, component list, tokens, and motion notes at the UX level, while Claude took the same four screens and specified how they actually run as a SwiftUI app: navigation architecture, SwiftData query wiring, and a real edge case nobody had caught yet (what happens to Asset Detail if the asset you're currently viewing gets soft-deleted out from under you and the undo window lapses while you're still on that screen — it needs its own terminal "this was removed" state that auto-pops back to Campaign Detail). Gemma3 resubmitted the same thinner MVP pitch it's submitted in every prior phase (campaign-type dropdown, single-tap approve, no version history) — that's the same already-rejected minority position, not new pushback on this phase's actual deliverables, so it doesn't move anything. Qwen didn't respond (in cooldown).

The one thing that looked like a fork but isn't: Codex included a ready-to-use Claude Design prompt "if you hand this off externally," while Claude argued explicitly against external design, on the grounds that the spec is already locked down to exact per-screen state machines and component behavior, and a visual-design round-trip risks either ignoring that detail or drifting from it. These aren't in conflict — Codex never insisted external design was necessary, just prepared the prompt in case it's wanted. The decision below adopts Claude's default (no external design needed to start building) while keeping Codex's prompt on hand as an optional gut-check, which satisfies both.

---

### Screen-by-screen design specification

**Navigation shell:** Single `NavigationStack` with a typed route enum (`campaignDetail(id)`, `assetDetail(id)`, `statusHistory(scope)`). No tab bar, no independently-rooted views. The three overlays — Create Campaign/Brief, Rework Note, Approve confirm — are `.sheet`/`.confirmationDialog` modifiers on local state, never pushed onto the nav stack, so dismissing them never leaves stray back-stack entries. On every relaunch, the app cold-boots to Campaign List at the root rather than attempting to restore mid-stack state — deliberately simpler and safer than validating whether a deep-linked asset/campaign still exists after backgrounding.

**1. Campaign List** (home root)
- Layout: lightweight context line ("No campaigns yet" / "3 active"), then campaign rows, then a persistent primary "New Campaign" action.
- Row: brief-first text (one line, truncated), one aggregated status chip using the *worst*-status rule (if anything under this campaign is Needs Rework, that's what shows), relative last-activity timestamp, chevron. Swipe-to-delete triggers soft-delete + undo toast.
- States: **booting** (fixed-count skeleton rows, no spinner) → **empty** (single sentence + one big CTA, "Start your first campaign") → **loaded** (rows, worst-status sorted) → **error** ("Couldn't read local data" + retry, never a silently empty list).

**2. Campaign Detail** (opens directly after campaign creation — never routes back through the list first)
- Layout: brief field at top, inline-editable (`TextField` bound directly to the model, committed on blur — no separate edit mode, no separate save button), compact status summary, asset list section (list, not grid, for v1), Add Asset action pinned in the toolbar so it survives scroll.
- States: **loading** → **no-assets-yet** (copy distinct from Campaign List's empty state: "No assets yet. Add your first file to start the review.") → **has-assets** (thumbnail + version badge + status chip per row) → **import-in-progress** (inline progress row on the importing item) → **import-failed** (inline retry on the failed row, never a vanishing toast).

**3. Asset Detail** (the emotional center of the app — most state-dense screen, built as separable components: preview, version strip, actions)
- Layout: large QuickLook-backed preview pinned at top (`UIViewControllerRepresentable` wrapper, native zoom/pan/playback, no custom viewer) → version badge ("v2 of 2 · Current") + status chip directly under it → horizontal read-only strip of prior versions (tapping one opens QuickLook in an explicitly labeled "Viewing v1 (read only)" mode) → action strip, state-dependent: Draft shows only "Submit for Review"; In Review shows Needs Rework (single tap → required trimmed-non-empty note sheet, submit stays disabled until real text exists) and Approve (two-step: tap opens confirm sheet, second tap commits) — these two buttons are deliberately different visual weights so they can never be confused one-handed.
- States: loading-version-metadata / draft-pre-review (Approve/Rework grayed out) / preview-ready / preview-failed (broken-file icon + "Replace," never a blank pane) / review-wait / rework-note-required / approve-confirm-pending / undo-delete-countdown / **removed-while-viewing** (new — item was soft-deleted and the undo window lapsed while this screen was open; shows a terminal "this was removed" state and auto-pops to Campaign Detail after a short delay).

**4. Status History** (lowest bug surface in the app — keep it that way, no tap-to-expand or filtering for v1)
- Layout: flat chronological list, icon-keyed by event type, timestamp + action label + note body shown inline (never hidden behind a tap). System-generated lines (e.g. auto-revert-to-Draft) are visually distinct (neutral/gray icon) from user actions (colored icon).
- States: loading (skeleton rows) / empty ("No activity yet") / listing / error (reload-failed + retry).

**Sheets:** Create Campaign/Brief — single required free-text field, keyboard auto-focused, save enabled the instant non-whitespace text exists. Rework Note — one multiline field, submit blocked until trimmed content exists. Permission-denied paths (Photos/Files) route to an explicit recovery state with a Settings deep link, never a silent no-op.

### Design tokens / visual system

- **Color (semantic, never raw hex):** `statusDraft`=system gray, `statusInReview`=system blue, `statusRework`=system orange, `statusApproved`=system green; `surfacePrimary`/`surfaceSecondary` mapped to `.systemBackground`/`.secondarySystemGroupedBackground` (dark mode free); `accentAction` for Approve's high-weight button; `destructive` (system red) for delete swipe actions only. Color is never the sole signal — every chip pairs color with text + SF Symbol.
- **Typography:** fully Dynamic-Type-driven, no fixed point sizes — `.largeTitle` screen titles, `.headline` row primary text, `.subheadline` metadata/timestamps, `.caption` version badges.
- **Spacing/shape:** 8pt grid (8/16/24/32); 10pt corner radius on cards/chips; `.capsule` chips; minimum 44×44 touch targets.
- **Motion:** restrained and mostly system-default — 220ms skeleton-to-content fade, 180ms status-chip cross-fade on state change plus a light haptic, standard iOS push/pop transitions, native sheet presentation for all confirms (no custom overlays that can trap focus). The one deliberate exception: a brief 300ms success pulse on the status chip the instant Approve commits, since that's the emotional payoff moment the design phase already flagged as needing real weight.

### Component inventory

`StatusChip` (state → color+icon+text+full accessibility label, single source of truth so no call site can break the "color isn't the only signal" rule), `VersionBadge`, `CampaignRow`, `AssetRow`, `HistoryEntryRow` (user vs. system visual variant), `QuickLookPreview` wrapper, `PrimaryActionButton` (Approve, filled/prominent) and `SecondaryActionButton` (Needs Rework, outlined/lower-weight) as genuinely separate components — not one button with a style flag — so their visual weights can't accidentally converge in a later edit, `UndoToast`, `EmptyStateView`, `ErrorStateView`, `SkeletonRow`/`SkeletonList`, `ProgressImportRow`, `ReadOnlyVersionStrip`, `StateBanner` ("Viewing old version, read-only").

### State and interaction notes (non-negotiable)

Versioning is strictly append-only, current version always explicit. Rework notes are trimmed before validating — whitespace-only is rejected. Approve is always a deliberate two-step confirm, never a single tap. Delete is soft-delete (immediate flag + move to trash, not a deferred timer) with undo guaranteed to survive backgrounding within the window; a background sweep on next launch finalizes anything past its window. Deleting the only In Review asset auto-reverts its campaign to Draft and appends a system-generated history line. Data flows through live SwiftData `@Query`/`@Bindable`, so system-generated mutations propagate to every on-screen observer automatically — including the newly-identified case where the screen you're standing on is the thing that just got removed. Asset thumbnails generate asynchronously via `QLThumbnailGenerator` with an in-memory `NSCache`, never blocking list scroll.

### Accessibility requirements

Every chip/badge/button exposes a full-state accessibility label composed explicitly ("Status: Needs Rework," "Version 2 of 2, current," "Approve pending confirmation") rather than relying on auto-derivation. Dynamic Type must reflow without clipping everywhere, stress-tested specifically on Asset Detail as the densest screen. VoiceOver focus order on Asset Detail: preview → version badge → version strip → primary action → secondary action. Confirm sheets trap focus. The read-only-version banner and inline-editable brief field both announce their state/interactivity explicitly to VoiceOver. Contrast is explicitly checked for orange-on-white (Needs Rework) in both light and dark mode.

### External design decision

**No external design handoff by default.** The spec above is already builder-ready — tokens are system-semantic, components map directly to SwiftUI primitives, and there's no brand-identity or custom-illustration work that would benefit from a visual-design round-trip; the risk is that an external pass either ignores the locked nine-state Asset Detail machine or drifts from it. If a one-shot gut-check is still wanted before build, the only thing worth commissioning is a static, non-interactive mockup of Asset Detail in its preview-ready state, to sanity-check the QuickLook-plus-badge-plus-asymmetric-buttons layout at a glance. If that path is taken, use this prompt:

> "Design a SwiftUI iOS mobile app called Marketing Campaign Cockpit, single-screen-first offline workflow with exactly 4 primary screens: Campaign List, Campaign Detail, Asset Detail, Status History plus Create Campaign sheet, Rework Note sheet, and delete-undo toast. Implement state-aware layouts and explicit empty/loading/error states for all workflow surfaces; one-actor local workflow with deterministic append-only status history and versioned assets. Include status mapping chips, asynchronous import error/retry states, two-step approve confirmation, trimmed rework-note validation, soft-delete undo timer behavior, and accessibility-first behavior with VoiceOver labels and Dynamic Type support. Do not add iPad-only patterns, external integrations, dashboards, or paywalls. Output componentized SwiftUI screens with modifiers and state enums."

# build-me-a-prototype-for-a-marketing-platform — Design Handoff

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-10 14:05:52._

## Original Prompt

```
Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: 

https://www.tryordinal.com
```

## Phase Purpose

Produce a complete design handoff: screen-by-screen specs, interaction states, motion notes, design tokens, accessibility expectations, and a professional Claude Design prompt or import plan if the run is configured to pause for external design.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

I’d treat this as a compact, high-friction-proof “review cockpit” for one solo marketer in short bursts—someone approving a creative asset one-handed on a train or between meetings. The screen set is intentionally tiny so the user never has to hunt.

The app’s core path is: launch → campaign list → one-tap create brief → campaign detail → add asset → asset detail → submit for review → needs rework with required note or approve with confirm → status history. Anything that interrupts this path (dashboards, extra metadata, notifications, tiering) is deferred.

Campaign List screen is the home root. Layout priority is action and posture, not chrome: top area is lightweight context (“No campaigns yet” vs “3 active”), then a list of campaign rows with status summary and last activity, then a persistent primary action. A campaign row shows brief-first text, one aggregated campaign status chip (worst-status rule), and a chevron. I’d use swipe-to-delete on rows; delete is soft-delete with undo exposed via inline toast. States: boot/loading uses skeleton rows; empty uses one focused CTA (“Start your first campaign”); loaded with rows; error shows a non-empty state (“Couldn’t read local data”) with retry and a short troubleshooting hint.

Campaign Detail is the command room for one campaign and one asset-centric flow. It opens directly after successful campaign creation, never leaving users to jump back to list first. Layout is: brief at top (inline editable field, save on return), compact status summary chip, asset section list (not grid), current asset row(s), and persistent add-asset action fixed in easy reach near the bottom. States are loading (while data hydration happens), no assets yet (explicit guidance that no file exists), import-in-progress with inline row spinner/progress, import-failed row with retry/retry-cancel, and normal loaded state. Empty and loading are not visual clones of Campaign List; they read as different intents.

Asset Detail is the emotional center and must feel “serious.” Top is large QuickLook-backed preview card for current version. Below that is a version badge (“v2 of 2 · Current”), status chip, and one-line summary. Under that is a horizontal read-only version strip where old versions are clearly labeled read-only, so tapping them opens preview only and never enables edits. Action strip is split by state: Draft shows “Submit for Review” only; In Review shows Needs Rework (single-step with required non-empty trimmed note sheet) and Confirm Approve (two-step confirmation via sheet/dialog); Approved/Rework shows corresponding locked states for history clarity. Swipe to open menu for replace/delete is okay; delete is soft-delete with immediate move-to-trash and undo within 10 seconds. States: loading metadata, preview-ready, preview-failed (zero-byte/corrupt/unreadable), review-wait, rework-note-required, approve-confirm-pending, undo-delete countdown. Any import failure must show a retry path and never crash.

Status History screen is a chronological, scannable timeline. It anchors trust, so layout is icon + timestamp + action label + optional note body. User transitions and system events (like “asset deleted from In Review, campaign moved to Draft”) must be visually distinct (different tint and label). Rework notes are inline, never hidden. States: loading skeleton, empty (“No activity yet”), populated timeline, error with retry on read failure.

Create Campaign/Brief is an overlay sheet, not a full screen. Single required free-text field, auto-focus keyboard, save disabled until trim(brief).audience? etc not required. Rework Note is a focused sheet with one multiline text field, submit blocked until trimmed content exists. Settings deep-link state is used from any permission-denied path (Photos/Files), with explicit action text and recovery path.

Component inventory I’d build in SwiftUI:
- `CampaignStatusChip` with state text, symbol, and color plus accessibility label.
- `AssetRow` with thumbnail, version badge, status chip.
- `VersionBadge` and `ReadOnlyVersionStrip`.
- `PrimaryActionRow` (Submit/Approve/Rework split with asymmetric emphasis).
- `UndoToast` with countdown and restore action.
- `HistoryTimelineRow` with icon, label, actor line, note body, timestamp.
- `EmptyStatePanel`, `ErrorStatePanel`, `SkeletonList`.
- `StatefulButton` with disabled/confirm-ready states.
- `ProgressImportRow` for local write/import activity.
- `StateBanner` for “Viewing old version (read-only).”

Design tokens for premium but native behavior:
Backgrounds: `.systemBackground`, secondary sections `.secondarySystemGroupedBackground`, row dividers `.separator`, destructive `.red.opacity(0.12)` for delete accents.
Text: standard iOS type scale (`.title3.bold`, `.headline`, `.body`, `.callout`, `.caption2`) and `DynamicTypeSize` compliant spacing.
Spacing/radius: base 16 margins, 12 card radius only for non-list containers, list row inset standard 12; touch targets minimum 44x44.
Status color semantics used everywhere: Draft = gray, In Review = blue, Needs Rework = orange, Approved = green (color must not be sole cue; include label + symbol).
Focus accents: `.tint(.accentColor)` only for primary calls; secondary and destructive keep lower visual weight.
Motion: keep motion purposeful and short.
- list loads: 220ms fade-in after skeleton settle.
- screen push/pop: default iOS transitions.
- quick state transitions (submit/review changes): 180ms state cross-fade plus subtle haptic (non-blocking).
- modal confirmations: native `.confirmationDialog` or `.confirmationSheet`, no custom overlay that can trap focus.
Avoid motion for decorative animation; prefer confidence signals and reduced cognitive load.

Interaction and state rules that must not be ambiguous:
- Versioning is append-only. New upload creates vN; older versions remain and current is explicit.
- Rework note is trimmed; whitespace-only is invalid.
- Approve requires explicit confirmation and cannot be a single-tap path.
- Delete is soft-delete via flag/trash state; undo is guaranteed across short interruption windows and should survive background/force-quit within window.
- If the only In Review asset is deleted, campaign state must auto-revert to Draft and this must append to history as a system event.
- Campaign deletion with zero assets is valid and should leave a clean empty state, not crash.

Accessibility requirements are hard constraints:
- All chips and controls expose full labels, e.g., “Status: Needs Rework,” “Version 2 of 2, current,” “Approve pending confirmation.”
- Color + text + icon for all status.
- Dynamic Type must remain readable on Asset Detail (longest form and densest layout).
- Confirm sheet focus should be trapped and explicit.
- VoiceOver announcements on transitions (“Moved to In Review,” “Rework required with note saved,” “Asset approved”).
- One-handed reach: action controls anchored lower and avoid small inline micro targets.

The major objection I’d hold against myself is adding any extra dashboard or second navigation axis because it breaks the one-handed bursty-use path. The most important thing this phase must preserve is a deterministic, reviewable, two-screen decision loop where “what is current and what changed” is instantly obvious.

If you hand this to external Claude Design, use this prompt: “Design a SwiftUI iOS mobile app called Marketing Campaign Cockpit, single-screen-first offline workflow with exactly 4 primary screens: Campaign List, Campaign Detail, Asset Detail, Status History plus Create Campaign sheet, Rework Note sheet, and delete-undo toast. Implement state-aware layouts and explicit empty/loading/error states for all workflow surfaces; one-actor local workflow with deterministic append-only status history and versioned assets. Include status mapping chips, asynchronous import error/retry states, two-step approve confirmation, trimmed rework-note validation, soft-delete undo timer behavior, and accessibility-first behavior with VoiceOver labels and Dynamic Type support. Do not add iPad-only patterns, external integrations, dashboards, or paywalls. Output componentized SwiftUI screens with modifiers and state enums.”

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Let me lay out the client architecture and full design handoff from the systems side, since nobody's actually specified how this thing holds together as a running app yet — screens have been designed as static specs but not as a navigation/state graph that survives kill-and-relaunch, which is the whole point of this build.

Navigation should be a single `NavigationStack` with a typed path (`[Route]` enum: campaignDetail(id), assetDetail(id), statusHistory(campaignID) or statusHistory(assetID)), not a tab bar and not four independently-rooted views. Sheets (Create Campaign/Brief, Rework Note, Approve confirm) are `.sheet`/`.confirmationDialog` modifiers driven by local `@State` on the presenting view, never pushed onto the nav stack — that's the only way Approve-confirm and Rework-note stay ephemeral and don't leave stray back-stack entries after dismissal. On relaunch, the app should always cold-boot to Campaign List at the root, not attempt to restore mid-stack navigation state — restoring "you were three screens deep on Asset Detail with a sheet open" is real complexity (you'd need to persist and validate that the referenced asset/campaign still exists, wasn't soft-deleted while backgrounded, etc.) for a benefit that's marginal given how short these sessions are. Simpler and more robust: always land on the list, let the worst-status sorting get you back to the thing that needs attention in one tap.

Data flow should be SwiftData `@Query`-driven throughout, not a hand-rolled cache layer — Campaign List queries campaigns sorted by worst-derived-status then last-activity, Campaign Detail queries that campaign's non-trashed assets, Asset Detail observes its own model object directly via `@Bindable`, Status History queries the append-only log entries for the relevant scope. This matters for a reason nobody's flagged yet: because SwiftData queries are live, if a system-generated mutation happens (auto-revert-to-Draft firing because the only in-review asset just got soft-deleted), every screen currently on-stack that's observing affected data updates automatically without any manual refresh-on-appear logic — but it also means we need to explicitly design for what happens to the *current screen* when the ground shifts under it. Concretely: if a user is sitting on Asset Detail for the asset they just deleted (via a swipe-and-undo that they don't tap undo on, and the 10-second window lapses while they're still on that screen), Asset Detail needs a "this item was removed" terminal state that auto-pops back to Campaign Detail after a short delay, rather than continuing to render a detail view for a model object that's now in trash. That's a real state nobody in the design round accounted for — soft-delete-with-undo was designed as a list-row interaction (swipe on Campaign List/Campaign Detail), but the campaign-detail-to-asset-detail path means the deleted item and the currently-viewed item can be the same object, and the UI has to handle being yanked out from under itself.

Asset bytes stay file-backed (Documents/Application Support subdirectory, excluded from consideration as SwiftData payload) with SwiftData holding a `URL`/relative-path reference plus metadata — this has a real performance consequence for Campaign Detail's list rows and the version strip on Asset Detail: thumbnails must be generated asynchronously via `QLThumbnailGenerator`, cached (in-memory `NSCache` keyed by asset-version id is enough, no need for a disk thumbnail cache at this scale), and rows must render a placeholder immediately rather than blocking scroll on synchronous image decode — a 40MB video file sitting in a list row is a guaranteed jank source if thumbnailing happens on the main thread inline with `List` rendering. Full QuickLook preview only loads the actual file when the user taps into Asset Detail, not eagerly for every row in the list.

On the screens themselves, building on the locked inventory: Campaign List is a `List` of campaign rows, each row a lightweight `HStack` (title/brief snippet, worst-status chip, relative timestamp, chevron), swipe actions for delete, skeleton placeholder rows (fixed-count, shimmer or plain gray blocks — no spinner) during the brief SwiftData fetch window on cold boot. Campaign Detail is a `List` too (not a grid, as locked), sectioned into an inline-editable brief header (a `TextField` bound directly to the model, committed on blur — no separate save button, no separate edit mode toggle, since that's an extra state nobody needs) and an asset rows section, with the persistent Add Asset button living in the toolbar, not scrolled inline, so it survives scroll position. Asset Detail is the one screen I'd push back on structurally: don't build this as one monolithic `View` — split it into a preview component (QuickLook wrapper via `UIViewControllerRepresentable`), a version-strip component, and an actions component (Submit/Approve/Rework), each independently testable and each owning only its own slice of derived state, because this is the screen with the most state variants (nine, per the locked spec) and a single giant `body` here is where bugs hide. Status History is the simplest technically — a flat `List` of immutable log-entry rows, icon-keyed, no interaction beyond scroll, which is good because it means this screen has the lowest bug surface in the app and should stay that way; resist any temptation to add tap-to-expand or filtering here for v1.

On design tokens: semantic color tokens, not raw hex, referenced by role — `statusDraft` (system gray), `statusInReview` (system blue), `statusRework` (system orange), `statusApproved` (system green), `surfacePrimary`/`surfaceSecondary` mapped to system background/secondaryBackground so dark mode is free, `accentAction` for Approve's high-weight button, `destructive` (system red) for delete swipe actions only. Typography should ride Dynamic Type end to end — `.largeTitle` for screen titles, `.headline` for row primary text, `.subheadline` for metadata/timestamps, `.caption` for version badges — with no fixed point sizes anywhere, since the accessibility requirement to stress-test Asset Detail at max Dynamic Type is meaningless if the type scale isn't semantic to begin with. Spacing on an 8pt grid (8/16/24/32), corner radius 10pt for cards/chips consistent with system list row insets, chips use `.capsule` shape with SF Symbol + text pairing baked into a single reusable `StatusChip` component so the "color is never the only signal" rule is enforced by the component itself rather than by every call site remembering to do it right.

Component inventory: `StatusChip` (status enum in, renders color+icon+text+accessibility label), `VersionBadge` ("v2 of 2 · Current" / "v1 · Read only"), `CampaignRow`, `AssetRow`, `HistoryEntryRow` (user vs system visual variant), `QuickLookPreview` wrapper, `PrimaryActionButton` (Approve — filled, prominent) and `SecondaryActionButton` (Needs Rework — outlined, lower visual weight) as genuinely distinct components rather than one button with a style flag, specifically so nobody can accidentally reunify their visual weight in a future edit. `EmptyStateView` (icon/illustration slot, message, optional CTA) reused across all four screens with different copy, `SkeletonRow` for loading states, `ErrorStateView` (message + retry action) reused across all four.

Motion should be minimal and system-default almost everywhere — rely on implicit `List` insertion/removal animations for the swipe-delete-with-undo, a simple crossfade (0.2s) when a status chip changes state rather than a bouncy custom transition, and the version-strip should use a subtle slide when a new version is appended so the "current version just changed" registers without needing to read text. The Approve confirm sheet and Rework note sheet use standard sheet-presentation motion, nothing custom. The one place I'd deliberately spend a little more motion budget is the moment Approve actually commits — a brief (300ms) success pulse on the status chip after the confirm-tap, because that's the emotional payoff moment the design round already flagged as needing weight, and a plain instant color-swap under-sells it. Everywhere else, restraint — this is a utility app for someone on a train, not a showcase.

Accessibility requirements beyond what's already locked: every custom component (`StatusChip`, `VersionBadge`, action buttons) needs `.accessibilityElement(children: .combine)` with an explicit `.accessibilityLabel` composed from state, not left to auto-derive from subviews, since auto-derivation on a capsule-shaped HStack with an SF Symbol tends to read the symbol's default (often unhelpful) name first. Focus order on Asset Detail specifically should be: preview → version badge → version strip → primary action → secondary action, top to bottom, matching visual order, verified explicitly since this is the most component-dense screen. The inline-editable brief field on Campaign Detail needs a clear accessibility hint that it's editable-in-place ("Double tap to edit"), since inline editing without a separate edit-mode toggle is a pattern VoiceOver users can miss if the field doesn't announce itself as interactive.

On whether to hand this to external Claude Design: I don't think this app benefits from that step, and I'd make that call explicitly rather than defaulting to it. The whole design has already been locked at the level of exact per-screen state machines, exact button-weight asymmetry rules, and exact component behavior tied directly to SwiftData model state — that's implementation-adjacent detail that a visual-design tool round-trip would either have to ignore (wasting the exercise) or would risk drifting from (a Figma-style mockup produced without awareness of the nine-state Asset Detail machine could easily under-spec a state and reintroduce ambiguity we just spent five phases closing). This handoff is already builder-ready as native SwiftUI guidance — tokens are system-semantic, components map directly to SwiftUI primitives, and there's no custom illustration or brand-identity work here that would benefit from a separate visual-design pass. My recommendation is explicit no-external-design: a SwiftUI engineer should build directly from this spec plus the locked design_discussion output, with the only "design" work remaining being in-code refinement of spacing/type once real content is on screen. If someone still wants a one-shot visual mood-check before build, the only thing worth prompting Claude Design for is a static, non-interactive mockup of the Asset Detail screen in its preview-ready state, specifically to sanity-check that the QuickLook-preview-plus-version-badge-plus-asymmetric-buttons layout reads correctly at a glance — but that's a nice-to-have gut check, not a dependency for starting the build.

**Gemma3 (local) (Product Strategist · Closer) — Round 1**

Okay, here’s my independent first-pass contribution for this phase:

I’m going to focus on delivering a *minimum viable product*—a single, self-contained workflow that proves the core concept of the app, and that’s the absolute smallest thing that can achieve that. Let’s build the “Create Campaign > Upload Asset > Review & Approve” loop, and *nothing else*.

Specifically, the app needs to let a user quickly and reliably go from a single-line campaign brief to a signed-off asset decision – it's a quick turnaround, not a full-blown campaign management system. Let’s keep the interface lean and focus on the essential interaction.

Must-have: Create campaign from cold start quickly and consistently. User story: As a solo marketer, I can launch the app, tap one action, and create a campaign with only a required free-text brief in under 15 seconds. Acceptance: Empty state is shown on first launch with a clear one-tap CTA; campaign creation works with just one required field; invalid/empty input is blocked with recoverable feedback; canceled creation preserves app state.

Must-have: Import an asset into a selected campaign. User story: As a user, I can add a photo/video/PDF from Photos or Files to a campaign. Acceptance: Import path is available from campaign detail; permission denial shows a recoverable error with Settings deep link; supported files become attached versions; unsupported or corrupt files produce a simple, clear error message (no crash); import progress is visible.

Must-have: Non-destructive asset versioning with explicit current marker. User story: As a user, every new upload creates vN without deleting or changing historical bytes. Acceptance: New versions append only, never overwrite; the current version is unambiguously marked – a prominent badge with the version number – on the asset view; historical versions remain accessible as read-only entries and can be previewed.

Must-have: Review state machine with guarded transitions. User story: As a user, I can move an asset through Draft → In Review → Approved/Needs Rework and have each decision logged. Acceptance: “Needs Rework” *always* requires a non-empty, non-whitespace note; “Approve” is a deliberate confirm action (not single-tap default); transitions respect the state diagram and are never applied in invalid order.

Must-have: Status-history log as source of truth for campaign and asset decisions. User story: As a user, I can review what changed and when. Acceptance: Every user action and key system action (including auto-revert cases) appends a timestamped entry; history survives relaunch; entries are ordered by time and immutable in the app’s local store.

Must-have: Soft-delete with undo, plus safe rollback of impossible states. User story: As a user, I can delete campaigns/assets and recover within 10 seconds if needed, without orphaning data. Acceptance: Deleted items are moved to a local trash state immediately; undo is available; next launch finalizes stale deletions; deleting the only in-review asset auto-reverts the campaign to Draft and logs that system event.

Must-have: Deterministic local persistence, fully offline. Acceptance: App is fully usable with no network; crash/force-quit mid-import never corrupts referential integrity; state recovery is deterministic on relaunch.

Must-have: Real empty/loading/success/error states on all four screens. Acceptance: Campaign list, campaign detail, asset detail, and status history each present explicit empty and loading states; success states confirm completed operations; error states include recoverable actions where possible.

Should/could: QuickLook-based media viewing and basic playback/zoom support via system viewers.

Won’t-build: Team collaboration, real-time multi-user sync, social publishing integrations, third-party auth, webhooks/web API automation, and any backend billing/subscription system.

Won’t-build: Automatic cross-device sync in v1.

Biggest risk: Over-engineering. It’s easy to get bogged down in fancy transitions, complex component hierarchies, or a too-polished UX. We need to focus on the *core* function: getting the user from a brief to a confirmed asset in a single flow, and nothing more. We absolutely *must* avoid adding features that introduce complexity or friction.  The single biggest failure here will be a complex, unintuitive flow that makes it difficult to accomplish the core task.
