## Final Output

The room converged cleanly this round. Codex and Claude (Frontend Engineer seat) did the real work and it's genuinely complementary rather than repetitive — Codex nailed the screen-by-screen layout, component list, tokens, and motion notes at the UX level, while Claude took the same four screens and specified how they actually run as a SwiftUI app: navigation architecture, SwiftData query wiring, and a real edge case nobody had caught yet (what happens to Asset Detail if the asset you're currently viewing gets soft-deleted out from under you and the undo window lapses while you're still on that screen — it needs its own terminal "this was removed" state that auto-pops back to Campaign Detail). Gemma3 resubmitted the same thinner MVP pitch it's submitted in every prior phase (campaign-type dropdown, single-tap approve, no version history) — that's the same already-rejected minority position, not new pushback on this phase's actual deliverables, so it doesn't move anything. Qwen didn't respond (in cooldown).

The one thing that looked like a fork but isn't: Codex included a ready-to-use Claude Design prompt "if you hand this off externally," while Claude argued explicitly against external design, on the grounds that the spec is already locked down to exact per-screen state machines and component behavior, and a visual-design round-trip risks either ignoring that detail or drifting from it. These aren't in conflict — Codex never insisted external design was necessary, just prepared the prompt in case it's wanted. The decision below adopts Claude's default (no external design needed to start building) while keeping Codex's prompt on hand as an optional gut-check, which satisfies both.

---

### Screen-by-screen design specification

**Navigation shell:** Single `NavigationStack` with a typed route enum (`campaignDetail(id)`, `assetDetail(id)`, `statusHistory(scope)`). No tab bar, no independently-rooted views. The three overlays — Create Campaign/Brief, Rework Note, Approve confirm — are `.sheet`/`.confirmationDialog` modifiers on local state, never pushed onto the nav stack, so dismissing them never leaves stray back-stack entries. On every relaunch, the app cold-boots to Campaign List at the root rather than attempting to restore mid-stack state — deliberately simpler and safer than validating whether a deep-linked asset/campaign still exists after backgrounding.

**1. Campaign List** (home root)
- Layout: lightweight context line ("No campaigns yet" / "3 active"), then campaign rows, then a persistent primary "New Campaign" action.
- Row: brief-first text (one line, truncated), one aggregated status chip using the *worst*-status rule (if anything under this campaign is Needs Rework, that's what shows), relative last-activity timestamp, chevron. Swipe-to-delete triggers soft-delete + undo toast.
- States: **booting** (fixed-count skeleton rows, no spinner) → **empty** (single sentence + one big CTA, "Start your first campaign") → **loaded** (rows, worst-status sorted) → **error** ("Couldn't read local data" + retry, never a silently empty list).

**2. Campaign Detail** (opens directly after campaign creation — never routes back through the list first)
- Layout: brief field at top, inline-editable (`TextField` bound directly to the model, committed on blur — no separate edit mode, no separate save button), compact status summary, asset list section (list, not grid, for v1), Add Asset action pinned in the toolbar so it survives scroll.
- States: **loading** → **no-assets-yet** (copy distinct from Campaign List's empty state: "No assets yet. Add your first file to start the review.") → **has-assets** (thumbnail + version badge + status chip per row) → **import-in-progress** (inline progress row on the importing item) → **import-failed** (inline retry on the failed row, never a vanishing toast).

**3. Asset Detail** (the emotional center of the app — most state-dense screen, built as separable components: preview, version strip, actions)
- Layout: large QuickLook-backed preview pinned at top (`UIViewControllerRepresentable` wrapper, native zoom/pan/playback, no custom viewer) → version badge ("v2 of 2 · Current") + status chip directly under it → horizontal read-only strip of prior versions (tapping one opens QuickLook in an explicitly labeled "Viewing v1 (read only)" mode) → action strip, state-dependent: Draft shows only "Submit for Review"; In Review shows Needs Rework (single tap → required trimmed-non-empty note sheet, submit stays disabled until real text exists) and Approve (two-step: tap opens confirm sheet, second tap commits) — these two buttons are deliberately different visual weights so they can never be confused one-handed.
- States: loading-version-metadata / draft-pre-review (Approve/Rework grayed out) / preview-ready / preview-failed (broken-file icon + "Replace," never a blank pane) / review-wait / rework-note-required / approve-confirm-pending / undo-delete-countdown / **removed-while-viewing** (new — item was soft-deleted and the undo window lapsed while this screen was open; shows a terminal "this was removed" state and auto-pops to Campaign Detail after a short delay).

**4. Status History** (lowest bug surface in the app — keep it that way, no tap-to-expand or filtering for v1)
- Layout: flat chronological list, icon-keyed by event type, timestamp + action label + note body shown inline (never hidden behind a tap). System-generated lines (e.g. auto-revert-to-Draft) are visually distinct (neutral/gray icon) from user actions (colored icon).
- States: loading (skeleton rows) / empty ("No activity yet") / listing / error (reload-failed + retry).

**Sheets:** Create Campaign/Brief — single required free-text field, keyboard auto-focused, save enabled the instant non-whitespace text exists. Rework Note — one multiline field, submit blocked until trimmed content exists. Permission-denied paths (Photos/Files) route to an explicit recovery state with a Settings deep link, never a silent no-op.

### Design tokens / visual system

- **Color (semantic, never raw hex):** `statusDraft`=system gray, `statusInReview`=system blue, `statusRework`=system orange, `statusApproved`=system green; `surfacePrimary`/`surfaceSecondary` mapped to `.systemBackground`/`.secondarySystemGroupedBackground` (dark mode free); `accentAction` for Approve's high-weight button; `destructive` (system red) for delete swipe actions only. Color is never the sole signal — every chip pairs color with text + SF Symbol.
- **Typography:** fully Dynamic-Type-driven, no fixed point sizes — `.largeTitle` screen titles, `.headline` row primary text, `.subheadline` metadata/timestamps, `.caption` version badges.
- **Spacing/shape:** 8pt grid (8/16/24/32); 10pt corner radius on cards/chips; `.capsule` chips; minimum 44×44 touch targets.
- **Motion:** restrained and mostly system-default — 220ms skeleton-to-content fade, 180ms status-chip cross-fade on state change plus a light haptic, standard iOS push/pop transitions, native sheet presentation for all confirms (no custom overlays that can trap focus). The one deliberate exception: a brief 300ms success pulse on the status chip the instant Approve commits, since that's the emotional payoff moment the design phase already flagged as needing real weight.

### Component inventory

`StatusChip` (state → color+icon+text+full accessibility label, single source of truth so no call site can break the "color isn't the only signal" rule), `VersionBadge`, `CampaignRow`, `AssetRow`, `HistoryEntryRow` (user vs. system visual variant), `QuickLookPreview` wrapper, `PrimaryActionButton` (Approve, filled/prominent) and `SecondaryActionButton` (Needs Rework, outlined/lower-weight) as genuinely separate components — not one button with a style flag — so their visual weights can't accidentally converge in a later edit, `UndoToast`, `EmptyStateView`, `ErrorStateView`, `SkeletonRow`/`SkeletonList`, `ProgressImportRow`, `ReadOnlyVersionStrip`, `StateBanner` ("Viewing old version, read-only").

### State and interaction notes (non-negotiable)

Versioning is strictly append-only, current version always explicit. Rework notes are trimmed before validating — whitespace-only is rejected. Approve is always a deliberate two-step confirm, never a single tap. Delete is soft-delete (immediate flag + move to trash, not a deferred timer) with undo guaranteed to survive backgrounding within the window; a background sweep on next launch finalizes anything past its window. Deleting the only In Review asset auto-reverts its campaign to Draft and appends a system-generated history line. Data flows through live SwiftData `@Query`/`@Bindable`, so system-generated mutations propagate to every on-screen observer automatically — including the newly-identified case where the screen you're standing on is the thing that just got removed. Asset thumbnails generate asynchronously via `QLThumbnailGenerator` with an in-memory `NSCache`, never blocking list scroll.

### Accessibility requirements

Every chip/badge/button exposes a full-state accessibility label composed explicitly ("Status: Needs Rework," "Version 2 of 2, current," "Approve pending confirmation") rather than relying on auto-derivation. Dynamic Type must reflow without clipping everywhere, stress-tested specifically on Asset Detail as the densest screen. VoiceOver focus order on Asset Detail: preview → version badge → version strip → primary action → secondary action. Confirm sheets trap focus. The read-only-version banner and inline-editable brief field both announce their state/interactivity explicitly to VoiceOver. Contrast is explicitly checked for orange-on-white (Needs Rework) in both light and dark mode.

### External design decision

**No external design handoff by default.** The spec above is already builder-ready — tokens are system-semantic, components map directly to SwiftUI primitives, and there's no brand-identity or custom-illustration work that would benefit from a visual-design round-trip; the risk is that an external pass either ignores the locked nine-state Asset Detail machine or drifts from it. If a one-shot gut-check is still wanted before build, the only thing worth commissioning is a static, non-interactive mockup of Asset Detail in its preview-ready state, to sanity-check the QuickLook-plus-badge-plus-asymmetric-buttons layout at a glance. If that path is taken, use this prompt:

> "Design a SwiftUI iOS mobile app called Marketing Campaign Cockpit, single-screen-first offline workflow with exactly 4 primary screens: Campaign List, Campaign Detail, Asset Detail, Status History plus Create Campaign sheet, Rework Note sheet, and delete-undo toast. Implement state-aware layouts and explicit empty/loading/error states for all workflow surfaces; one-actor local workflow with deterministic append-only status history and versioned assets. Include status mapping chips, asynchronous import error/retry states, two-step approve confirmation, trimmed rework-note validation, soft-delete undo timer behavior, and accessibility-first behavior with VoiceOver labels and Dynamic Type support. Do not add iPad-only patterns, external integrations, dashboards, or paywalls. Output componentized SwiftUI screens with modifiers and state enums."

## Feedback
All six required outputs are present and concrete: per-screen specs (layout, components, state variants) for all four screens plus the three sheets, a semantic design-token system (color roles, Dynamic-Type-driven typography, 8pt spacing grid, restrained motion with one deliberate exception), a named component inventory with rationale for why Approve/Rework are separate components rather than a styled toggle, non-negotiable state/interaction rules, accessibility requirements that go beyond boilerplate (explicit VoiceOver focus order on the densest screen, explicit contrast check called out), and an explicit, reasoned no-external-design decision with a fallback prompt kept on hand.

The output correctly synthesizes rather than just concatenates the two substantive contributions: Codex's UX/token/component layer and Claude's systems layer (navigation-as-typed-route-enum, SwiftData `@Query` wiring, async thumbnailing) are genuinely complementary, and the coordinator correctly identifies the Codex-prepared-prompt vs. Claude's-no-external-design-argument as non-conflicting rather than forcing a false disagreement. It also catches and resolves a real, previously-unspecified gap — the "removed-while-viewing" terminal state for Asset Detail when the currently-displayed asset gets soft-deleted and its undo window lapses — which is exactly the kind of hidden-decision risk this phase's acceptance checks are meant to catch. Gemma3's resubmitted thinner-MVP pitch is correctly identified as the same already-rejected minority position from `app_features`/`design_discussion`, not new pushback, so it's properly discounted rather than diluting consensus.

Nothing here contradicts any earlier locked phase (four-screen ceiling, asymmetric Approve/Rework friction, append-only versioning, soft-delete-not-deferred-timer, auto-revert-to-Draft edge case). Both acceptance checks are satisfiable: a SwiftUI engineer has concrete types and patterns to build against, and the design direction (native iOS, system-semantic tokens, no cross-platform reskin) is specific to the category rather than generic. No repairs needed.

Qwen didn't respond (in cooldown).

The one thing that looked like a fork but isn't: Codex included a ready-to-use Claude Design prompt "if you hand this off externally," while Claude argued explicitly against external design, on the grounds that the spec is already locked down to exact per-screen state machines and component behavior, and a visual-design round-trip risks either ignoring that detail or drifting from it. These aren't in conflict — Codex never insisted external design was necessary, just prepared the prompt in case it's wanted. The decision below adopts Claude's default (no external design needed to start building) while keeping Codex's prompt on hand as an optional gut-check, which satisfies both.

---

### Screen-by-screen design specification

**Navigation shell:** Single `NavigationStack` with a typed route enum (`campaignDetail(id)`, `assetDetail(id)`, `statusHistory(scope)`). No tab bar, no independently-rooted views. The three overlays — Create Campaign/Brief, Rework Note, Approve confirm — are `.sheet`/`.confirmationDialog` modifiers on local state, never pushed onto the nav stack, so dismissing them never leaves stray back-stack entries. On every relaunch, the app cold-boots to Campaign List at the root rather than attempting to restore mid-stack state — deliberately simpler and safer than validating whether a deep-linked asset/campaign still exists after backgrounding.

**1. Campaign List** (home root)
- Layout: lightweight context line ("No campaigns yet" / "3 active"), then campaign rows, then a persistent primary "New Campaign" action.
- Row: brief-first text (one line, truncated), one aggregated status chip using the *worst*-status rule (if anything under this campaign is Needs Rework, that's what shows), relative last-activity timestamp, chevron. Swipe-to-delete triggers soft-delete + undo toast.
- States: **booting** (fixed-count skeleton rows, no spinner) → **empty** (single sentence + one big CTA, "Start your first campaign") → **loaded** (rows, worst-status sorted) → **error** ("Couldn't read local data" + retry, never a silently empty list).

**2. Campaign Detail** (opens directly after campaign creation — never routes back through the list first)
- Layout: brief field at top, inline-editable (`TextField` bound directly to the model, committed on blur — no separate edit mode, no separate save button), compact status summary, asset list section (list, not grid, for v1), Add Asset action pinned in the toolbar so it survives scroll.
- States: **loading** → **no-assets-yet** (copy distinct from Campaign List's empty state: "No assets yet. Add your first file to start the review.") → **has-assets** (thumbnail + version badge + status chip per row) → **import-in-progress** (inline progress row on the importing item) → **import-failed** (inline retry on the failed row, never a vanishing toast).

**3. Asset Detail** (the emotional center of the app — most state-dense screen, built as separable components: preview, version strip, actions)
- Layout: large QuickLook-backed preview pinned at top (`UIViewControllerRepresentable` wrapper, native zoom/pan/playback, no custom viewer) → version badge ("v2 of 2 · Current") + status chip directly under it → horizontal read-only strip of prior versions (tapping one opens QuickLook in an explicitly labeled "Viewing v1 (read only)" mode) → action strip, state-dependent: Draft shows only "Submit for Review"; In Review shows Needs Rework (single tap → required trimmed-non-empty note sheet, submit stays disabled until real text exists) and Approve (two-step: tap opens confirm sheet, second tap commits) — these two buttons are deliberately different visual weights so they can never be confused one-handed.
- States: loading-version-metadata / draft-pre-review (Approve/Rework grayed out) / preview-ready / preview-failed (broken-file icon + "Replace," never a blank pane) / review-wait / rework-note-required / approve-confirm-pending / undo-delete-countdown / **removed-while-viewing** (new — item was soft-deleted and the undo window lapsed while this screen was open; shows a terminal "this was removed" state and auto-pops to Campaign Detail after a short delay).

**4. Status History** (lowest bug surface in the app — keep it that way, no tap-to-expand or filtering for v1)
- Layout: flat chronological list, icon-keyed by event type, timestamp + action label + note body shown inline (never hidden behind a tap). System-generated lines (e.g. auto-revert-to-Draft) are visually distinct (neutral/gray icon) from user actions (colored icon).
- States: loading (skeleton rows) / empty ("No activity yet") / listing / error (reload-failed + retry).

**Sheets:** Create Campaign/Brief — single required free-text field, keyboard auto-focused, save enabled the instant non-whitespace text exists. Rework Note — one multiline field, submit blocked until trimmed content exists. Permission-denied paths (Photos/Files) route to an explicit recovery state with a Settings deep link, never a silent no-op.

### Design tokens / visual system

- **Color (semantic, never raw hex):** `statusDraft`=system gray, `statusInReview`=system blue, `statusRework`=system orange, `statusApproved`=system green; `surfacePrimary`/`surfaceSecondary` mapped to `.systemBackground`/`.secondarySystemGroupedBackground` (dark mode free); `accentAction` for Approve's high-weight button; `destructive` (system red) for delete swipe actions only. Color is never the sole signal — every chip pairs color with text + SF Symbol.
- **Typography:** fully Dynamic-Type-driven, no fixed point sizes — `.largeTitle` screen titles, `.headline` row primary text, `.subheadline` metadata/timestamps, `.caption` version badges.
- **Spacing/shape:** 8pt grid (8/16/24/32); 10pt corner radius on cards/chips; `.capsule` chips; minimum 44×44 touch targets.
- **Motion:** restrained and mostly system-default — 220ms skeleton-to-content fade, 180ms status-chip cross-fade on state change plus a light haptic, standard iOS push/pop transitions, native sheet presentation for all confirms (no custom overlays that can trap focus). The one deliberate exception: a brief 300ms success pulse on the status chip the instant Approve commits, since that's the emotional payoff moment the design phase already flagged as needing real weight.

### Component inventory

`StatusChip` (state → color+icon+text+full accessibility label, single source of truth so no call site can break the "color isn't the only signal" rule), `VersionBadge`, `CampaignRow`, `AssetRow`, `HistoryEntryRow` (user vs. system visual variant), `QuickLookPreview` wrapper, `PrimaryActionButton` (Approve, filled/prominent) and `SecondaryActionButton` (Needs Rework, outlined/lower-weight) as genuinely separate components — not one button with a style flag — so their visual weights can't accidentally converge in a later edit, `UndoToast`, `EmptyStateView`, `ErrorStateView`, `SkeletonRow`/`SkeletonList`, `ProgressImportRow`, `ReadOnlyVersionStrip`, `StateBanner` ("Viewing old version, read-only").

### State and interaction notes (non-negotiable)

Versioning is strictly append-only, current version always explicit. Rework notes are trimmed before validating — whitespace-only is rejected. Approve is always a deliberate two-step confirm, never a single tap. Delete is soft-delete (immediate flag + move to trash, not a deferred timer) with undo guaranteed to survive backgrounding within the window; a background sweep on next launch finalizes anything past its window. Deleting the only In Review asset auto-reverts its campaign to Draft and appends a system-generated history line. Data flows through live SwiftData `@Query`/`@Bindable`, so system-generated mutations propagate to every on-screen observer automatically — including the newly-identified case where the screen you're standing on is the thing that just got removed. Asset thumbnails generate asynchronously via `QLThumbnailGenerator` with an in-memory `NSCache`, never blocking list scroll.

### Accessibility requirements

Every chip/badge/button exposes a full-state accessibility label composed explicitly ("Status: Needs Rework," "Version 2 of 2, current," "Approve pending confirmation") rather than relying on auto-derivation. Dynamic Type must reflow without clipping everywhere, stress-tested specifically on Asset Detail as the densest screen. VoiceOver focus order on Asset Detail: preview → version badge → version strip → primary action → secondary action. Confirm sheets trap focus. The read-only-version banner and inline-editable brief field both announce their state/interactivity explicitly to VoiceOver. Contrast is explicitly checked for orange-on-white (Needs Rework) in both light and dark mode.

### External design decision

**No external design handoff by default.** The spec above is already builder-ready — tokens are system-semantic, components map directly to SwiftUI primitives, and there's no brand-identity or custom-illustration work that would benefit from a visual-design round-trip; the risk is that an external pass either ignores the locked nine-state Asset Detail machine or drifts from it. If a one-shot gut-check is still wanted before build, the only thing worth commissioning is a static, non-interactive mockup of Asset Detail in its preview-ready state, to sanity-check the QuickLook-plus-badge-plus-asymmetric-buttons layout at a glance. If that path is taken, use this prompt:

> "Design a SwiftUI iOS mobile app called Marketing Campaign Cockpit, single-screen-first offline workflow with exactly 4 primary screens: Campaign List, Campaign Detail, Asset Detail, Status History plus Create Campaign sheet, Rework Note sheet, and delete-undo toast. Implement state-aware layouts and explicit empty/loading/error states for all workflow surfaces; one-actor local workflow with deterministic append-only status history and versioned assets. Include status mapping chips, asynchronous import error/retry states, two-step approve confirmation, trimmed rework-note validation, soft-delete undo timer behavior, and accessibility-first behavior with VoiceOver labels and Dynamic Type support. Do not add iPad-only patterns, external integrations, dashboards, or paywalls. Output componentized SwiftUI screens with modifiers and state enums."

---

CONSENSUS: YES

title: Ios Architecture Review
output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Here's where the room landed. Codex laid the architectural skeleton (SwiftUI/domain/storage layering, the five core model types, framework list, testing pyramid) and Claude's QA pass did real corrective work on top of it rather than just restating it — two genuine bug catches that change what gets built, not just how it's described:

1. **The Photos/Files permission-denied state is dead code as currently scoped.** `PhotosPicker` and `.fileImporter` run out-of-process and hand the app only what the user picked — there's no `NSPhotoLibraryUsageDescription` prompt to deny. The Settings-deep-link recovery UI every design phase has specified only has a real trigger if camera capture (should/could tier) gets built later. Codex didn't push back on this — it's a correction to an assumption baked into four prior phases, and it changes what should actually get built for the must-have import path (handle security-scoped resource lifecycle correctly instead) versus what's speculative UI for a state that can't occur.
2. **Concrete, previously-missing ordering rules that close a whole bug class**, not per-screen ad hoc handling: on import, write the file to disk and confirm success *before* creating the SwiftData record; on delete-finalize, delete the SwiftData record first, then the file. Either direction, the failure mode if the app dies mid-operation is an orphaned file (already an accepted, named limitation) rather than a dangling metadata pointer that every other screen would have to defensively handle.

Codex and Claude agree, without friction, on everything else that matters: Application Support (not Documents) for asset bytes so users can't tamper with backing files via the Files app; SwiftData with a repository-actor write boundary and `ModelContext` kept main-actor-only while actual file I/O happens off it; a real `Schema`/`SchemaMigrationPlan` from day one (Claude's addition — for a single-device app with no backend, a failed store open on a future model change is total data loss, not a degraded experience); QuickLook presented only after a synchronous file-existence/non-zero-size check; zero third-party packages, since PhotosUI, QuickLook, UniformTypeIdentifiers, SwiftData, and FileManager cover everything; and StoreKit/CloudKit/Vision/ARKit/BGTaskScheduler/push all correctly excluded as unjustified for this phase. Testing converges too: state-machine and derived-status logic as plain testable Swift (not embedded in view bodies), in-memory SwiftData containers for unit tests, and `app.terminate()` + fresh `launch()` in XCUITest as the actual mechanism for the relaunch-survival claims this whole project is built around.

Gemma3 restated the architecture in simpler terms but also proposed collapsing to three screens (dropping Campaign List as the entry point) — that contradicts the four-screen inventory locked across `design_discussion` and `design_handoff`, and screen count isn't this phase's decision to reopen anyway, so it's noted and set aside, not adopted. Qwen didn't respond again (cooldown) — no new vote.

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture recommendation:** Three-layer split — SwiftUI views/view models own UI state only; a small domain layer (plain Swift types/services, not embedded in view bodies) owns the review-state-machine, version-current-pointer, and worst-status-derivation rules so they're unit-testable without instantiating a view hierarchy; a storage layer owns exactly two things — SwiftData for structured metadata/object graphs via a single `ModelContext` boundary plus a repository actor for writes, and the filesystem for asset bytes. `ModelContext` is main-actor-bound; any file I/O (copies, deletes) happens in a background `Task` and only touches the `ModelContext` once that I/O is confirmed complete — never block the UI thread on disk work, never mutate SwiftData off the main actor.

**Data model to freeze:** `Campaign` (owns many assets) → `Asset` (owns ordered, append-only `AssetVersion` rows plus a `currentVersionID` pointer that's the only thing that changes on a new upload) → `AssetVersion` (immutable once written) → `ReviewEvent` (immutable, append-only, drives Status History) → soft-delete represented as an explicit state flag + scheduled-deletion timestamp on the relevant entity, never an immediate row removal.

**Apple framework choices:** SwiftUI + `NavigationStack` (per the locked design_handoff), SwiftData (`@Model`, `@Query`, `@Bindable`) for metadata, PhotosUI (`PhotosPicker`) and `.fileImporter`/`UIDocumentPickerViewController` for asset intake, `UniformTypeIdentifiers` to validate imported files are genuinely the image/video/PDF type they claim before accepting them as a version, QuickLook (`QLPreviewController` via `UIViewControllerRepresentable`) and `QLThumbnailGenerator` (cached in an in-memory `NSCache`) for preview/thumbnails, `FileManager` for atomic file writes (temp file → rename), `OSLog` for diagnostics. Explicitly excluded and justified as out of scope: StoreKit (no purchase flow — tiering was deferred in `app_features`), CloudKit (no sync — single-device by design), Vision/CoreML/ARKit (no task in this app needs them; thumbnailing is a rendering utility, not ML), `BGTaskScheduler`/push (the soft-delete finalize sweep runs synchronously on next foreground launch, needing no background entitlement).

**Persistence and local data plan:** Asset bytes are file-backed in `Application Support/marketing_assets/...` (not Documents — keeps users from renaming/deleting backing files via the Files app and breaking referential integrity), written atomically (temp file, then rename), with collision-safe immutable names keyed to asset/version IDs. SwiftData holds only metadata + relative path references. Fixed ordering rules to prevent dangling pointers: on import, the file write must succeed before the SwiftData record is created; on delete-finalize, the SwiftData record is deleted before the backing file. Either direction, an interrupted mid-operation death leaves an orphaned file (accepted, already-named unbounded-storage limitation) rather than a dangling metadata pointer needing defensive handling on every screen. A real `Schema` + `SchemaMigrationPlan` is defined from day one, even if trivial/empty at v1, since a failed store open with no backend and no sync means total, unrecoverable data loss. On launch, a startup reconciliation pass finalizes any pending imports/deletes left incomplete by a prior force-kill.

**Permissions/privacy plan:** Request-on-first-use only, nothing preemptive. The must-have import path (`PhotosPicker`/`.fileImporter`) requires **no** photo-library or file-system permission prompt at all — it runs out-of-process, so the "permission denied → Settings deep link" recovery state only has a real trigger if/when camera capture (should/could tier) is added, requiring `NSCameraUsageDescription`. What the import path does need is correct security-scoped resource handling: `fileImporter` URLs are only valid inside a `startAccessingSecurityScopedResource()`/`stopAccessingSecurityScopedResource()` window, so the copy into app storage must happen synchronously inside that window. No network calls, no background collection, no analytics/remote logging SDKs anywhere in the app. Standard iCloud device backup is left enabled and disclosed (not app-managed sync) per prior phases' decision.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party packages. Every requirement maps cleanly onto the Apple frameworks listed above, so the permissive-dependency policy is satisfied trivially — there's nothing to license-check. If a dependency is ever proposed later, it must be justified explicitly in-contract with a clear gap Apple's frameworks can't fill.

**Testing implications:** Domain logic (state transitions, whitespace-trimmed rework-note validation, worst-status derivation, auto-revert-on-delete) lives as plain Swift and is unit-tested against an in-memory `ModelConfiguration(isStoredInMemoryOnly: true)` SwiftData container — fast, disk-free coverage of the force-kill-equivalent scenarios by reopening a fresh container against the same on-disk store mid-sequence and asserting state. Integration tests cover file-import copy atomicity, stale-pointer cleanup, and launch-time recovery after interrupted writes. UI tests use `XCUIApplication.terminate()` followed by a fresh `launch()` as the explicit, named mechanism for the actual force-quit/relaunch acceptance criteria this whole project is built around — not a proxy or simulation. QuickLook presentation always does a synchronous file-existence/non-zero-size check immediately before presenting, routing to the existing preview-failed state on failure rather than trusting the SwiftData record's mere existence.

**Acceptance checks satisfied:** The app is buildable natively with no vague architecture gaps — every screen/state from `design_handoff` maps to a named framework and a named persistence rule. No ML/LLM/AR is used anywhere, correctly, since nothing in this app's locked scope needs it. No dependency is introduced, so the permissive-license policy is trivially satisfied.

# build-me-a-prototype-for-a-marketing-platform — Ios Architecture Review

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-10 14:08:23._

## Original Prompt

```
Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: 

https://www.tryordinal.com
```

## Phase Purpose

Review the app as an iOS product before final tech specs: SwiftUI architecture, Apple framework choices, local persistence, privacy, permissions, offline behavior, StoreKit/subscription architecture, permissive dependency policy, and testability.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Backend / Systems Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

I’d build this as a strict local-first SwiftUI architecture with a narrow layering model: SwiftUI views + view models for UI state, a small domain layer for review/version/business rules, and a storage layer that owns only two things—SwiftData for structured metadata and deterministic object graphs, and the filesystem for byte content. That avoids ORM ambiguity and keeps “append-only + crash-safe + offline” enforceable.

The data model I would lock before tech specs should be Campaign, Asset, AssetVersion, ReviewEvent, and DeletionMarker/TrashState. Campaign owns many assets, Asset owns ordered versions, ReviewEvent is immutable and append-only, and soft-delete is represented as an explicit state flag plus scheduled-deletion timestamp rather than immediate row removal. I would intentionally make “current version” a pointer on Asset (e.g., `currentVersionID`) and treat every new upload as `nextVersion` that never mutates existing version rows. Campaign/asset aggregate status should be derived from current asset states at query/render time or updated through a single domain service immediately after each transition to prevent drift. I would model state transitions in one service (Draft → In Review → Approved / Needs Rework) with enforced invariants and one-way transition checks so UI can’t create invalid states.

For persistence I’d use SwiftData with `@Model` entities, `@Query`, and a single `ModelContext` boundary plus a repository actor for writes. Versioned media bytes must never go through SwiftData payloads. They should be copied into `Application Support/marketing_assets/...` with collision-safe immutable names (asset/version IDs) and `FileManager` atomic write (temp file then rename). On read, validate file existence and readable metadata before creating/updating UI. If a write fails, never leave dangling version rows; stage import in a “pending” row and commit to active version only after successful copy. On launch, run a startup reconciliation pass that finalizes stale pending imports/deletes and promotes incomplete writes to cleanly removed states. This is what keeps the “force-kill mid-import” edge case from corrupting referential integrity.

For iOS frameworks, the right set is small: SwiftUI for UI/navigation, SwiftData for offline model persistence, PhotosUI for library selection (or DocumentPicker where needed), UniformTypeIdentifiers for file filtering, QuickLook / QuickLookThumbnailing for preview and list thumbnails, and FileManager/OSLog. StoreKit, CloudKit, Vision, and RealityKit should stay out for now; this phase has no paid value gate, no cross-device sync contract, and no ML/AR requirement. If a subscription is introduced later, the only sane path is StoreKit 2 with on-device verification and no server-side purchase logic, but I would explicitly keep that out of this build and out of the user trust chain around the core review loop.

Permissions and privacy should be request-on-first-use and scoped: `NSPhotoLibraryUsageDescription` for Photos imports, no preemptive prompts, and no background collection. Files picker import from Files typically uses no new entitlement, but if security-scoped URLs are used, open for scope only during copy and release immediately. No network APIs should be present in this phase, no background upload jobs, no analytics SDKs, and no remote logging. Keep app data in the user’s sandbox; if you leave iCloud device backup enabled (recommended for recoverability), disclose that this is not app-managed sync and that phone-loss still means recoverability depends on the user’s own backup practices.

The single biggest risk I see in this direction is silent storage bloat plus complexity around “deletion under active view.” Append-only versions are non-negotiable and therefore grow forever; without explicit cleanup guidance, disk usage can become unbounded. I would preserve an explicit product limitation note: retention policy is deferred, not enforced in v1, and users should get clear storage footprint visibility in diagnostics or About/limitations. The second-order deletion risk is if a viewed asset is soft-deleted and the undo window expires while the detail screen is open; the storage and state layers must support hard transition of that in-memory navigation target into a terminal removed state and a controlled unwind, otherwise stale references crash-like UX appear. That’s one of the concrete rules this review phase must preserve.

Dependency policy should be strict: zero third-party packages in phase one. The full product can be built with Apple frameworks only and therefore no permissive-license compliance risk exists in the initial contract. If any dependency is proposed later, it must have a clear gap-filling role and an explicit fallback to in-house SwiftUI/Apple-framework code if removed.

Testing implications: unit tests should target domain rules and persistence contracts with an in-memory SwiftData container. Test transition guardrails (invalid state, whitespace-trimmed note validation, auto-revert when the last in-review versioned asset is deleted), and soft-delete reconciliation with clock control. Integration tests should cover file import copy atomicity, stale pointer cleanup, and launch recovery after interrupted writes. UI tests should verify the critical flows and recoverable states (empty/loading/error/success on all four screens, confirm-to-approve path, rework note non-empty validation, undo window behavior, and import permission denied recovery path). Even without backend endpoints, this is highly testable because everything is local and deterministic.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

Here's my pass on this, and I'm going to be blunt about a couple of things the locked design got wrong at the plumbing level, because if we carry them into tech_specs unexamined we'll build UI for states that can never actually occur, and we'll skip a decision that actually determines whether this app can silently corrupt itself.

Start with permissions, because I think the locked design has a real bug in it. The design phases keep specifying a "Photos/Files permission denied → Settings deep link" recovery state as a must-have. If we import via `PhotosPicker` (PhotosUI, backed by `PHPickerViewController`) and `.fileImporter` (backed by `UIDocumentPickerViewController`), neither of those requires a photo-library or file-system permission prompt at all — they run out-of-process and hand the app only what the user explicitly picked. There is no `NSPhotoLibraryUsageDescription` prompt to deny in that flow. So that permission-denied state, as currently scoped, is dead code for the must-have import path. The only place a real permission-denial state exists is if camera capture gets added (should/could tier), which needs `NSCameraUsageDescription` and can genuinely be denied with a Settings-deep-link recovery. My decision: build the permission-denied/Settings-deep-link UI, but wire it only to camera capture; don't waste effort simulating a Photos-permission-denial flow that can't happen with the picker APIs we're using. What file import does need real handling for is the security-scoped resource lifecycle — `fileImporter` URLs are only valid inside a `startAccessingSecurityScopedResource()`/`stopAccessingSecurityScopedResource()` window, so the copy into app storage has to happen synchronously inside that window, not deferred, or we'll get silent read failures that look like corrupt-file bugs but are actually a sandboxing bug.

On persistence: I want Application Support, not Documents, for the file-backed asset bytes, and I want that decided now, not left as "Documents/Application Support" the way it's been written interchangeably across every prior phase. If asset files live in Documents with file-sharing enabled, the user can open the Files app and rename or delete the backing file out from under us, which breaks the exact referential-integrity guarantee we've spent five phases protecting. Application Support, with `isExcludedFromBackup` left false (so it does participate in standard iCloud device backup, consistent with what was already disclosed), and file-sharing/document-browser capability left off entirely, keeps the user from ever touching these files directly. That closes an entire class of "why is my asset broken" bug reports before they exist.

On SwiftData specifically, since that's the actual risk surface here: define an explicit versioned `Schema` and a (even trivial, empty) `SchemaMigrationPlan` from day one, not "add migration later," because the first time we ship a model change without one, existing users' local stores can fail to open — and for a single-device app with no backend, a failed store open is total data loss, not a degraded experience. I also want a concrete, stated rule for what "finalize" means in the soft-delete sweep, because right now it's specified as "background sweep finalizes anything past its window" without saying what order operations happen in. My call: on finalize, delete the SwiftData metadata record first, commit that, and only then delete the backing file. If the app dies between those two steps, the failure mode is an orphaned file quietly wasting disk — which is already an accepted, named v1 limitation (unbounded version storage) — rather than a dangling metadata row pointing at a missing file, which would force every other screen to defensively handle "record exists, file doesn't" as a first-class case. Same logic in reverse during import: write the file to disk and confirm the write succeeded before creating the SwiftData record that points at it, never the other way around. One rule, applied consistently in both directions, and it eliminates an entire category of dangling-pointer bugs instead of handling them ad hoc per screen.

Concurrency: `ModelContext` is main-actor-bound, so importing a large video file has to do the actual disk copy off the main actor in a background `Task`, and only touch the `ModelContext` once that copy is confirmed complete — otherwise we either block the UI thread on file I/O or we're mutating SwiftData from a background context, which is its own bug class. This needs to be a named architectural rule in tech_specs, not something each screen figures out independently.

On QuickLook: use `QLPreviewController` via `UIViewControllerRepresentable` as locked, but the concrete risk is presenting it against a file that's been deleted or is still mid-copy — QuickLook doesn't gracefully degrade, it just shows broken or blank content. The rule has to be: check file existence and non-zero size synchronously right before presenting, and route to the already-designed preview-failed state if that check fails, rather than trusting the SwiftData record's mere existence as proof the file is presentable.

StoreKit: nothing to build. Free/Pro tiering was explicitly deferred out of this MVP in `app_features`, so there's no purchase flow, no `Product` fetching, no `Transaction.updates` listener needed right now — I'm calling that out mainly to close the loop so tech_specs doesn't accidentally scope it back in because the product brief phase talked about it. Same for background modes and push: no `BGTaskScheduler`, no notification permission, no background entitlements at all — the finalize-sweep runs synchronously on next foreground launch, which is cheap and needs zero special entitlements. ML/AR: there is no user-facing task in this app that needs Vision, CoreML, or ARKit, and I'm not going to manufacture one — the "thumbnail generation" need is fully served by `QLThumbnailGenerator`, which is not an ML framework, it's a rendering utility. Any ML/AR use here would be unjustified scope, full stop.

Dependencies: zero third-party packages are needed. Every requirement in this app maps cleanly onto SwiftData, SwiftUI, PhotosUI, QuickLook (`QLPreviewController`/`QLThumbnailGenerator`), and `UniformTypeIdentifiers` for validating that an imported file is actually the image/video/PDF type it claims to be before we accept it as a version. The permissive-dependency policy is satisfied trivially because there's nothing to license-check.

On testing, the thing I want locked now: the review state machine (Draft → In Review → Approved/Needs Rework) and the derived "worst status" / version-current logic have to live as plain, pure Swift logic — a computed property or a small non-view type — not embedded inside SwiftUI view bodies, specifically so it's unit-testable without instantiating a view hierarchy. Pair that with an in-memory `ModelConfiguration(isStoredInMemoryOnly: true)` SwiftData container for unit tests, which gives us fast, disk-free tests for every one of the force-kill-and-relaunch scenarios the earlier QA pass demanded — we can't literally force-kill in a unit test, but we can simulate the equivalent by opening a fresh `ModelContainer` against the same on-disk store mid-sequence and asserting state, which is functionally the same risk surface. For the true force-quit/relaunch acceptance criteria, XCUITest's `app.terminate()` followed by a fresh `launch()` is the actual mechanism — that needs to be named explicitly as a required UI test pattern in tech_specs, not left implicit, because it's the only way to honestly test the relaunch-survival claims this whole project has been built around.

The biggest objection to my own direction: deciding metadata-before-file-delete and file-before-metadata-create as fixed ordering rules assumes we're comfortable accepting "orphaned file on disk" as the failure mode in every case, forever — that's consistent with the already-accepted unbounded-storage limitation, but if a future phase ever adds a storage-cap or cleanup feature, this ordering choice is exactly the kind of thing that needs to be revisited, not silently kept. I'd rather name that dependency explicitly now than have it rediscovered as a surprise later. The detail I most want preserved into the final output: the Photos/Files permission-denial state is real only for camera capture, not for the must-have PhotosPicker/fileImporter import path — that's a correction to carried-forward assumptions, not a restatement of them, and if it gets lost, tech_specs will spec UI for a state that can't be triggered while under-speccing the one (camera denial) that can.

**Gemma3 (local) (Frontend Engineer · Visionary) — Round 1**

Okay, let’s get this done.

I’m going to focus on delivering a minimal, brutally efficient review cockpit for a solo marketer—someone who needs to quickly assess an asset and decide whether to approve it, and not spend hours fiddling with extraneous controls. Let's prioritize *action* over polish. I’m betting the biggest failure here will be over-engineering, adding complexity, and making it unnecessarily difficult to quickly assess the asset and move on.

Here’s my proposed breakdown:

**Core Navigation:** A single `NavigationStack` is essential. It’s simple, predictable, and efficient. We don’t need a tab bar or deeply nested navigation; just three screens: Campaign Detail, Asset Detail, and Status History.

**Campaign Detail:** This will be the entry point. It’ll display the campaign’s brief (inline-editable), a summarized status (worst-status rule, as a visual cue), and a clear “Add Asset” button (pinned to the bottom for quick access). The goal is to quickly jump to the asset.

**Asset Detail:** This is the heart of the review process. It’ll show the asset itself (QuickLook), a version badge (explicitly stating the current version), and the status/review controls (Approve, Rework, etc.). I’m going to avoid a grid layout for the asset itself; it’s too visually noisy. A single, large image preview will suffice.

**Status History:** A chronologically ordered list of all events related to the asset – approvals, reworks, deletions, etc. This is purely for traceability and confidence. It shouldn't be interactive in any way.

**Data Flow:** I’m going to use SwiftData for the core data management. It’s performant, reliable, and aligns with Apple’s recommended approach.  I'll be heavily reliant on `@Query` to fetch data efficiently.

**Key Risks & Considerations:**

*   **The "Removed-While-Viewing" Edge Case:** This is my biggest concern. What happens if the asset is soft-deleted while the user is looking at it? We need a clear, terminal state that immediately informs the user that the asset is no longer available.  This is critical to avoid frustration and a broken user experience.
*   **State Management:** I’m going to prioritize a simple, explicit state management approach with SwiftData. Avoid complex state machines or custom controllers.
*   **Performance:** Given the need for rapid asset review, performance is critical. I’ll prioritize asynchronous image loading and efficient data fetching.
*   **Security/Permissions:** We'll need to handle photo/file access permissions gracefully and provide the user with clear feedback.

I’ll focus on a solid, functional foundation. The fancy transitions, animations, and UI elements can be added later – let's prioritize the core workflow first. Let's build a tool that *works*, not a beautifully polished distraction.

---

**Design Tokens (High-Level):**

*   **Colors:** System-driven – the default gray and blue from the system interface to maintain consistency.
*   **Typography:** Dynamic Type for flexibility across screen sizes.
*   **Spacing:** 8pt grid for consistent layout.

I'm prepared to dive into the implementation details once the overall structure is agreed upon. Let’s get to work.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: YES

Here's where the room landed. Codex laid the architectural skeleton (SwiftUI/domain/storage layering, the five core model types, framework list, testing pyramid) and Claude's QA pass did real corrective work on top of it rather than just restating it — two genuine bug catches that change what gets built, not just how it's described:

1. **The Photos/Files permission-denied state is dead code as currently scoped.** `PhotosPicker` and `.fileImporter` run out-of-process and hand the app only what the user picked — there's no `NSPhotoLibraryUsageDescription` prompt to deny. The Settings-deep-link recovery UI every design phase has specified only has a real trigger if camera capture (should/could tier) gets built later. Codex didn't push back on this — it's a correction to an assumption baked into four prior phases, and it changes what should actually get built for the must-have import path (handle security-scoped resource lifecycle correctly instead) versus what's speculative UI for a state that can't occur.
2. **Concrete, previously-missing ordering rules that close a whole bug class**, not per-screen ad hoc handling: on import, write the file to disk and confirm success *before* creating the SwiftData record; on delete-finalize, delete the SwiftData record first, then the file. Either direction, the failure mode if the app dies mid-operation is an orphaned file (already an accepted, named limitation) rather than a dangling metadata pointer that every other screen would have to defensively handle.

Codex and Claude agree, without friction, on everything else that matters: Application Support (not Documents) for asset bytes so users can't tamper with backing files via the Files app; SwiftData with a repository-actor write boundary and `ModelContext` kept main-actor-only while actual file I/O happens off it; a real `Schema`/`SchemaMigrationPlan` from day one (Claude's addition — for a single-device app with no backend, a failed store open on a future model change is total data loss, not a degraded experience); QuickLook presented only after a synchronous file-existence/non-zero-size check; zero third-party packages, since PhotosUI, QuickLook, UniformTypeIdentifiers, SwiftData, and FileManager cover everything; and StoreKit/CloudKit/Vision/ARKit/BGTaskScheduler/push all correctly excluded as unjustified for this phase. Testing converges too: state-machine and derived-status logic as plain testable Swift (not embedded in view bodies), in-memory SwiftData containers for unit tests, and `app.terminate()` + fresh `launch()` in XCUITest as the actual mechanism for the relaunch-survival claims this whole project is built around.

Gemma3 restated the architecture in simpler terms but also proposed collapsing to three screens (dropping Campaign List as the entry point) — that contradicts the four-screen inventory locked across `design_discussion` and `design_handoff`, and screen count isn't this phase's decision to reopen anyway, so it's noted and set aside, not adopted. Qwen didn't respond again (cooldown) — no new vote.

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture recommendation:** Three-layer split — SwiftUI views/view models own UI state only; a small domain layer (plain Swift types/services, not embedded in view bodies) owns the review-state-machine, version-current-pointer, and worst-status-derivation rules so they're unit-testable without instantiating a view hierarchy; a storage layer owns exactly two things — SwiftData for structured metadata/object graphs via a single `ModelContext` boundary plus a repository actor for writes, and the filesystem for asset bytes. `ModelContext` is main-actor-bound; any file I/O (copies, deletes) happens in a background `Task` and only touches the `ModelContext` once that I/O is confirmed complete — never block the UI thread on disk work, never mutate SwiftData off the main actor.

**Data model to freeze:** `Campaign` (owns many assets) → `Asset` (owns ordered, append-only `AssetVersion` rows plus a `currentVersionID` pointer that's the only thing that changes on a new upload) → `AssetVersion` (immutable once written) → `ReviewEvent` (immutable, append-only, drives Status History) → soft-delete represented as an explicit state flag + scheduled-deletion timestamp on the relevant entity, never an immediate row removal.

**Apple framework choices:** SwiftUI + `NavigationStack` (per the locked design_handoff), SwiftData (`@Model`, `@Query`, `@Bindable`) for metadata, PhotosUI (`PhotosPicker`) and `.fileImporter`/`UIDocumentPickerViewController` for asset intake, `UniformTypeIdentifiers` to validate imported files are genuinely the image/video/PDF type they claim before accepting them as a version, QuickLook (`QLPreviewController` via `UIViewControllerRepresentable`) and `QLThumbnailGenerator` (cached in an in-memory `NSCache`) for preview/thumbnails, `FileManager` for atomic file writes (temp file → rename), `OSLog` for diagnostics. Explicitly excluded and justified as out of scope: StoreKit (no purchase flow — tiering was deferred in `app_features`), CloudKit (no sync — single-device by design), Vision/CoreML/ARKit (no task in this app needs them; thumbnailing is a rendering utility, not ML), `BGTaskScheduler`/push (the soft-delete finalize sweep runs synchronously on next foreground launch, needing no background entitlement).

**Persistence and local data plan:** Asset bytes are file-backed in `Application Support/marketing_assets/...` (not Documents — keeps users from renaming/deleting backing files via the Files app and breaking referential integrity), written atomically (temp file, then rename), with collision-safe immutable names keyed to asset/version IDs. SwiftData holds only metadata + relative path references. Fixed ordering rules to prevent dangling pointers: on import, the file write must succeed before the SwiftData record is created; on delete-finalize, the SwiftData record is deleted before the backing file. Either direction, an interrupted mid-operation death leaves an orphaned file (accepted, already-named unbounded-storage limitation) rather than a dangling metadata pointer needing defensive handling on every screen. A real `Schema` + `SchemaMigrationPlan` is defined from day one, even if trivial/empty at v1, since a failed store open with no backend and no sync means total, unrecoverable data loss. On launch, a startup reconciliation pass finalizes any pending imports/deletes left incomplete by a prior force-kill.

**Permissions/privacy plan:** Request-on-first-use only, nothing preemptive. The must-have import path (`PhotosPicker`/`.fileImporter`) requires **no** photo-library or file-system permission prompt at all — it runs out-of-process, so the "permission denied → Settings deep link" recovery state only has a real trigger if/when camera capture (should/could tier) is added, requiring `NSCameraUsageDescription`. What the import path does need is correct security-scoped resource handling: `fileImporter` URLs are only valid inside a `startAccessingSecurityScopedResource()`/`stopAccessingSecurityScopedResource()` window, so the copy into app storage must happen synchronously inside that window. No network calls, no background collection, no analytics/remote logging SDKs anywhere in the app. Standard iCloud device backup is left enabled and disclosed (not app-managed sync) per prior phases' decision.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party packages. Every requirement maps cleanly onto the Apple frameworks listed above, so the permissive-dependency policy is satisfied trivially — there's nothing to license-check. If a dependency is ever proposed later, it must be justified explicitly in-contract with a clear gap Apple's frameworks can't fill.

**Testing implications:** Domain logic (state transitions, whitespace-trimmed rework-note validation, worst-status derivation, auto-revert-on-delete) lives as plain Swift and is unit-tested against an in-memory `ModelConfiguration(isStoredInMemoryOnly: true)` SwiftData container — fast, disk-free coverage of the force-kill-equivalent scenarios by reopening a fresh container against the same on-disk store mid-sequence and asserting state. Integration tests cover file-import copy atomicity, stale-pointer cleanup, and launch-time recovery after interrupted writes. UI tests use `XCUIApplication.terminate()` followed by a fresh `launch()` as the explicit, named mechanism for the actual force-quit/relaunch acceptance criteria this whole project is built around — not a proxy or simulation. QuickLook presentation always does a synchronous file-existence/non-zero-size check immediately before presenting, routing to the existing preview-failed state on failure rather than trusting the SwiftData record's mere existence.

**Acceptance checks satisfied:** The app is buildable natively with no vague architecture gaps — every screen/state from `design_handoff` maps to a named framework and a named persistence rule. No ML/LLM/AR is used anywhere, correctly, since nothing in this app's locked scope needs it. No dependency is introduced, so the permissive-license policy is trivially satisfied.

title: Tech Specs
output:
CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** Four-layer split, enforced by real module boundaries rather than folder convention. `CockpitDomain` (Foundation-only, zero SwiftData/SwiftUI imports) owns the review state machine, worst-status derivation, and note validation as pure functions — this is the layer whose correctness the whole product's trust depends on, and it must be testable with a sub-second `swift test` run, no simulator. `CockpitData` owns the five SwiftData `@Model` types and actor-backed repository implementations, translating internally between plain `UUID`s (what the domain layer speaks) and SwiftData's `PersistentIdentifier` so the domain layer never has to know SwiftData exists. `CockpitServices` owns the filesystem — atomic asset-file writes, QuickLook thumbnailing, soft-delete/reconciliation, import ordering. The app target itself stays thin: SwiftUI views, a `Route`-driven `NavigationStack`, and an `@Observable AppEnvironment` built once at launch and injected via `.environment(_:)` so no view ever touches a `ModelContext` directly. This is a refinement of Codex's protocol set, not a departure from it — Claude's package split is how those same protocols get compiler-enforced isolation instead of just being organized by convention.

**File/module layout:** `CockpitDomain` (state machine, derivation, validation — no platform imports), `CockpitData` (SwiftData `@Model`s + repository actors), `CockpitServices` (file store, import coordinator, thumbnail cache, soft-delete coordinator), app target (views, view models, `AppEnvironment`, `Route`).

**Data models (frozen):** `Campaign` → `Asset` (holds `currentVersionID`, the only field a new upload changes) → `AssetVersion` (immutable once written, **file-backed via `relativeFilePath`, never embedded bytes** — this rejects Gemma3's `content: Data` approach as a direct contradiction of the locked `ios_architecture_review` decision) → `ReviewEvent` (append-only, carries `actor: .user/.system` as a real field, not inferred at render time, backing Status History's visual distinction). Soft-delete is `isTrashed` + `scheduledDeletionAt: Date?` on the relevant entity, never a row removal.

**Persistence strategy:** SwiftData repositories are actors wrapping background contexts; the main-actor `ModelContext` is read-only via `@Query`/`@Bindable`. Import order: file write succeeds and is confirmed before the SwiftData record is created. Delete order: SwiftData record is deleted before the backing file. `ImportCoordinator` and `SoftDeleteCoordinator` are the single enforcement points for those orderings, not something every call site re-derives. A real (if trivial) `VersionedSchema`/`SchemaMigrationPlan` ships from day one. `SoftDeleteCoordinator.reconcile(now:)` runs on cold launch *and* on every `scenePhase` transition to `.active` — the fix for the background-without-force-quit gap nobody had caught before this round.

**Testing strategy:** `CockpitDomain` gets a pure Swift Testing suite, no SwiftData, disk-free, proving every transition guard and edge case (double-approve, blank-note rework, auto-revert-on-delete) at CI speed. `CockpitData` gets XCTest against `ModelConfiguration(isStoredInMemoryOnly: true)`, including the reopen-a-fresh-container-mid-sequence trick to approximate force-kill. `CockpitServices` gets integration tests against a real temp `Application Support` directory. The app target gets XCUITest using literal `terminate()` + fresh `launch()` for relaunch-survival claims, plus a background-not-kill test targeting the `scenePhase` reconciliation path specifically.

**Acceptance checks satisfied:** parallel build lanes have a fully-typed, non-overlapping contract to code against (data_domain/primary_ui/services_utilities/polish_resilience), and the spec names concrete build and verification mechanisms for every claim made in earlier phases.

# build-me-a-prototype-for-a-marketing-platform — Tech Specs

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-10 14:11:07._

## Original Prompt

```
Build me something like ordinal. I want to copy their feature set. Here is the link: 

https://www.tryordinal.com
```

## Phase Purpose

Decide architecture, technology stack, data model, interfaces/APIs, and non-functional requirements with justified trade-offs.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

I’m taking a hard line on risk containment: this should ship as a strict local-first iOS SwiftUI app with a fixed, testable contract and one explicit persistence rule set, because the whole product promise depends on surviving relaunch and not corrupting local history. I’m choosing a three-layer model—domain, data/services, and UI—and enforcing all mutation through a repository actor boundary so writes are serialized, restart-safe, and observable by SwiftData fetches without ad-hoc cache invalidation logic.

Architecture overview: domain owns immutable business rules (state transitions, validity checks, version/current derivation), services owns storage and file IO, UI owns event-driven presentation state only. `ReviewStateMachine`, `Versioning` and `Rework validation` are pure domain logic, not view logic. The app shell is one `NavigationStack` that always cold-starts at campaign list; no deep stack restore. SwiftUI view models drive short-lived sheet/dialog state only, not navigation history. Concurrency is explicit: file I/O in background tasks, `ModelContext` mutations on main actor only.

File/module layout: `App/` contains app entry, dependency graph, DI container, and route state; `Domain/` contains states, rules, errors, and pure services; `Data/` contains `SwiftData` models, repositories, file-store, migration plan, and startup reconciliation; `Services/` contains file copy/thumbnail/undo/recovery helpers and OS logging; `Features/` has `CampaignList`, `CampaignDetail`, `AssetDetail`, `StatusHistory`, `SharedComponents`; `Resources/` has app strings and accessibility labels; `Tests/` has `Domain`, `Services`, and `UI` targets. One accepted constraint stays in scope from the start: no third-party dependencies.

Data models: persisted entities are `Campaign` -> `Asset` -> `AssetVersion` and `ReviewEvent`; plus a soft-delete marker embedded on `Campaign` and `Asset` with `isDeleted + deletedAtScheduled` and no immediate row removal. `Asset.currentVersionID` is the only mutable pointer on asset updates; versions themselves are append-only and immutable once written. `ReviewEvent` is append-only and timestamped, with both user and system events. Derived fields (current asset status, campaign worst status, last activity) must be computed from persisted state or via single-domain selectors, never hand-authored in view code. The domain must explicitly reject empty/whitespace-only briefs and rework notes and must block illegal transitions.

Persistence strategy: store only metadata in SwiftData and bytes on disk under `Application Support/marketing_assets/` with immutable IDs in filenames. Import rule: copy-to-disk succeeds before SwiftData metadata is created; delete-finalize rule: SwiftData metadata is deleted before backing file removal. This prevents dangling metadata pointers and turns interrupted crashes into orphaned files only (named limitation: storage growth remains unbounded in v1). Repositories run a startup reconciliation pass for incomplete imports/deletes after force-kill, and every QuickLook open must synchronously validate file existence and size > 0 first. `Schema` and `SchemaMigrationPlan` must exist from v1 with at least a baseline migration plan so model changes cannot silently brick local stores on update. `NSPhotoLibraryUsageDescription` and file picker denial states are not required for `PhotosPicker`/`fileImporter` path; permission-recovery UI should only exist for true gated features like camera (if added later).

Testing strategy: unit-test domain as pure Swift with in-memory SwiftData for transition safety, status derivation, and trimming/validation rules; integration tests for atomic copy/delete ordering and stale-import/deletion recovery; UI tests for all required state surfaces (empty/loading/error/success) and end-to-end cycle including relaunch integrity (`XCUIApplication().terminate()` + fresh `launch()`), plus the `only in-review asset deleted` auto-revert edge case and undo behavior. Biggest risk if we don’t do this: we’ll pass happy-path UI demos and still lose referential integrity after a kill/relaunch; the spec must preserve the startup recovery pass and both ordering rules above as non-negotiable.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Visionary) — Round 1**

Let me set the technical shape for this phase, thinking as the person who has to actually wire the four locked screens into a running SwiftUI app that survives force-kill.

My architecture bet is a strict three-package split underneath the app target, not just a "layered folder structure" — because the acceptance check for this phase is explicitly that parallel build lanes can't invent incompatible types, and folders don't enforce that, compiler boundaries do. I want a local Swift Package called `CockpitDomain` that has zero imports beyond Foundation — no SwiftData, no SwiftUI, nothing platform-flavored — and owns exactly the review state machine, the worst-status derivation, rework-note trimming/validation, and the event/status enums. That package should be testable with `swift test` in under a second with no simulator boot, which is the boldest thing I want out of this build: the actual business logic that this whole product's trust depends on (can Approve happen twice, can Rework fire with a blank note, does deleting the last in-review asset really auto-revert) should be provable in a CI-speed unit suite, not something we only find out by tapping through the simulator. On top of that sits `CockpitData`, which owns the five SwiftData `@Model` types plus repository protocols and their SwiftData-backed implementations, translating between plain `UUID`s (what the domain layer speaks) and SwiftData's `PersistentIdentifier` internally so `CockpitDomain` never has to know SwiftData exists. Alongside it, `CockpitServices` owns the filesystem: the atomic asset-file writer, the QuickLook thumbnail cache, the import coordinator that does the "write file, confirm, then create record" ordering we already locked, and the soft-delete/reconciliation coordinator. The app target itself is thin: SwiftUI views, a `Route` enum driving a single `NavigationStack`, and an `@Observable AppEnvironment` that's constructed once at launch and handed down via `.environment(_:)`, assembling the concrete repositories and services so no view ever touches a `ModelContext` directly.

On the data model, I'd freeze it exactly as the architecture review already sketched but make the concurrency-safe shape explicit: `Campaign` holds `id`, `briefText`, `createdAt`, `isTrashed`, `scheduledDeletionAt: Date?`, and a cascade relationship to `Asset`. `Asset` holds `id`, `currentVersionID: UUID`, `status: ReviewStatus`, `isTrashed`, `scheduledDeletionAt: Date?`, and cascade relationships to `AssetVersion` and `ReviewEvent`. `AssetVersion` is immutable once written — `id`, `versionNumber`, `relativeFilePath`, `fileType` (a UTType identifier string, checked against `UniformTypeIdentifiers` at import time so a renamed `.mov` claiming to be a `.jpg` can't sneak past QuickLook), `fileSizeBytes`, `createdAt`. `ReviewEvent` is append-only — `id`, `kind` (an enum: created, versionAdded, submittedForReview, approved, needsRework, autoRevertedToDraft, assetTrashed, assetRestored, campaignTrashed, campaignRestored, deletionFinalized), `actor` (`.user` or `.system`, so Status History's visual distinction has a real backing field instead of inferring it from the event kind at render time), an optional `note`, and `timestamp`. The one technical detail I want to nail down now, because nobody's actually specified it and it's exactly the kind of thing that becomes an undocumented judgment call at 11pm during build: the soft-delete undo countdown must not be driven by a live `Timer` or `Task.sleep` as its source of truth, because those die the instant the app backgrounds or gets killed, and we've already locked "undo must survive backgrounding within the window" as a hard requirement. The real mechanism is a stored `scheduledDeletionAt` timestamp written at the moment of soft-delete; the UI countdown is just a cosmetic poll against that timestamp, and the actual eligibility check — "has this passed its window" — is computed by comparing `scheduledDeletionAt` to `Date()` both in the startup reconciliation pass and on every `scenePhase` transition to `.active`, not only on cold launch. Without that second trigger, a user who backgrounds and foregrounds without a full force-quit would see stale trashed items sitting past their window indefinitely, which quietly breaks the "undoable within 10 seconds" contract nobody would notice until QA specifically tried it.

For the state machine itself, I want `ReviewStateMachine` to be a pure function — `transition(from: ReviewStatus, action: ReviewAction) -> Result<ReviewStatus, TransitionError>` — where `ReviewAction` is its own enum (`submitForReview`, `approve`, `needsRework(note: String)`) so the "rework requires a non-empty trimmed note" rule and the "invalid transitions are impossible" rule live in one switch statement that every call site (view, test, future feature) goes through, instead of being re-derived per screen. Same instinct for `WorstStatusDerivation.worstStatus(for: [ReviewStatus]) -> ReviewStatus?`, which is what Campaign List's aggregate chip and Status History's campaign-level rollup both need to agree on, so it can't drift into two different orderings of "urgency" in two different views.

Persistence-wise, repositories are actors — `CampaignRepository`, `AssetRepository`, `ReviewEventRepository` — each wrapping a background `ModelActor`-conforming context so writes never touch the main-thread `ModelContext` used for `@Query`/`@Bindable` reads. Every mutating call is `async throws` and returns plain `UUID`s or throws a typed error (`ImportError`, `PersistenceError`), never a raw SwiftData error bubbling into view code. The file-write-before-record-create and record-delete-before-file-delete ordering from the architecture review lives inside `ImportCoordinator` and `SoftDeleteCoordinator` respectively, so it's enforced in exactly one place rather than trusted to every call site to get right.

Testing strategy: `CockpitDomain` gets a pure Swift Testing (`@Test`, not XCTest) suite with no SwiftData dependency at all — this is where every transition-guard, note-trimming, and worst-status edge case gets proven, fast and disk-free. `CockpitData` gets XCTest with `ModelConfiguration(isStoredInMemoryOnly: true)` for repository and reconciliation-ordering tests, including the "reopen a fresh container mid-sequence" trick already agreed on to approximate force-kill without literally force-killing. `CockpitServices` gets integration tests against a real temp `Application Support` directory for atomic-write and orphan-cleanup behavior. And the app target gets XCUITest with real `terminate()` + fresh `launch()` for the actual relaunch-survival acceptance criteria, plus a test that specifically backgrounds (not kills) mid-undo-window to catch the `scenePhase` reconciliation gap I just flagged.

My honest objection to my own direction: three local packages is more upfront ceremony than a single-screen-flow MVP strictly needs, and if the domain/data boundary is drawn sloppily — say, a repository protocol that leaks `PersistentIdentifier` into `CockpitDomain` — the whole point of the split (a SwiftData-free, instant-test-suite core) quietly evaporates and we're left with the overhead of three packages and none of the benefit. The thing that must survive into the final spec to prevent that: domain-layer protocols speak only `UUID` and plain Swift value types, never SwiftData types, full stop — that boundary is the one rule I'd fight to keep un-negotiated.
